articles.title: Father of All Forecasters
articles.author: Charles Brusso
articles.description: This text discusses the man who developed the foundation of weather forecasting, Cleveland Abbe.
articles.intro: Cleveland Abbe (1838-1916) was an American meteorologist responsible for creating the foundation of weather forecasting. In this informational text, Charles Brusso discusses what motivated Abbe to study the weather and how we continue to benefit from his work today.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the aspects of weather that Abbe studied to ensure that his predictions were accurate.
articles.permissions: "Father of All Forecasters" by Charles Brusso, COBBLESTONE, March 2012 (text). Copyright © 2012 Carus Publishing Company, Cobblestone Publishing. Used with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Did you know that the National Weather Service gathers data from across the country to help create local weather reports every day? In fact, its on-line national map (www.weather.gov/view/largemap.php) refreshes itself every five minutes to offer updated watches, warnings, and advisories. Cleveland Abbe would be so proud!

Abbe was born in New York City on December 3, 1838. In the summers, he worked on his grandfather’s farm outside Windham, Connecticut. Fascinated by nature, he learned how important the weather could be on the farm. Heavy rains might delay planting or drown plants before they could be harvested. Storms could knock crops flat.

After studying science and mathematics, Abbe became a teacher. While teaching at the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor, he also studied astronomy. In 1868, he was hired as director of Ohio’s Cincinnati Observatory. Abbe knew that atmospheric conditions such as clouds, haze, fog, and rain could affect astronomical observations. He became fascinated with studying weather in other places to predict what the weather would be like.

But how would he get the data he needed? Abbe proposed setting up a network of weather observers around Cincinnati. The observers would record eight different things, from local barometric pressure and temperature to details about atmospheric conditions, such as the wind, clouds, and rain or snowfall amounts. They would send their data by telegraph to Abbe at a central office, where he would coordinate the information to predict the weather for the next few days. His predictions could then be sent back by telegraph to each weather station, so they could share the results.

Abbe convinced the Cincinnati Chamber of Commerce to give him money to set up a network of 20 observers and help get his weather bulletin printed in the city’s daily papers. The first official public forecast — predicting weather for the Ohio River valley over the next two days — appeared September 22, 1869.

The success of Abbe’s local weather reports, or “probabilities,” helped him gain the attention of larger groups, including the U.S. government. Early in 1870, President Ulysses S. Grant signed a bill establishing the Weather Bureau, and Abbe became its chief meteorologist. Originally part of the U.S. Army Signal Corps, the bureau used observers in distant military stations around the country to gather data. Abbe’s first official national weather report was delivered on February 19, 1871.

For the first six months, Abbe was the only one who knew enough about meteorology to create forecasts. His skill earned him the nickname “Old Probabilities.” Abbe worked hard to make each forecast consistent and precise. Every report included information on temperature, wind direction, barometric pressure, and current weather conditions.

But Abbe couldn’t do it all by himself, so he taught others to measure and predict the weather. By 1872, the Weather Bureau was sending out more than 500 daily weather bulletins for locations across the country. Abbe also wrote articles, did research, and organized state weather services across the country. He even thought of dividing our country into four standard time zones to help coordinate weather observations.

Abbe wanted his observers to have the best equipment for carrying out their work. The bureau’s instrument division tested thousands of barometers, anemometers, and hygrometers. Bureau scientists also designed and built many of their own instruments.

Despite failing health, Abbe was part of the U.S. Weather Bureau until his death on October 28, 1916 at the age of 77. His hard work established today’s National Weather Service (renamed from the U.S. Weather Bureau in 1967) and built the foundations of modern meteorology.

options.label: by showing the impact of Abbe's observations on the farm and the continued research into meteorology
by presenting Abbe's goals and ambitions while he attended school and later when he found a job
by explaining the importance of Abbe having multiple weather stations to record information
by examining how effectively Abbe was able to predict weather in comparison with his students

articles.title: The 13th
articles.author: Shelley Walden
articles.description: When the COVID-19 lockdown begins, a superstitious teenage girl attempts to reverse her bad luck.
articles.intro: Superstitions exist in many different cultures. In "The 13th," Angela believes her bad luck has contributed to the 2020 COVID-19 pandemic. Determined to reverse this bad luck, Angela begins a quest to find a good luck charm.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Angela thinks about good and bad luck, and the impact she believes luck has on her life.
articles.permissions: "The 13th" by Shelley Walden. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: As Angela stared out the school bus window, her stomach sank. There was nothing in the sky to look at — no clouds, birds, hot air balloons — nothing. This was strange because Albuquerque was the hot air balloon capital of the world and it was a perfect morning for them: crisp, clear, and not too windy. The absence of colorful dots floating through the sky felt like a bad omen on a day when she needed a good one.
The bus stopped and her best friend, Milagro, climbed the steps. Her hair was wild, as if she’d just rolled out of bed. Angela waved her over and Milagro smiled.
“Hey,” Milagro said as she sat down beside her. “Ugh, I slept through my alarm clock and my morning was crazy. I didn’t even have time to brush my hair.”
Milagro unzipped her backpack and pulled out a pink plastic brush and hair elastic. She worked the brush through a few tangles.
“Are you okay?” Milagro asked. “You look all worried.”
“Yeah, I’m fine,” Angela said. “It’s just that it’s Friday the 13th.”
“So? That’s just a silly superstition. Besides, my mom says Friday the 13th isn’t even a thing. It’s Tuesday the 13th you need to worry about.”
“Well, I believe in it. My grandmother died on Friday the 13th.”
“Whoa, Angela, really?” Milagro replied as she brushed her long hair into a ponytail. Then she scrunched up her face as she thought about it. “That’s just a coincidence though.”
“You’re telling me you aren't worried?” Angela asked.
“About what?”
“COVID-19. It’s all over social media. There are a bunch of cases in New York. People are getting really sick. Some are even dying. And my mom said there are some cases here now, too.”
“Yeah, like five. It’s not a big deal; we’re nowhere near New York. Can I borrow your phone?” Angela fumbled around in her bag, but couldn’t find it.
“I must have left it charging,” she said. “Where’s yours?”

“My mom took it away when she grounded me. I don’t get it back till Monday. How about a mirror?” Angela reached into her backpack. She had a compact in there somewhere. Finally, she found it at the bottom of the bag, underneath a heavy science textbook. She pulled it out, popped it open and gasped.
“Oh no!” she said, as she shook her head. “No, no, no, no.”
There was a crack that ran down the middle of the mirror and a few broken pieces on each side.
“It’s okay,” Milagro said. “I’ll ask Kat if I can use her phone.”
“No, it’s not that,” Angela said, as she rubbed her eyebrows. “Breaking a mirror is seven years of bad luck. Probably more since it’s Friday the 13th. This is terrible!”
“Are you serious?” Milagro replied. “Angela, you can’t cause bad luck. Superstitions aren’t real. It’s just a broken mirror. You probably didn’t even break it today. It could have been days ago.”

But as Angela closed the compact, she couldn’t help but worry. Back when her dad was around, he was obsessed with luck. When he had a lucky night at the casino, he’d credit it to the rabbit’s foot he kept in his back pocket or to the stranger who blew on his dice or to his salt and pepper beard, which he refused to shave. He’d come home singing Frank Sinatra, his baritone voice waking her from her sleep, and they’d dance together in the kitchen, his dark eyes shining. But on the unlucky nights…well, she didn’t like to think about those. And she certainly didn’t like to think about where he might be or why he never called.
What would he have done about the mirror? Would he have known a way to reverse her bad luck? She wished he was around to ask.
But he wasn’t. So Angela zipped up her backpack, fidgeted with her necklace and looked out the window, hoping everything would be okay.
She made it through most of the day without anything bad happening. There were no pop quizzes, fire alarms, or fish sticks for lunch. Maybe everything was going to be okay, after all.

But during her last period, the students were told to return to their homerooms. When she arrived, her teacher, Mr. Lucero, stood in front of the class with his arms crossed. Angela put her backpack on her lap and ran her fingers through her short, wavy hair, waiting for him to talk.
“So, some of you may have heard about the governor’s announcement today,” he said.
“No school!” someone in the class yelled. A few kids laughed.

“Not exactly.” Mr. Lucero cleared his throat. “As some of you may know, a few days ago the first COVID-19 case was confirmed in New Mexico,” he said. “And now there are ten cases. So the governor has decided to take steps to slow the spread. And one of those steps is to cancel school for the next three weeks.”

The class erupted in cheers, drowning out the rest of Mr. Lucero’s instructions. But Angela remained silent. She clutched her backpack, envisioning the broken glass that was still at the bottom, already wreaking havoc. Her thoughts raced. Had the mirror played a role in this? If so, how could she fix it? There had to be a way.

As soon as Angela got home, she googled Broken mirror bad luck fix . Tons of articles came up. She clicked on one with “How to Avoid Bad Luck” in the title and skimmed it. It said she could reverse her luck by taking every piece of the mirror and burying it under the moonlight. Thank God.

While the sun lowered in the sky, turning the Sandia Mountains a reddish pink, Angela opened the mirror. But when she did, she noticed a small sliver of glass was missing. The article said she needed every piece for it to work. She searched every nook and cranny of her bag, but there was nothing. How could it have vanished?

Still, it didn’t hurt to try. So, when the stars appeared, she grabbed a shovel, went into her backyard, and dug. Then she tossed the mirror into the hole and buried it, hoping it would be enough to banish her bad luck.

That night, as Angela lay in bed, she couldn’t sleep. The worries and ‘what ifs’ built and built. As she tossed and turned, she knew that burying the mirror wasn’t enough. She needed something else to really shake the bad luck. But what?

The first few weeks of lockdown were weird. Movie theaters closed, then restaurants, and soon the only places that remained open were grocery stores. Angela’s mom, who was an elementary school teacher, was home all the time now. Since daycare was cancelled, her little brother, Elijah, was home too. There were good things about this: they played lots of games together and Angela got to watch more TV than normal. But there were odd things, too. Like her mom suddenly developed an obsession with cleanliness and made them wash their hands all the time. She wiped down everything with disinfectant wipes, even their mail. Her mom also monitored their toilet paper usage: she removed the rolls from the bathroom and dispensed one to three squares per bathroom trip because who knew when they would be able to find toilet paper in the grocery stores again, or food, even? Dinner was made of random ingredients from the back of their freezer, things like lima bean casserole and posole soup made from the hominy left over from Christmas.

During the first two weeks of lockdown, Angela’s teachers emailed her packets, so she had some work to do. But then spring break arrived and things got really depressing. Her family was supposed to go visit cousins in Colorado, but their trip was canceled. Milagro and her family canceled their trip to Texas, too. It seemed like the whole world was stuck inside their homes, unable to go anywhere.
Angela missed her friends. She missed soccer and dance practice, too. And she really, really missed sleeping; she hadn’t had a good night’s sleep since school ended.
Then virtual school started. Angela tried to call-in for her classes, but it messed-up her mom’s Zoom classroom because her screen kept freezing.
“We can’t use Zoom at the same time,” her mom said the first morning after a few attempts teaching class. “It’s not working.”
“But I have school.”
“I’m sorry, Angela, but the Internet isn’t strong enough for two Zoom calls. And I have a whole classroom of 4th graders depending on me.”
“But what about my classes?”
Her mother sighed. “I’ll talk to your teachers and we’ll figure something out.”
Angela didn’t think that was fair. She was going to fall behind and get bad grades and what if she got held back? Her mom didn’t seem to care.
“So I can’t use the Internet at all? What am I supposed to do?”
Her mom held her hands up. “I don’t know,” she said. “We’ll figure it out later. Can you watch Elijah until my call ends?”

Angela glared at her mom but said nothing. Their house was small and had thin walls, so if she stayed inside she’d hear her mom’s voice all morning. She grabbed Elijah and a few of his toy trucks and headed into the backyard.

Angela hated their yard. It was a small dirt patch with an old plastic slide in one corner and nothing else. There was no shade, and the high desert sun was relentless. Elijah was happy pouring dirt from one truck to another, but what was she supposed to do? Sit there and sweat?
“Ugh!” she screamed. So much for burying her bad luck. She wished she was back in school, back with her friends, anywhere but there. She had to find a way to fix this.

But how could she reverse her bad luck? She didn’t have her dad’s rabbit’s foot; he had taken it with him. She’d heard four leaf clovers were lucky. But there were hardly any in Albuquerque. Occasionally she’d seen some growing in the grass at playgrounds, but the playgrounds were all closed.
“Good morning,” a woman said from the other side of their wire fence.
Angela jumped, startled by the voice. She looked up and saw her neighbor, Ms. Chavez. She must have been in her seventies but had the energy of someone much younger. She was carrying a cardboard box.
“Oh, hi, Ms. Chavez,” Angela said.
“How are you guys doing?”
“Fine.” It was a lie, of course, but what was she supposed to say? During a global pandemic? Nothing was fine, and who knew if it would ever be again.
“What’s in the box?” Angela asked.
“Some rocks and gemstones. My friend has a few claims, so I go with him and dig sometimes. I have more than I could ever use. Do you and Elijah want some?”
“Wow, cool!” Elijah said, as he came running to the fence. “You dug all of these yourself?”
“Sure did.” Ms. Chavez passed the box over the fence. “Take whatever you want. There’s some agate, garnet, fluorite and a bunch of other stuff.”
Elijah took out a purple rock and held it up to inspect it. His eyes squinted behind his blue plastic glasses.
“Are any of them lucky?” Angela asked.
“Why, you need some luck?”
“Yeah.”
“I guess we all could use a little bit of that right now. But, sorry, I don’t know.”
“Oh. Well, thanks anyway. They’re really pretty.” Angela picked up a gray rock that had a deep blue vein in the center of it.
“Can I have this one?”
“Sure,” Ms. Chavez said.
“Thanks.” The weight of the stone grounded Angela and made her feel less agitated. But it wasn’t enough. She needed more; she needed luck.
“Hey, do you know any place nearby where there might be clover?” she asked her neighbor.
“Clover?”
“Yeah, like four-leaf ones.”
Ms. Chavez laughed. “We’re in the middle of a desert, not Ireland. I can’t remember the last time I saw one of those.”
Angela let out an exasperated sigh.

“If it’s luck you’re looking for, you’re better off finding a roadrunner’s feather. My brother-in-law is Pueblo, and they believe the roadrunner protects you from evil. My niece used to have a roadrunner’s feather hanging over her crib.”
A roadrunner, of course! Angela sometimes saw them running by the acequias. They would be easier to find than a clover.
“Good idea,” Angela said. “Thanks!” She turned to go.
“But Angela?”
“Yeah?”
“You know this virus is bigger than one good luck charm, right? COVID has spread all over the world now. There are some things we can’t stop; we just have to learn to live with them.”

Angela stopped in her tracks. Something about Ms. Chavez’s words irritated her. She knew the virus was out of her control, of course she knew that. Hospitals were filling up across the country and people were dying. And yet, she couldn’t help but feel there was something she needed to do to fix everything, to reverse the bad karma her broken mirror had brought into the world, to stop the dark thoughts racing around her head.
“Yeah, I know. Come on Elijah, let’s go look for a roadrunner.”

It was the time of the year when water returned to the acequias. During the winter, the canals were dry. But, in the spring, water was siphoned off from the Rio Grande, flowing through the centuries old acequias, where it was occasionally released to irrigate the farms lining the valley.

That first day, Angela didn’t find a roadrunner. But she refused to give up. She and her brother developed a routine. Each morning, as the weeks went by, they would walk to the acequia near their house and stroll along the dirt paths that ran next to the ditch. Angela would look for a roadrunner and Elijah would chase bright green frogs, collect branches or play on the small wooden bridges that crossed over the water.

Angela would have never played by the acequias at his age: she was too scared of La Llorona and the stories her friends told her about a weeping woman who drowned children in ditches. But Elijah didn’t know those ghost stories and she didn’t want to tell him. The real world was already scary enough.

Roadrunners weren’t great at flying, so she mainly looked on the ground or the low fences and adobe walls that separated houses from the ditch paths. She saw other birds: ravens, ducks, and hawks, but no roadrunners. It was like they were hiding from her.

They returned home for her mom’s lunch break and Angela went to her room to try to log-in for a class. As the weeks passed, she fell behind, and homework was a struggle, especially math. Dividing fractions seemed so unimportant compared to everything else going on in the world.

There weren’t that many cases in Albuquerque. But Angela’s mom told her that the Navajo Nation, which spread across parts of New Mexico and Arizona, had the third highest rate of COVID infections in the entire country. Many families on the reservation lacked running water, making it difficult to wash their hands and prevent infection. Angela wished she could do something to help, but she didn’t know what to do.

Most afternoons, as the days slowly rolled past, Angela would go to her backyard and chat with Ms. Chavez. Her neighbor taught her all about rocks — which ones originated where, how to tell them apart and how to tumble them. She also taught Angela how to sew, and gave her some needles, thread, and fabric to practice. Angela enjoyed the repetitive movement of a needle pulling thread and how it helped clear her head.
But one day, Ms. Chavez wasn’t there. And she wasn’t there the next day, either.
“I’m worried about her,” Angela told her mom at dinner that night.
“I’m sure she’s fine,” her mom said. “But I’ll give her a call tonight to check.”
When she did, the news wasn’t good.
“I spoke with Ms. Chavez’s daughter,” her mom said. “She’s over there with her now. Ms. Chavez is sick.”
“With COVID?” Angela asked.
“She doesn’t know. She’s waiting for the test results. But her daughter is a nurse at the hospital and has been working with COVID patients, so she could have gotten it from her.”
Angela crossed her arms and felt her posture collapse. How could Ms. Chavez have COVID? How could it be right in her backyard when it was hardly anywhere else in the city?
It had to be the mirror’s fault. It had to be. She had to find a way to break the curse, for Ms. Chavez’s sake.

The next day, Angela’s mom told them to stay in the house. She didn’t want them walking around the neighborhood, in case they had been exposed to the virus. Angela looked over at Ms. Chavez’s yard, but there was no sign of her. And she wasn’t there the next day, or the day after that.

Angela couldn’t just sit back and watch as Ms. Chavez got sicker. She had to do something. So, on the third day, she and Elijah snuck out of the yard and walked to the acequias. Her mother would never know. She hardly ever watched them these days, anyway.

As they walked along the dirt paths bordering the ditch, Elijah threw rocks into the water. It had rained the night before, so the water was higher and faster than normal and the paths were muddy. Angela tried to avoid mucky spots and puddles while searching for a roadrunner. Every once in a while they would spot another person, but the adults would usually keep their distance, crossing the acequia to put six feet of social distance between them.

They walked further and further away from their house, until they reached areas Angela didn’t recognize. She should probably turn back. But it was so hot, and she needed to catch her breath for a minute.

As Elijah played on a narrow wooden bridge that crossed over the acequia, Angela sat on the dirt and took a sip from her water bottle. Suddenly, out of the corner of her eye, she spotted movement. Could it be?

Yes, it was. There, right at the base of a cottonwood tree, was a roadrunner, holding a wriggling lizard in its mouth. Just seeing it made her feel better. That alone could be enough to reverse her luck. But she wasn’t going to take any chances. She needed a feather.

She slowly approached the bird. It turned its head at a curious angle and looked back at her. Angela couldn’t help but admire the beautiful sky-blue spot surrounding its dark eyes and the red patch beyond that. It’s long salt and pepper feathers shifted as it tilted its body forward, preparing to run. Suddenly, it scampered away.

“Wait!” She lunged forward, almost grasping it. But she missed, and as she rose from the ground, it sprinted beyond her reach. Then it hopped onto a wooden fence. It stayed there for a second before jumping to the other side.
“Ugh!” Angela kicked the dirt. She had been so close, but it wasn’t enough. How would she ever catch a roadrunner, let alone get a feather from one?

She looked up at the fence, at the spot the roadrunner jumped over. And then she saw it. A curved feather attached to the fencepost! She reached out and grabbed it, examining the white edge. As she ran her finger along it, her body relaxed and her shoulders lowered. Everything was going to be okay. She put the feather in her backpack, along with her water bottle.
That’s when she heard the splash.
As Angela turned back toward the acequia, her stomach dropped.
“Help!” her little brother’s voice rang out.
He was in the water, his arms flailing wildly. The water was over his head and the current was pushing him away from her.
As Angela threw down her backpack and raced toward the ditch, one terrifying thought flashed through her mind. Elijah doesn’t know how to swim.

She jumped feet first into the water, without removing her shoes. Her feet landed on the muddy bottom and her head sprang up to the surface. As her face emerged, she saw her brother 10 feet away, struggling to stay afloat.

She started swimming, kicking her legs as hard and fast as she could. She took a stroke and then another and another, until she reached him. He was struggling, but alert and breathing. She grabbed him from behind, hooking her elbows under his shoulders, and pulled him onto his back. Then she released one arm and used it to grab the side of the acequia.

A dog barked and she saw a man standing above her on the ditch path. He was wearing a homemade facemask and walking a Labrador retriever on a leash. He unleashed his dog and tossed the leash down to her.
“Grab this!” he yelled. “I’ll pull you up.”

Angela faced Elijah toward the bank and put the leash into his hand. Then she pushed him up, as he grabbed onto the leash. The man pulled on his end until Elijah was safely on the ground above. Then the man helped lift her out of the acequia.
The man examined Elijah, checking his breathing and pulse. Elijah’s glasses were gone, washed away by the current, and he was shivering from the snow-fed water.
“He’s a little roughed up,” the man said, “But he’s going to be okay.”
As Angela hugged her brother, she trembled uncontrollably and felt tears running down her cheeks. She’d almost lost him.
“How could you let this happen!” Angela’s mother screamed when they arrived home. “What were you doing playing near the acequia? You weren’t supposed to leave the house!”
“I’m sorry,” was all Angela could think to say. “It was an accident!”
“An accident?” Her mother replied as she paced around the kitchen table. “He could have drowned, Angela! Why weren’t you watching him?”
“Why weren’t you watching him?” Angela spat back.
This stopped her mother in her tracks. “Excuse me?”
“You never watch us. You’re too busy teaching other peoples’ kids to care about your own.”
Her mother’s jaw dropped, and she stared at her daughter as if she’d been slapped.
“That’s not true,” her mother said, softly.
“Yes it is!”
“I’m trying my best,” her mom said as she sat down at the table and put her hands to her temples. “This is really hard, Angela. It’s hard for everyone.”
“Well, try harder!”
Angela ran to her room and slammed the door behind her. She threw herself onto her purple comforter and turned on her music as loud as it would go. Then she let the tears flow.

She knew she had messed up. Maybe her bad luck had contributed to what happened, but it wasn’t to blame. She was the one who chose to disobey her mom and leave the house, to bring Elijah to the acequias, to chase after a stupid bird when she should have been watching him. It was all her fault.

When the tears finally stopped and the sobs subsided, she stared at her backpack. She got up, unzipped it and took out the roadrunner’s feather. Then she went to her desk, where she kept her scented candle, piles of mystery novels, and multi-colored pens. As she reached for some matches, she noticed the framed family photo she kept on her desk. It was taken years prior — before her father spent all of their savings at the casino, before her mother filed for divorce, before he left. Her whole family was at White Sands National Park, standing atop a gypsum dune, smiling as if they didn’t have a care in the world. It looked like they were in a life-size Zen garden.

Angela turned the photo facedown. Then she opened the matches and struck one until a flame appeared. She lowered the match to the candle’s wick, igniting it. Then she took the feather, stuck it in the flame and watched it burn into a pile of ash.
She was done with luck. From now on, she was going to make her own.

The next morning, while Elijah watched TV, Angela and her mom sat at the breakfast table. Angela kept pushing the cornflakes around with her spoon, hoping her mother wouldn’t notice she wasn’t eating. Her mom took a sip of coffee and then lowered her mug.
“Angela, we need to talk about what happened yesterday,” she said. “I’m sorry I yelled at you like that. I shouldn’t have. But you made a really poor decision. Elijah could have gotten hurt.”
“I know. I’m sorry. I messed up.”
“Promise me it won’t ever happen again.”
“I promise.”
Her mom went to speak again, but then the phone rang. She went into the other room to talk. She returned a few minutes later.

“That was Ms. Chavez’s daughter,” she said. “The bad news is that Ms. Chavez’s COVID test came back positive and you guys need to be tested. The good news is that she’s doing much better and they think she’s going to be okay.”
“That’s good,” Angela said. “I was worried about her.”
“Me, too, honey. COVID is scary.”

“Yeah,” Angela said, lowering her spoon. “Mom, I have something to tell you.” She was nervous, but she knew she couldn’t hold it in anymore. “I’ve been scared a lot lately. And not just cause of Ms. Chavez. It’s everything. I’m always worried and I’m having trouble sleeping. I don’t want to eat most days and I’m nervous all the time.”
Angela’s mom reached out and grabbed her hand.
“Oh, honey. You know you can always talk with me, right? You don’t have to go through this alone. And I’m sorry I haven’t been there for you lately. I want to do a better job.”
Tears rolled down her mother’s cheeks.
“It’s just — this whole thing — it is scary and it’s really hard. And I thought I could do it all, but I can’t. Not alone. Not without your dad.”
Angela couldn’t remember the last time her mom had mentioned him. He had been the ghost in the room for a long time, the person they constantly thought about, but never mentioned.
“I miss him,” Angela said.
“I know, honey. But your dad — well I guess he just let himself get a little too out of control. And he needs to fix that for himself. I hope someday he does.”
“Me, too.”
Angela’s mom smiled and then gave her a big hug. It was the longest and closest one they’d shared in a long time.
“Well,” her mom said, wiping away tears. “Let’s get your COVID test scheduled. I know you don’t want to get it, but you need to.”
“It’s okay,” Angela said. “I’d rather know one way or the other. And I don’t want to risk getting anyone sick.”

Although she wasn’t crazy about the idea of getting a swab stuck up her nose, Angela knew it would be okay. Maybe her luck would be good, and it would be negative. Or maybe it would be positive. The results were completely out of her control. But the decision to get tested so she could have peace of mind and keep others safe, that was something she had power over. So rather than focus on her fear, or the what-ifs, she focused on that — on the ways her actions could help other people.

It took a few days for the results to come back, but when they did both Angela and her brother were negative. In the meantime, her mother switched to a high-speed Internet plan and made some adjustments to her teaching schedule, so that Angela could attend her classes. There were still times when Angela had to be outside watching Elijah, but she set up a table and chair in the yard, so that she could either catch-up on her homework, play on her phone or practice her sewing stitches.
One afternoon a few weeks later, Ms. Chavez emerged from her house. She looked a little weaker than before, but just as feisty.
“Well, that was a rough couple of weeks,” Ms. Chavez said. “How have you guys been doing? I missed you.”
“We missed you, too,” Angela said. “Are you all better?”
“Yes. It was scary there for a while. But nothing a little ‘Sana, Sana Colita de Rana,’ couldn’t fix.”
“Colita de Rana?” Angela asked.
“Oh, it’s just a little good luck poem you say to children when they’re sick. ‘Heal, heal, little frog’s tail. If you don’t heal today, you’ll heal tomorrow.’”
“I love frogs!” Elijah said.
Ms. Chavez laughed. “I do, too. Frogs are the best. I’m glad to see you’re doing well, Elijah. I like your new glasses.”
Elijah thanked her and then went back to playing with his trucks.
“So, I’ve seen you sewing out here, Angela. It looks like you’re getting pretty good.”
“Thanks. I’m enjoying it.”

“Well, good. Because I’ve been thinking about a project we could do together. I don’t want anyone else catching this virus. And the Navajo Nation has been hit really hard and they’re looking for people to sew masks and donate them. So maybe you and I could make some masks — some for your family and friends and some for the Navajo. How does that sound?”
Angela smiled. It was a real chance to make a difference, to do something positive at a time when so many other things were out of her control.
“I would love that.”

So, over the following weeks, Angela and Ms. Chavez measured, cut, and stitched until they had a collection of masks in different shapes and sizes. As Angela inspected her handiwork, she looked up at the sky.

There, in the distance, she saw a lone hot air balloon flying through the clouds. Angela didn’t know when it would land or where — it was impossible to predict such things. Pilots could use the burner to lift the balloon up or let gravity pull it down, but the direction the wind blew them in was beyond their control. That’s why they had chase crews on the ground to track the balloon’s movements. By working together, they could land the balloon safely and make the world a little more beautiful, if only for a moment.

options.label: There are things that happen in life that we cannot control.
Superstitions can help people feel in control of their lives.
Bad luck can be reversed if one believes it can be.
What goes around, comes around.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements best describe a major theme of the story?

options.label: "'Well, I believe in it. My grandmother died on Friday the 13th'" (Paragraph 8)

"And yet, she couldn't help but feel there was something she needed to do to fix everything, to reverse the bad karma her broken mirror had brought into the world, to stop the dark thoughts racing around her head" (Paragraph 79)
"She knew she had messed up. Maybe her bad luck had contributed to what happened, but it wasn't to blame." (Paragraph 132)
"Angela smiled. It was a real chance to make a difference, to do something positive at a time when so many other things were out of her control." (Paragraph 171)

questions.prompt: Which of the following details best supports the claim that Angela feels helpless?

options.label: Angela is given an opportunity to tell her mom about her fears and worries.
Angela realizes that her mother is behind the bad luck in her family.
Angela realizes that a good luck charm helped save Elijah's life.
Angela is given an opportunity to use her sewing skills.

questions.prompt: How does Elijah's accident help develop the plot of the story?

options.label: She is hopeful.
She is in despair.
She is uncertain.
She is confused.

questions.prompt: What does the description of the hot air balloon in paragraph 174 suggest about Angela's mood?
How does Angela's thoughts on luck change throughout the story?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In "The 13th," how does Angela's fear about COVID-19 drive her actions? Think of a scary situation in your life. How did you react? Was it positive or negative? Why do you think fear caused you to react the way you did?

In "The 13th," Angela is convinced that a roadrunner feather will reverse her bad luck. What are your views on superstitions? Who are the superstitious people in your life and what are their attitudes toward good and bad luck?

In "The 13th," Angela is negatively impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic but manages to respond positively, in the end. We were all negatively impacted by the pandemic. What were some of the ways in which you were able to respond positively to your new circumstances?

articles.title: Four-Leaf Clover
articles.author: Ella Higginson
articles.description: The speaker of the poem explains where and how to find a four-leaf clover.
articles.intro: Ella Higginson (1862-1940) was an American award-winning author of fiction, poetry, and essays whose writing drew international attention to the Pacific Northwest region of the United States. "Four-Leaf Clover," her most well-known poem, explains how to find a four-leaf clover.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the main idea of each stanza.
articles.permissions: Public Domain

blocks.text: I know a place where the sun is like gold,
And the cherry blooms burst with snow,
And down underneath is the loveliest nook,
Where the four-leaf clovers grow.
One leaf is for hope, and one is for faith,
And one is for love, you know,
And God put another in for luck —
If you search, you will find where they grow.
But you must have hope, and you must have faith,
You must love and be strong – and so —
If you work, if you wait, you will find the place
Where the four-leaf clovers grow.

options.label: Luck is a combination of hope, faith, love, patience, and hard work.
Nature is beautiful and will bring luck to those who appreciate it.
A four-leaf clover can only be found in beautiful, sunny places.
Four-leaf clovers will bring luck to the person who finds it.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses a theme of the poem?

options.label: discovering a beautiful place
uncovering a rare plant
creating your own luck
learning to love others

options.label: Snow is covering the trees.
Four-leaf clovers grow where it is cold.
The area is exploding with four-leaf clovers.
The white flowers on the tree look like snow.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Four-leaf clovers are believed to be lucky. What is luck? Do you believe in luck? Why or why not? What is fate? Do you believe people are in control of their fate or what happens to them? Why or why not? How are luck and fate similar?

Because four-leaf clovers are rare, if you find one it is thought to bring you luck. What are some other things in nature that are symbols for things such as luck, love, bad luck, or hope. Why do you think humans view nature as symbolic?

articles.title: Amphibians
articles.author: Joseph O. Legaspi
articles.description: In this poem, the speaker compares immigrants to amphibians.
articles.intro: Joseph O. Legaspi is a poet who was born and raised in the Philippines. When Legaspi was 12, he immigrated to Los Angeles with his family. In this poem, a speaker compares immigrants to amphibians.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the experiences that amphibians and immigrants share.
articles.permissions: Copyright © 2014 by Joseph O. Legaspi. Reprinted from Split This Rock's The Quarry: A Social Justice Poetry Database . We encourage you to use and share these poems widely and ask only that you credit the author and name Split This Rock's The Quarry: A Social Justice Poetry Database as the source. Please also include a direct link to the database for any reproduction of poems.

blocks.text: Amphibians live in both.
Immigrants leave their land,
hardening in the sea.
Out of water.
In Greek, amphibian means
“on both sides of life.”
Terra and aqua . Shoreline.
In fresh water:
amphibians lay
shell-less eggs;
immigrants give birth
to Americans.
Tadpoles, polliwogs
metamorphose: gills
in early stages. On land,
amphibians develop lungs.
Immigrants develop lungs.
Through damp skin
amphibians oxygenate.
Immigrants toil
and sleep breathlessly.
Skin forms glands.
Eyes form eyelids.
Amphibians seek land; immigrants, other lands.
Their colors brighten, camouflage.
They’ve been known to fall
out of the sky.
Fully at home in the rain.

options.label: Immigrants are forced to adapt to their surroundings when they move to a new country.
Amphibians are strong animals because of their ability to live on land and in water.
Immigrants struggle to be accepted in the new countries that they make their homes.
Amphibians and immigrants are better off once they go through a transformation.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement identifies the main theme of the poem?

options.label: "In Greek, amphibian means / 'on both sides of life.'" (Lines 5-6)
"Terra and aqua. Shoreline. / In fresh water" (Lines 7-8)
"Immigrants toil / and sleep breathlessly." (Lines 20-21)
"Their colors brighten, camouflage." (Line 25)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the poem best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: They highlight how easily immigrants can move from one home to another.
They reveal how out of place immigrants can feel in a new land.
They emphasize the process that immigrants have to go through to adjust to a new land.
They prove that some immigrants can't adapt to life in a new land.

questions.prompt: How do lines 13-17 develop the speaker's ideas about immigrants in the poem?
What does the water in the poem represent in the lives and experiences of immigrants?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the poem, the speaker compares immigrants to amphibians because they both change as they move to a new environment. Can you think of other comparisons that could also describe the experiences of immigrants? If so, describe them.

In the poem, the speaker describes the many changes immigrants have to go through when they come to America. How does the experience of an immigrant help us understand what it means to be brave? How might bravery be an important quality for someone who is traveling or moving to a new place?

The speaker discusses the physical changes that amphibians go through. How do you think someone's identity might be affected when they arrive in a new country? What new ideas might they have about who they are and where they belong? Describe a time when you changed as a result of a new experience.

articles.title: Most Valuable Player
articles.author: Sarah Van Arsdale
articles.description: A speaker describes what it would be like to have a trophy for softball.
articles.intro: Sarah Van Arsdale is an author, poet, and professor. In this poem, a speaker describes her goals.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, make note of the speaker’s goals and aspirations.
articles.permissions: "Most Valuable Player" by Sarah Van Arsdale. First published in Korone , vol. V, 1988. Copyright © 1988 by Sarah Van Arsdale. Used by permission of the author. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: If I had a trophy
I’d put it on the middle shelf
of my bookcase. I’d dust
it every day
and polish it once a week.
It would have a statue of a woman
holding a bat, her golden arm
cocked up a little
waiting for the pitch.
When my friends came over
I’d stand next to the bookcase casual-like
till they said, “Is that a trophy?”
I’d read the inscription every morning.
I’d ask someone to take my picture
with my trophy.
My trophy would say
“Softball Player” on the bottom,
and everyone would know
that in summer I tie on my cleats
run onto the field,
slapping high fives.
They’d know I take third base,
put my glove to the dry dirt,
scatter dust in the air.
They’d hear the fans shout,
“Hey, some catch!”
when the white ball comes slamming
into my glove,
and, “Watch out, she’ll steal home,”
as my cleats dig and dig.
They’d feel the weight of the little statue
And think, “I bet she’s going out
With her team tonight,”
“I bet she could teach me how to throw,”
“I bet she plays softball,”
and I do,
I do.

options.label: People want to be recognized.
Sometimes people don't mind when their skills go unrewarded.
The support of friends and family is important to succeed.
Rewards and trophies do not determine an individual's skill or success.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following identifies the theme of the poem?

options.label: "I'd dust / it every day / and polish it once a week." (Lines 3-5)

articles.title: The Underground Railroad
articles.author: USHistory.org
articles.description: This text provides a brief overview of the history and the impact of the Underground Railroad.
articles.intro: In the 1850's and 1860's, the United States became even more divided on the issue of slavery. States in the South still practiced slavery, while many states in the North prohibited slavery. As a result, many slaves tried to run away to freedom in North. The Underground Railroad was established to provide a secret way for slaves to escape from slavery in the South to freedom in the North.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the Underground Railroad helped slaves to freedom in the North.
articles.permissions: "The Underground Railroad" by USHistory.org. Copyright © 2016, CC BY 4.0. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: Any cause needs speakers and organizers. Any mass movement requires men and women of great ideas. But information and mobilization are not enough. To be successful, revolutionary change requires people of action — those who little by little chip away at the forces who stand in the way. Such were the “conductors” of the Underground Railroad. Not content to wait for laws to change or for slavery to implode itself, railroad activists helped individual fugitive slaves find the light of freedom. The Underground Railroad was not an actual railroad, but was instead a network of safe houses and routes slaves could take to escape from the South to freedom in the "Harriet Tubman" by H. Seymour Squyer is in North. the public domain. The Underground Railroad operated at night. Slaves were moved from “station” to “station” by abolitionists. These “stations” were usually homes and churches — any safe place to rest and eat before continuing on the journey to freedom in the North, sometimes as far away as 1. Content(adjective)in a state of peace, happiness, or satisfaction 2. “Implode” in this sense means to self-destruct. 3. A fugitive slave was someone who ran away from the slave owner and went into hiding to avoid being taken back. 4. Abolitionism was a movement to "abolish," or end, slavery. Canada. Often whites would pretend to be the masters of fugitives to prevent their capture. Sometimes lighter-skinned African Americans took this role. In one spectacular case, Henry “Box” Brown arranged for a friend to put him in a wooden box, where he had only a few biscuits and some water. His friend mailed him to the North, where bemused abolitionists received him in Philadelphia. Most of the time, however, slaves crept northward on their own, looking for the signal that designated the next safe haven. This was indeed risky business, because slave catchers and sheriffs were constantly on the lookout. Over 3,200 people are known to have worked on the railroad between 1830 and the end of the Civil War. Many will remain forever anonymous.

Perhaps the most outstanding “conductor” of the Underground Railroad was Harriet Tubman. Born a slave herself, after she escaped to Philadelphia, she began working on the railroad to free her family members. Throughout the 1850s, Tubman made 19 separate trips into slave territory. She was terribly serious about her mission. She threatened to shoot any slave who had second thoughts with the pistol she carried on her hip. By the end of the decade, she was responsible for freeing about 300 slaves. When the Civil War broke out, she used her knowledge from working the railroad to serve as a spy for the Union. Needless to say, slave owners did not appreciate the Underground Railroad. Although they disliked Abolitionist talk and literature, the railroad was far worse. To them, these were simple cases of stolen property. Slave catchers often traveled to the North to try to recapture freed slaves. When Northerners rallied around freed slaves and refused to compensate former slave owners, Southerners felt they were being robbed of property. This disagreement over freed slaves lay yet another brick of the foundation for the South to eventually secede from the Union and help spark the American Civil War.

options.label: They were responsible for driving the trains that took slaves from slavery in the South to freedom in the North.
Even though it was not an actual railroad, the "conductors" were individuals responsible for helping slaves travel from the South to the North.
They conducted trains and boats that took fugitive slaves back to their masters in the South.
They carried pistols on their hips that were known by people in the North as "conductors."

questions.prompt: Why does the author choose to call the individuals who worked on the Underground Railroad "conductors"?

"Most of the time, however, slaves crept Northward on their own, looking for the signal that designated the next safe haven." (Paragraph 4)
"Perhaps the most outstanding 'conductor' of the Underground Railroad was Harriet Tubman." (Paragraph 5)

articles.title: Turning the Tide
articles.author: Shay Maunz
articles.description: Scientists are creating artificial reefs to help protect our oceans' ecosystems.
articles.intro: The environment is everything that surrounds animals in the natural world. An ecosystem is a community of all the living things in one environment. In this article, Shay Maunz describes a new way scientists are helping ocean ecosystems.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how artificial reefs benefit the ocean.
articles.permissions: "Turning the Tide" by Shay Maunz ©TIME For Kids. All rights reserved. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: The Tappan Zee Bridge, north of New York City, carried automobiles across the Hudson River for 62 years. But in 2017, a new bridge replaced it. The next year, the old bridge was demolished. Workers picked apart the structure piece by piece.

Now those pieces are being recycled. Massive chunks of metal and concrete have been taken from the Tappan Zee, cleaned, transported to one of several locations off the New York State coastline, and dropped into the Atlantic Ocean. The old bridge is beginning a new life as an artificial reef.
Under the Sea

Natural reefs are stonelike underwater ridges made of living things called corals. Coral reefs are an important part of the ocean because they serve as a habitat for marine life. They attract tourists to a region, which is good for local economies. Reefs also help prevent erosion and protect coastal communities from flooding. “Reefs are great for nature and for people,” Bill Ulfelder told TIME for Kids. He’s with the Nature Conservancy in New York.

But coral reefs are in trouble. That’s why people are finding ways to build them from artificial materials. Artificial reefs aren’t made of coral, but they can provide many of the same benefits. Over time, coral may even begin to grow on them.

Ulfelder’s group works to revitalize New York waterways, which were harmed by pollution in the 20th century. In recent years, the water quality has improved. Artificial reefs are bringing marine animals back to the area. “Reefs are the big cities of the ocean. They’re vibrant places, with an incredible diversity of marine life,” Ulfelder says. “By creating artificial reefs, we’re able to create new pockets of that kind of habitat.”
Help for Troubled Waters
Artificial reefs are created by placing large objects on the ocean floor. Sometimes, objects are built specifically to be used as reefs. They are often made of limestone, steel, and concrete.

Structures that are no longer being used can also be recycled into reefs. That’s what happened with the Tappan Zee Bridge. Aircraft carriers, subway cars, and military tanks have all been reused in this way. Before being dropped into the ocean, the objects are cleaned and stripped of any materials that could pose a threat to ocean life.

Many artificial reefs are made using decommissioned ships. In 2005, Jim Walsh helped turn a ship called the Carthaginian II into a reef off the coast of Hawaii. Now, more than a decade later, it’s a popular spot for scuba diving, fishing, and submarine tours. Providing another option for tourists reduces the number of visitors to natural coral reefs. This eases pressure on natural habitats.

When the Carthaginian II landed on the ocean floor in 2005, it was little more than a chunk of metal on a bare patch of sand. Now, Walsh estimates that the ship is home to at least 75 animal species, including unicorn fish, goatfish, and moray eels. “It’s amazing to see how much life is on that ship,” Walsh says.

options.label: Environmental conservationists are protecting marine life by installing artificial reefs underwater.
Environmental conservationists are perplexed about how to solve the problem of water pollution.
Environmental conservationists believe that it is more important to help ocean animals than people.
Environmental conservationists are people who work to take old structures and give them a new purpose.

articles.title: Eight reasons to not be spooked by spiders this Halloween
articles.author: Lizzy Lowe
articles.description: Lizzy Lowe provides eight reasons for why you shouldn't be afraid of spiders.
articles.intro: Many people have a fear of spiders, but how reasonable is this fear? Lizzy Lowe provides eight reasons for why people don't need to be frightened of spiders.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on unique skills that spiders have.
articles.permissions: " Eight reasons not to be spooked by spiders this Halloween " by Lizzy Lowe, Macquarie University, October 30, 2017. Copyright © The Conversation 2017, CC-BY-ND.

blocks.text: Spiders are a traditional part of a Halloween scare, and for some people, they have been a source of fear for years. But in reality, spiders deserve to instill a sense of amazement, not fear.
Spiders have amazing survival strategies, beautiful color displays, complex social interactions, and play important roles in our ecosystems.
Here are eight reasons (one for each leg... or eye!) to love, not hate spiders.
1. They like to play dress-up
There are many species of spider that have evolved to look very unspiderlike in order to avoid being eaten or to sneak up on their prey.
One fantastic example is a group of spiders that look like bird poo when they curl up on a leaf. They even leave a white pool of silk to complete the effect.
This is a sneaky strategy to avoid predation by birds and can fool even the sharpest of eyes.
Other spiders have evolved to look like ants to such an extent that it’s very difficult to distinguish between the spiders and their hosts.
To complete the illusion these spiders even move like ants and often hold up their front two legs to look like antennae.
2. They love their kids
You might think they are cold-blooded killers, but some spider moms will do anything for their kids.
A wolf spider mom will carry all her offspring around on her back until they are big enough to avoid predators and catch food on their own.
There is even a spider that loves her kids so much that after they’re born she fills her body with delicious nutrients and lets her babies eat her while she’s still alive. Now that’s dedication.
3. They’re great housemates
Unlike your average housemate, spiders definitely pull their weight around the house.
They are quiet, clean, considerate and will happily clear out all of the unwanted bugs living under your cupboards.
Spiders are generalist predators that play important roles in controlling pests, including cockroaches and flies around your house if you’ll let them.
4. They have an artistic side
The large circular webs that some spiders build are primarily used to catch prey, but they can also be a canvas for spiders who like to create masterpieces.
St Andrews cross spiders are common across Australia and are well known for the striking white cross in their webs which helps them to attract prey.
But for some reason, the juveniles like to get creative and often draw a circle of zig-zags.
Other spiders are hoarders and store interesting things like bits of old food, snail shells, flowers, and leaves in their webs.
There are even spiders who use debris to draw images of larger spiders — presumably to trick predators into thinking that they are larger than they actually are. Or perhaps it’s a self-portrait.
5. They’re great fishing buddies
A spider might not be the first mate you would choose to take on a fishing trip, but Dolomedes spiders are great anglers.
They live in and around the water and are able to catch small fish, tadpoles, and even frogs. They can also catch you insects to use for bait.
6. They dress (and dance) to impress
We all know it can be hard to catch the eye of that special someone, but the little peacock spiders have courtship down to an art.
In addition to the colourful patterns on their flared abdomens, the males dance with their back legs and drum with their front legs to attract a female.
7. They can be social butterflies
Social insects such as bees and ants have communal living down to an art. But did you also know there are also social spiders?
Sociality has evolved several times in spiders, and groups will often work together to catch food and raise offspring.

In Australia, there are social huntsmen who live in groups of up to 300 individuals under the bark of trees. These groups usually consist of one dominant female and many of her daughters who will help her to raise the younger generations.
8. They’re actually not very dangerous
A recent study showed that less than 0.5% of all spider species pose a threat to humans, and even fewer can actually kill you.
So don’t be spooked by any Halloween spiders you encounter this year.

options.label: Our planet would be devastated if spiders were removed from it.
Spiders have an interesting history rooted in Halloween that should be celebrated.
The deadly spiders have given all spiders a bad reputation.
Spiders are valuable creatures with impressive skills that shouldn't be feared.

articles.title: How far away was that lightning?
articles.author: Becky Bolinger
articles.description: Becky Bolinger discusses how you can tell how far away lightning is.
articles.intro: When lightning strikes, it's reassuring to know just how far that bolt of electricity is. In this informational text, Becky Bolinger discusses how you can calculate how far away lightning is.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the difference between seeing lightning and hearing thunder.
articles.permissions: " How far away was that lightning? " by Becky Bolinger, Colorado State University, June 7, 2018. Copyright © The Conversation 2018, CC-BY-ND.

blocks.text: You probably do it. It might be ingrained from when you were a kid, and now it’s almost automatic. You see the flash of lightning — and you immediately start counting the seconds till it thunders.

But does counting really get you a good estimate for how far away the lightning is? Is this one of those old wives’ tales,or is it actually based on science? In this case, we have physics to thank for this quick and easy — and pretty accurate — calculation.
So what happens when a big storm rolls in?
The lightning you see is the discharge of electricity that travels between clouds or to the ground. The thunder you hear is the rapid expansion of the air in response to the lightning’s intense heat.

If you’re really close to the lightning, you will see it and hear the thunder simultaneously. But when it’s far away, you see and hear the event at different times. That’s because light travels much faster than sound. Think of sitting in the nosebleed seats at a baseball game. You see the batter hit the ball a second before you hear the crack of the bat.

When observing an event on Earth, you see things almost the instant they happen — the speed of light is so fast you can’t even detect the travel time. The speed of sound is much slower, which gives us time to do our calculation.

Let’s simplify the speed equation: Sound travels a little over 700 miles per hour, or 700 miles in 3,600 seconds. That means 7 miles traveled every 36 seconds. Make this even easier and round down to 7 miles every 35 seconds… or 1 mile every 5 seconds! Count to 5: if you hear thunder, the lightning occurred within 1 mile.

Now that you know how far away that lightning strike was, is it far enough to be a safe distance from the storm? That’s actually a trick question. Thunder can be heard up to 25 miles away, and lightning strikes have been documented to occur as far as 25 miles from thunderstorms — known as a “bolt from the blue.” So if you can hear thunder, you’re close enough to be hit by lightning, and sheltering indoors or in an enclosed car is your safest bet.

And don’t count on the folk wisdom that lightning never strikes the same place twice to protect you. That one is just plain wrong. For example, lightning strikes the top of the Empire State Building an average of 23 times per year.

options.label: The higher you're able to count between when you see lightning and hear thunder, the safer it is to go outside to witness that event in nature.
Counting how many seconds pass between when you see lightning and hear thunder can tell you how far away it is because sound and light travel at different speeds.
While light and sound usually travel at the same speed, the light from lightning strikes so fast that we struggle to process it and end up processing sound first.
The widely held beliefs that you can calculate the distance of lightning by counting and that lightning doesn't strike twice are actually supported by science.

"So if you can hear thunder, you're close enough to be hit by lightning, and sheltering indoors or in an enclosed car is your safest bet." (Paragraph 8)

It stresses how most of the "facts" about lightning are actually legends.

questions.prompt: How does paragraph 9 contribute to our understanding about lightning?
What is the relationship between determining how far away lightning is and safety?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the text, the author discusses how we can see the different speeds of light and sound in our daily lives. Describe a time when you saw something occur before you heard it.

articles.title: Manifest Destiny, I Do Believe
articles.author: BirdBrain History
articles.description: A missionary and pioneer writes of her plans to travel west and "civilize" the Indians.
articles.intro: In this letter, Cordelia writes to her friend about her plans to become a pioneer, a common ambition during the "Manifest Destiny" era of western settlement in the 19th century.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, note how Cordelia mentions God and God's influence on her plans and actions.
articles.permissions: "Manifest Destiny, I Do Believe", © 2016, BirdBrain. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: Dear Emily,

This is farewell, dear friend. My family and I set out tomorrow. Out past the horizon to see what waits for us in the west. Will there be gold? Will there be trouble? Will there be some place we can call home? Truly, I am terrified. But I know it is the will of President Polk and of God for us to spread as far as we can. So, we will follow that setting sun to our destiny.

We are going into territory that no one has gone before. I don’t just mean the well you and I explored when we were young, dear Emily. No white people have set foot on the path we’re about to take. A pioneer, after all, is someone who explores a country for the very first time. Yes, those Indians were here first, but we do not think of them as Christians. Not yet, anyway.

Some people try and kill every Indian they see. But you know me. I am a woman of God. They have blood flowing through their veins just like any other person. That blood can be blessed. I’m a missionary, and it is my goal to bring anyone I can into the light of Christianity. I’ve seen the Indians with their wives and their children. They seem like they can be good people. They are good hunters, for sure, better than my husband even, but don’t tell him I said. I know in my heart that these Indians deserve God’s forgiveness, like the rest of us.

What makes me so daring to go out into the dark, you ask? I know that I will have God’s help. Manifest Destiny says that God has chosen us to take over this country, all the way to the coast. This means we are meant to overcome everything that gets in our way, be that wars or bad terrain or Indians. Nothing will stand in the way of the pioneers settling this great new land of ours. At least, I hope not.

It’s dark out there. There are no maps showing us where to go. Dangers could wait around every corner. But as the pioneers bravely march into these new mysterious places, we carry with us a light. We carry the light of civilization, man’s most advanced state of society. The wild land will become fields of crops. The Indians will become Christian. The darkness will become light ... at least in our eyes.

Please keep me in your prayers as my husband, Sarah, and I set out into the darkness. I have faith in Manifest Destiny, that the pioneers were meant to bring the light of civilization to this land. As missionaries, my husband and I will bring the word of God to the Indians, if they will have us. Really, Emily, I would have no confidence in myself were it not for God’s guiding hand. I know that this is his goal to bring the pioneers to the far sea. But to tell you the truth, I am afraid.
I’ll write you often and let you know if God’s work is unfolding.
Love,
Cordelia

options.label: God first created the land for Indians but then decided to give Americans control because he preferred their civilization.
God has given Americans the will to spread Christian American civilization from coast to coast.
It is guaranteed that missionaries will bring Christianity to American Indians, and Cordelia does not want to miss the opportunity to help.
Pioneers are more likely to succeed if they are also missionaries because then God has blessed them.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following statements best identifies the central theme of the text?

"Manifest Destiny says that God has chosen us to take over this country, all the way to the coast." (Paragraph 5) E. "The wild land will become fields of crops. The Indians will become Christian. The darkness will become light …" (Paragraph 6) F. "I would have no confidence in myself were it not for God's guiding hand." (Paragraph 7)

Cordelia's plans seem brave and necessary.

articles.title: Union wins a victory at Port Royal Sound, South Carolina
articles.author: Carolyn Reeder
articles.description: In the Civil War, the Union experienced an early victory in the Battle of Port Royal.
articles.intro: The American Civil War (1861-1865) was fought within the United States between northern and southern states. Eleven southern states withdrew from the United States to form the Confederate States of America, while the remaining states formed the Union. On November 7, 1861, the Union captured Port Royal Sound in South Carolina, marking one of the Union's early victories against the Confederacy.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the Union was able to overcome the Confederacy.
articles.permissions: From The Washington Post , November 8, 2011 © 2011 The Washington Post. All rights reserved. Used by permission and protected by the Copyright Laws of the United States. The printing, copying, redistribution, or retransmission of this Content without express written permission is prohibited.

blocks.text: People in the North were worried about the way the war was going. They had thought it would be easy to defeat the South, but here it was, six months into the war — the beginning of November 1861 — and the Confederates had won the three most important battles.

Now, though, exciting news gave hope to the North and shook the confidence of the South. The papers were full of stories about a huge fleet of Union ships heading south —not just warships but also ships carrying thousands of soldiers, ships full of supplies for the men, ships full of coal and a ship full of ammunition — 77 ships in all. Just one question was left unanswered: What part of the South would be invaded?
An important port

Some Confederate leaders were sure the enemy fleet would try to take over South Carolina’s Port Royal Sound, a wide inlet off the Atlantic near the state’s southern border. They were right. Union officials had been planning this expedition for months. They needed the harbor at Port Royal as a base for ships that patrolled the southern coast to keep the Confederacy from importing weapons and exporting cotton.

Two things happened when the Union fleet began to arrive outside the harbor. Plantation owners and people in the nearby town of Beaufort fled, leaving behind most of their belongings — including their slaves. And Confederate soldiers prepared to defend Southern territory, even though they had poor-quality weapons and were outnumbered more than 5 to 1.
Victory comes quickly

The November 7 battle was over in a matter of hours. The Confederate forts, one on each side of the wide entry to Port Royal Sound, didn’t stand a chance against cannon fire from the Union warships that steamed past them. The forts’ defenders fired back, of course, and though hitting moving targets is hard, they did cause some damage. Soon, though, they were forced to lower their flags and retreat. The Union soldiers went ashore, took over the forts and the Sea Islands, which surrounded the sound, and then occupied Beaufort.
A problem and solution

People in the North were overjoyed by the great naval victory at Port Royal Sound and the occupation of Southern territory. But the army soon realized it had a problem: what should they do with the slaves left behind by their owners — nearly 10,000 men, women and children who were making their way to the army camps. The solution? Hire them. Many stayed on the plantations, raising cotton and running the cotton gins — but now they earned wages paid by the U.S. government. Some lived in army camps and were paid for doing jobs for the military.

In the spring, missionaries arrived to set up schools, and within a year the government began building a town for former slaves. The town was called Mitchelville, named after the general whose idea it had been to give the former slaves living in the army camps a chance to live as free people did — in homes they built themselves in a town they governed themselves. It was a way to prepare them for life after the war.

“The Port Royal Experiment,” as it was called, showed that former slaves could live as free people. The experiment ended when the war did, however: After the army left the area, there were no more jobs — and that doomed the town of Mitchelville.

options.label: The Union's victory at Port Royal Sound was a clear indicator that the Union would win the war.
The Union's success at Port Royal Sound restored their confidence in being able to beat the Confederacy, despite the Confederacy's proven strength.
The Union's victory at Port Royal Sound and success developing Mitchelville was a turning point in the Civil War for the Confederacy.
The Union's failure to develop a town of former slaves made northerners question the future of a post-slavery nation.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement best expresses the central idea of the text?

options.label: "People in the North were worried about the way the war was going. They had thought it would be easy to defeat the South" (Paragraph 1)

"People in the North were overjoyed by the great naval victory at Port Royal Sound and the occupation of Southern territory." (Paragraph 6)
"In the spring, missionaries arrived to set up schools, and within a year the government began building a town for former slaves." (Paragraph 7)

options.label: The newspapers prematurely reported a Union victory at Port Royale Sound, causing Confederate troops to abandon their orders in the area.

articles.title: Why Does No One Ever Thank Me for the Magna Carta?
articles.author: BirdBrain History
articles.description: King John reflects on why everyone should express gratitude for his signing of the Magna Carta.
articles.intro: In this story, King John tells you why he signed the Magna Carta in 1215. The Magna Carta was England's first constitution and limited the power of the king by giving rights to the people. Today's U.S. Constitution uses some of the same concepts first written in the Magna Carta.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify the rights King John agreed to give his people by signing the Magna Carta.
articles.permissions: "Why Does No One Ever Thank Me for the Magna Carta?" by BirdBrain History. Copyright © 2016 by BirdBrain. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.

blocks.text: This article was brought to you by ... the years 1199-1225! I was not a popular king. How could I be, living in the shadow of my older brother, Richard? Richard was popular. The people loved him. They loved him even though he made them pay lots of taxes. Then Richard died, and I became king. The people hated me. Maybe it’s because I lost so many battles. Or maybe it’s because I tried to make the English people pay lots of taxes for those battles. But what no one ever seems to notice is that without the things I’ve done, the world may not have "A romanticised 19th-century recreation of become a place where many people can vote King John signing the Magna Carta" by James William Edmund Doyle is in the public domain. for their leaders. (Even if I did have to do it against my will.) It isn’t easy being king. You must recognize that being a liked king doesn’t mean being a good king. Don’t understand what I mean? Then listen to my story. My name is King John. There are parts of your life that you enjoy today that you probably don’t think about. Simple things, like the fact that if you buy something, you get to keep it. Your rights are the things you are allowed to do under the law and also ways that the law protects you. You have these rights because of a set of rules written by the leaders of your country ... although some believe that all people should just have these rights, no matter what is written down by their leaders. Believe it 1. King Richard of England ruled from 1189 C.E. until his death in 1199 C.E. 2. King John of England ruled from 1199 C.E. until his death in 1216 C.E. or not, some of the protection the law gives you now is based on the Magna Carta, which I was forced to sign in 1215. Of course, it was written in Latin, so I don’t expect you to read it. I can tell you how it came to be though, and how your rights came to be with it. It is a king’s job to keep his land rich, large, and safe so that he holds more power and can take better care of his people. That’s how I saw things anyway. It was a rough time when I ruled England. I fought to win back land that England once held in France ... and I lost the fight. Battles are very expensive and they cost the kingdom a lot of money. Where else was I supposed to get it back? A tax is a something people pay to their government. I asked the people to help the kingdom by paying a lot of taxes ... but they did not want to. Why did I ask them to pay? They live in my kingdom; they should help pay for it! Yes, yes, I know now they were mad because I was trying to take too much money from them, but ... well ...

It was those barons, the leaders of the people, who said I was taking too much money. They said they would fight me if I tried to ask for any more taxes. I, King John, being a good king, did not want a civil war. I didn’t want the Magna Carta either, but they wrote it up and they forced me to sign it. Again, it was against my will. The paper said I should not be able to take money from the people without their having some rights, those lovely things that you enjoy in the U.S. today. The people now had representation. Representation in government is when someone speaks for you when the government makes decisions. Some barons in England didn’t think the decisions I made to tax the people were what the people wanted. Now they would represent the people and help me make decisions, but I didn’t like this at all. It took away my power! Just remember, it was my seal that made the Magna Carta official. I may not have been happy about signing it. But now that I see it makes people very happy, I just want you to know that it was I, King John. There’s a part in the Magna Carta that the people seemed to really like. One freedom that the people have kept and fought for ever since. Habeas Corpus is a law that says a person who has is arrested must go before a court that will decide if that person should go to jail or not. This was made so that people would not be thrown behind bars and kept there for no reason. No one seems to thank me for it, but that’s because I fought against it. Sometimes when you don’t like someone, it’s just easier to throw them in jail ... forever ... right? You may say that it wasn’t me who gave you your rights. It was the barons who revolted. It was 3. The Magna Carta Libertatum was England’s first constitution. The Latin phrase means ‘the Greater Charter of the Liberties.” 4. Barons form the lowest rank of noble rulers in England. They led the people within their feudal lands – lands they had been granted by the king to organize for the common subjects. Barons represented the common people on their land but had to pay respects and taxes to all higher nobles, especially the king. 5. A civil war is a war between two opposing groups within the same country, often for control of the country. the people who would have started a civil war. You may say it was the barons who wrote the Magna Carta and made me sign it so they could have rights and pass them down to you. You may say that it was because I was a terrible king who lost all of my battles and tried to make up for it with high taxes. You may say that the people forced me do something good. But ... without me, would the rights ever have been made? Would the first version of the constitution that gives you so many rights have been made in 1215? The answer is no. But no one ever seems to thank King John.

options.label: Kings must protect their people and expand their territory through wars.
People deserve the right to representation in the government that they pay taxes to.
There should be the same universal human rights for all.
King John is misunderstood, for without him we would not have the first constitution with human rights.

"Now they would represent the people and help me make decisions, but I didn't like this at all. It took away my power!" (Paragraph 5)
"But now that I see it makes people very happy, I just want you to know that it was I, King John." (Paragraph 5)

He takes credit for the work of the people and the barons who forced him to sign the Magna Carta.
