articles.title: Why do volcanoes erupt?
articles.author: Heather Handley
articles.description: Heather Handley discusses why volcanoes erupt.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Heather Handley explains what volcanoes are and why they erupt.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the details provided about magma.
articles.permissions: " Why do volcanoes erupt? " by Heather Handley, Macquarie University, July 22, 2018. Copyright © The Conversation 2018, CC-BY-ND.

blocks.text: The rock inside the planet we live on can melt to form molten rock called magma. This magma is lighter than the rocks around it and so it rises upwards. Where the magma eventually reaches the surface we get an eruption and volcanoes form.

The top part of the Earth is made up of a number of hard pieces called tectonic plates. Magma and volcanoes often form where the plates are pulled apart or pushed together but we also find some volcanoes in the middle of tectonic plates.

Volcanoes have many different shapes and sizes, some look like steep mountains (stratovolcanoes), others look like bumps (shield volcanoes) and some are flat with a hole (a crater or caldera) in the centre that is often filled with water.
The shape of the volcano and how explosively it erupts depend largely on how “sticky” and how “fizzy” (how much gas) the magma is that is erupted.
For example, if you try to blow bubbles in cooking oil through a straw, the bubbles can escape quite easily because the cooking oil is runny.

If you try to blow bubbles in jam or peanut butter you would find it very difficult because the jam and peanut butter are very sticky, they wouldn’t move much at all if you tried to pour them out of the jar.
It is the same with volcanoes. When magma rises towards the surface gas bubbles start to form. Whether or not they can escape as the magma is rising affects how explosive the eruption will be.

Where the magma is runny like cooking oil and doesn’t have much bubbly gas mixed in it, such as places like Hawaii, then we see lots of slow-moving lava flows and shield volcanoes. Lava is what we call magma when it reaches the surface.

However, where the magma is very sticky, like jam or peanut butter, and if it contains a lot of bubbly gas then the gas can get stuck and eruptions can be very powerful and explosive, like the recent eruptions at Fuego volcano in Guatemala.
Damage caused by eruptions

In explosive eruptions the frothy, bubbly magma can be ripped apart into tiny bits called volcanic ash. This is not ash like you get after a barbecue or fire, it does not crumble away in your fingers. It is very sharp and is dangerous to breathe in.

Some explosive volcanoes can send ash high up into the sky and it can travel around the world over different countries. If aeroplanes travel through an ash cloud from a volcano it can cause a lot of damage to the engine.
Other explosive eruptions create fast-moving, hot clouds of volcanic ash, gas and rocks that travel down the sides of the volcanoes and destroy pretty much everything in their path.
The benefits of volcanoes

Despite the great damage they can cause, volcanoes also help us to live. Volcanic ash provides food for the soil around volcanoes which helps us grow plants to eat. The heat from some volcanoes is used to make energy to power lights, fridges, televisions and computers in people’s houses.

options.label: Volcanoes are the most destructive forces in nature and do little good for people, plants, or animals.
How large a volcanic eruption will be depends on the strength of the volcano's exterior and whether it can keep the lava in.
Volcanoes can vary in their shape and erupt in different ways which can be dangerous yet beneficial to humans.
Volcanic eruptions have a bad reputation for hurting people and causing damage, but the number of deaths caused by volcanoes is very small.

"Volcanoes have many different shapes and sizes, some look like steep mountains (stratovolcanoes), others look like bumps (shield volcanoes)…" (Paragraph 3)

"For example, if you try to blow bubbles in cooking oil through a straw, the bubbles can escape quite easily because the cooking oil is runny." (Paragraph 5) E. (Paragraph 6) F. "Despite the great damage they can cause, volcanoes also help us to live. Volcanic ash provides food for the soil around volcanoes which helps us grow plants to eat." (Paragraph 13)

articles.title: The Other Side
articles.author: Angela Johnson
articles.description: A speaker shares a daydream from their childhood.
articles.intro: Angela Johnson is an award-winning American children's book and poetry author. In this poem, a speaker describes their daydream.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the speaker feels.
articles.permissions: "The Other Side" from The Other Side: Shorter Poems by Angela Johnson. Copyright © 2000. Used with author's permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: I used to stand on top of the shed in the back of my
grandmama’s house and see the other side.
The other side of where I was.
The other side had tall buildings and I could buy
hot dogs and pretzels on the street.
At night the hum of the subway and faraway sirens
would put me to sleep.
I dreamed of the other side.
I’d seen it on vacation and TV.
The other side didn’t have a creek or magnolia trees
and warm women who smelled like cookies hugging you
on hot, sticky Sunday afternoons.
If I stood for a long time,
the other side would fade and
where I stood would light my world.

options.label: Using our imagination becomes easier as we age.
Imagination is an essential part of a happy childhood.
People with active imaginations often struggle to enjoy life.
Thinking of other places is exciting, but home is a special part of us.

questions.prompt: What is the theme of the poem?

options.label: "The other side had tall buildings and I could buy / hot dogs and pretzels on the street" (Lines 4-5)
"The other side didn't have a creek or magnolia trees" (Line 10)
"warm women who smelled like cookies hugging you / on hot, sticky Sunday afternoons." (Lines 11-12)
"If I stood for a long time, / the other side would fade" (Lines 13-14)

articles.title: From Bite to Bark: The Evolution of Dogs
articles.author: T.J. Resler
articles.description: T.J. Resler describes how wolves evolved into modern dogs.
articles.intro: T.J. Resler is an American author with a passion for telling stories. A former journalist, professor and research analyst, she researches and writes a wide variety of nonfiction and fiction for kids and adults. In this article, she describes wild wolves' evolution — the process of changing and adapting — into modern dogs.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the author’s opinion on the evolution of wolves into dogs.
articles.permissions: "From Bite to Bark: The Evolution of Dogs" by T.J. Resler. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: A little goldendoodle puppy may look more like a teddy bear than a wolf. But behind those cute curls is a bit of wild animal.
From fluffy goldendoodles to sleek greyhounds to giant mastiffs — all dogs come from ancient wolves. How those wild wolves turned into dogs is one of the coolest stories of animal evolution.
Taming Wolves

Dogs were the first domesticated animal. This means that they were the first animal that became tame enough to live with humans. People take credit for domesticating dogs, but scientists have figured out that it didn’t happen that way. The wolves tamed themselves.
“Wolves chose us,” Brian Hare told the news program 60 Minutes . He is an expert on animal evolution. “A population of wolves actually became attracted to humans.”
It wasn’t our fun personalities that won them over. It was our leftovers!

More than 20,000 years ago, humans hunted animals to survive. After feasting and using the most useful parts, they threw away the rest. Some wolves weren’t too afraid of people. These clever wolves realized that the leftovers would make easy meals for them. They started hanging around people to get those tasty treats. Over thousands of years, wolves slowly bonded with people and changed into dogs.
Humans’ Best Friend

The tamer these wolves became, the more they began to look and act dog-like. They no longer needed their large size and powerful jaws to hunt. So they evolved to be a little smaller. This was better for surviving on scraps.
It wasn’t only their bodies that became smaller. Their snouts, paws, and teeth also shrunk. Some of their pointy ears flopped down, and their fur became different colors.

All living things pass along many different traits. Traits refer to how a living thing looks, how they behave, and how they are built. Living things pass down traits to their children through microscopic sets of instructions called genes . As wolves evolved into dogs, their genes changed. These changes affected the wolves’ looks and behaviors.

Scientists have found interesting differences between wolf and dog genes. Scientist Bridgett vonHoldt is an expert on the study of genes. She told 60 Minutes that there are genes in dogs that make them friendlier than wolves.

Dogs became good at communicating with humans. They learned how to read our expressions and body language. They even learned some of our spoken words and signals. They also figured out how to attract our attention — by being cute. They learned to rest their chins on our laps or tap us with a paw. They even learned to raise their inner eyebrows in that “puppy eyes” way that people love. According to The New York Times , dogs have a special muscle that helps them raise their eyebrows. Wolves do not.

As the bond between people and dogs grew stronger, dogs made people’s lives easier in many important ways. They helped people hunt. They learned how to herd sheep. They pulled sleds piled with our stuff. They protected us and kept us company. As the saying goes, dogs became our best friends.
But the amazing story of how wolves evolved into dogs doesn’t explain how we ended up with pups as different as Chihuahuas and St. Bernards. That’s where people came in.
The Best Dog for the Job

Over time, people created dog breeds. They did it through a practice called selective breeding . People picked the “best dogs” — ones with traits the people really liked and wanted to see in other dogs. Then, they made sure the dogs had puppies. The desired traits were passed on to some of the puppies, so people repeated the practice. As a trait became stronger in each generation of dog, the pups with the trait became more different. Eventually, these special traits became a unique breed.

Of course, not everyone agreed on the “best dogs” or the most desirable traits. Some people wanted dogs with an excellent sense of smell or the ability to run fast. Both traits were helpful in hunting. Others bred nimble dogs that could herd sheep. They also wanted big, patient dogs to guard the livestock. And other people needed strong dogs with a lot of energy to pull sleds over snow. All of these dogs became different breeds.
Even More Dog Breeds

For many years, people bred dogs for work or protection. But by the mid-1800s, people realized that they loved having dogs simply as pets, too. They began to breed little dogs that could sit on their laps. They bred dogs with interesting looks that they could show off in a new competition: dog shows.

Today, we have more than 400 different dog breeds according to National Geographic ’s Dog Breed Guide. And that’s not even counting the “designer dog” crossbreeds. This includes breeds like cockapoos (cocker spaniel-poodle), puggles (pug-beagle), goldendoodles (golden retriever-poodle), and chiweenies (Chihuahua-dachshund). Or the many mixed-breeds called “mutts.”

Dogs now come in many different shapes, colors, and sizes. They also have many different types of behaviors. This makes dogs the most diverse land mammal. The largest dogs are a whopping 40 times bigger than the smallest dogs! That’s quite a change from the lone wolf.

options.label: Wolves and dogs share similar traits because they were bred by scientists to be alike.
Wolves and dogs both have positive traits that are helpful to the everyday lives of humans.
Modern dogs evolved from wolves through domestication, changing genes, and selective breeding.
Modern dogs evolved from wolves through training, changing genes, and breeding wolves with dogs.

discussionQuestions.prompt: On their way to becoming domesticated, wolves changed their appearance and behaviors to better fit in with humans. Can you think of any other scenarios where animals or people change parts of themselves to better fit in? Is it always positive to change part of yourself to fit in? Use examples from your own personal experiences, movies, or literature to support your thinking.

The author shares that over the years, evolutionary scientists have learned more about how wolves evolved into dogs. How do you think it feels to be a scientist making new discoveries about the past? Would you like to be an evolutionary scientist? Why or why not?

The text discusses how dogs have been bred to offer humans protection, companionship, and help. What are the advantages and disadvantages of domesticating animals in this way? Do you feel that this process of domestication is fair to animals who don't necessarily have a choice in the matter? Why or why not?
Today there are over 400 breeds of dogs. Which breed is your favorite? Why? What characteristics does it share with the first domesticated dogs described in the text?

articles.title: Crab Dancing at the Monmouth Diner
articles.author: Julia Specht
articles.description: A young girl learns an important lesson about being true to yourself while working at her family's diner.
articles.intro: Julia Specht is a writer based in Brooklyn, New York. In this short story, a young girl works at her family's diner. Diners are local restaurants that serve inexpensive, homestyle food.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the problem and solution in the story.
articles.permissions: "Crab Dancing at the Monmouth Diner" by Julia Specht. Copyright © 2024 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: “Order up!”

The service bell rang out through The Monmouth Diner. Dad sent a plate of french fries sliding smoothly down the bar into Izzy’s waiting hands. She caught it — he winked — and she felt a thrill run up her spine. After years of pleading, today was the first day Dad was trusting her to serve actual customers.

Izzy had been helping out at the restaurant ever since she could walk. She’d done everything — cleaned out the walk-in refrigerator, wiped down the menus, even scraped little bits of gum off the bottoms of the tables. “Good task for a short-stack like you,” her dad would say while ruffling her hair. “You’re small enough to get in all the corners.”

She never complained, not even about the gross stuff, because she loved her dad. Izzy’s dad taught her how every little thing that happens in the diner is important. She understood that it all fits together, and even though scraping gum isn’t the most fun thing in the world to do, she was proud of the diner and wanted it to be nice.

But talking to customers was always a job for the adults. You have to know how to charm them, how to smile in the right way, how to talk just enough, how to remember all the orders and substitutions that they might throw at you. Dad always said it took time to learn how to do that. Izzy had been working, rehearsing her memorization skills and her polite grin, and wanted to prove she was mature enough to take on the customers. Now that she was 14, she felt ready.

“That’s going to Table 6,” Dad called to her before turning back to the flat top. Izzy nodded as she balanced the plate of fries on her forearms. She rounded the corner of the service station, prepared her very best smile, and rehearsed what she was about to say. Here you go. Is there anything else I can get for you? She repeated the lines in her head over and over as she scanned the dining room.
And then her blood ran cold.

Kassy Goericke was at Table 6, with her shiny black hair and her name-brand jeans. Kassy, who smelled like Marc Jacobs Daisy. Kassy, who literally went viral with one of her dance videos. Beside her sat her three partners in popularity — Ava, Scarlett, and Hannah — all three just as glossy and shiny.
Before she could stop herself, Izzy ducked behind the register.

Izzy had been attending Monmouth Academy since 6th grade. Monmouth was a top-notch boarding school, nestled in the hills of Western Massachusetts. The campus buildings were Gothic, featuring Roman bricks with tall spires and ivy crawling up the walls. It was the type of place where students spent their summers scuba diving in Hawaii, while winters were reserved for skiing in Aspen. Parents were doctors, lawyers, or surgeons, folk who generously donated to the school on top of the hefty tuition fees. Izzy enrolled as a day student on a scholarship — one of the handful of “scholarship kids” whose parents’ combined annual salaries wouldn’t even cover the cost of a semester at Monmouth.

Students at the Academy came from all over the country, but Izzy was the only one from Monmouth itself — a “townie.” She tried her best to hide it. During her first week of school, she told people she was from Lexington, a rich suburb outside of Boston. She might have gotten away with it, too, had Kassy Goericke not actually been from Lexington. Kassy had been quick to tell everyone in their grade that Izzy was lying.

Since then, Kassy never missed an opportunity to call Izzy out. Usually it was over little things, like the brand of Izzy’s jeans or the smell of her body spray, and Izzy could handle it. But then there was the lift ticket disaster.

All year long, the girls wore the ski lift tickets from their Aspen skiing vacations, dangling them off their coat zippers. Izzy, who had never gone skiing in her entire life, stole the tickets from a jacket that had been sitting in the lost-and-found for three weeks. Kassy confronted her as they settled in for homeroom. “You didn’t have those lift tickets on your jacket yesterday, did you?”
Izzy felt everyone’s eyes on her and her face flared red. “Uh… ”
“Because I’m pretty sure you didn’t,” said Kassy. “So did you, like, fly to Colorado last night?”

A chorus of snickers rippled up from the crowd, and Izzy’s stomach twisted. She had fumbled for an answer, finally mumbling something about having to remove the tickets when getting the jacket cleaned. Kassy had rolled her eyes and scoffed. “This whole lying thing is so weird,” she said. “We already know you’re a scholarship kid or whatever. You’re not fooling anyone.”

After that, Izzy stopped trying to fit in and tried to disappear instead. But even though Kassy found ways to make fun of her because her family couldn’t afford tuition, and she didn’t live in the dorms, Izzy had been successful at hiding the restaurant. She was so proud of her family and their diner, and she didn’t want those wealthy kids to know she had to work. She didn’t want anyone to mock her family’s place.

Izzy tried to catch her breath as she crouched behind the register. She couldn’t stay there much longer. She scanned the room, trying to find an exit — maybe to the kitchen? Maybe she could hide in the walk-in fridge until the girls left?
“What are you doing down there?”
Izzy whipped around. Dad was looking down at her from the service window. “Is something wrong?” he asked.
“Ha, no!” Her fake laughter sounded more like a strange wheeze. “I just… dropped my pen.”
Dad raised an eyebrow. “Okay,” he said. “That order’s gonna get cold. Table 6, remember?”
“Yep,” said Izzy. “Got it!”

Dad turned and walked back to the kitchen. Izzy’s heart was pounding. She couldn’t go to Table 6 herself, but maybe… maybe Georgina, who had been working at the restaurant for years, would do it? She was the only other waitress on duty.
“Order up!” Dad yelled.
Izzy chucked the plate of fries onto the shelf inside the counter. “I got it!” she said.
Dad slid the order (reuben sandwich, extra pickle, hold the coleslaw) down the bar. “Table 2,” he said.

Of course it’s Table 2 , Izzy thought. Table 2 was directly in front of Table 6. This is okay , she thought. I can do this . She balanced the plate on her tray, turned to her left, and began crab-walking, moving sideways as she made her way across the dining room. A woman at another table looked up from her menu, and her jaw dropped; she poked her husband and pointed. But that was fine, that was totally fine! As long as Kassy didn’t see her, it didn’t matter.
Izzy put the plate down in front of the man at Table 2. “Here you go!” she said. “Is there anything else I can get for you?”
“Um.” The man stared at her as though she had four heads. “No, I think I’m… fine? Are you okay?”

“Great!” Izzy replied, hitting him with her very best competent waitress smile. “Let me know if there’s anything else you need.” She spun on her heels and began to crab walk back to the kitchen. But then she stopped cold.
Georgina was standing by the register, holding the plate of french fries Izzy had hidden on the shelf. She looked utterly confused and glanced up at Izzy. “What’s this?” she mouthed.
Izzy’s stomach dropped. She shrugged. “Dunno!” she mouthed back.

Georgina knitted her eyebrows together. She put the plate on the bar, then waved at Dad, who turned away from the grill. Come on, don’t tell him , Izzy thought. She watched the two of them in conversation. Dad looked up at Izzy, and she saw a ripple of confusion pass across his face, followed by a cloud of anger. He gestured for her to come over.
Izzy trudged across the dining room, embarrassment flooding her body. Dad pointed at the plate. “What’s this?” he said. “Is this the order for Table 6?”
Izzy opened her mouth to make an excuse, but she knew there was no point in lying.
“Yes,” she whispered.
Dad sighed. “Why didn’t you bring it over?”
Izzy couldn’t think of a good reason. She sputtered, trying to explain, but no words came out. Dad pinched the bridge of his nose.
“You know,” he said, “maybe you need a little more time to get good at this.”
“But Dad!” said Izzy.
“Just go take a break,” sighed Dad. “We’ll talk once the lunch rush is over.”

Izzy ran to the walk-in and ducked inside. She looked down at the apron she’d tied so carefully, placed her fingers on the neat bun in her hair that she’d spent so much time styling that morning. Guess I’ll be scraping gum off of tables forever , she thought. There was no way Dad would ever let her speak to a customer again. He had trusted her, and she’d messed it up. And why? Because of Kassy Goericke?
There was a knock at the door. “Izzy?” a voice said. Was that… Hannah? No… please . Izzy wiped her face with the corner of her apron and took a deep breath.
“Hey Iz?” This voice was Dad’s. “Your friend wanted to say hi.”
She’s not my friend , Izzy thought, but she kept herself from saying it. “Okay!” she said. “I’ll be right out.”
What was he thinking? Why would he bring someone into the kitchen? He’s so clueless , Izzy thought, he literally doesn’t get anything . She tried to squish her fury down into her stomach.
Chin up. Back straight. Don’t let them make you feel small.
Izzy cracked open the door. “Hi,” she said and smiled.

“Hey,” said Hannah. She was looking down at the floor, scuffing the tile with the toe of her sneaker. The two of them said nothing. Pans clattered. Glasses clanked. Arturo, the busboy, scurried behind Hannah, almost knocking her over.
“You two, scoot!” he yelled. “You’re clogging everything up!”
“We should go outside,” Izzy said.

She led Hannah through to the back door, and the two girls walked out into the parking lot. It was a gray day, and the sky was spitting cold rain on their faces. Izzy could feel her hair getting damp, and Hannah pulled her pink hoodie tight around her body. They stood in silence. Then, Hannah finally broke it.
“Nice moves,” she said.
“What are you talking about?” said Izzy.
“You know,” said Hannah. She turned to the side and started shuffling, the same way Izzy had shuffled across the dining room earlier. “Your little crab dance.”
Izzy cringed. “Oh, great,” she said. “Are you gonna make fun of me now?”
Hannah laughed a little. “Well, you have been acting a little strange,” she said.
Izzy felt tears prick at the corners of her eyes again. “I didn’t want you to see me.”
“I get it,” Hannah sighed. She looked down at the ground. “Kassy can be a little mean.”
“A little?” Izzy scoffed.
“She’s just… she’s insecure sometimes, and she takes it out on other people.” Hannah shrugged. “You know, I think it’s really cool that you work at a diner.”
Izzy looked at Hannah’s face, trying to spot a sign that she was making fun of her. She couldn’t find one.
“You do?” Izzy said.
“Well, yeah,” said Hannah. “It’s like… people trust you to do stuff. And you know what you’re doing. No one else in our grade does anything like that.”
Izzy blushed. “Well, I know it really well,” she said. “It’s my dad’s place, so I grew up here.”

Hannah’s eyes widened. “Wait. This place belongs to your dad?” she said. “My parents and I come here every time they visit. They love it.” She placed her hand on Izzy’s elbow. “You should feel really proud,” she said. “It’s, like, so awesome that this place is yours.”
Izzy beamed. She never expected that one of these girls would appreciate the restaurant the way she did.
“You know what I think you should do?” said Hannah. “I think you should add some pincers to the crab dance. Like this.” She held up her hands like crab claws and snapped them in the air.
Izzy burst out laughing. “I like it!” Izzy said. “Maybe get a little lower, like this?” She squatted and shuffled back and forth. Hannah copied her, and the two girls dissolved into giggles.

As she walked back out to the floor, Izzy felt her excitement from the morning returning. The hustle and bustle of the restaurant made her feel warm — the ding of the order bell, the scrape of Dad’s spatula against the flat top, the clink of glasses as they were loaded into the dishwasher — all of it filled her with a sense of pride. In comparison to this place, Kassy’s opinion didn’t matter.
Izzy leaned on the bar. “Hey,” she called out to Dad. “I’m ready.”
Dad looked up from the flat top. “Did you not want to bring the order to Table 6 because of those girls?” he whispered.
Izzy nodded. “But I’m fine now. I can handle it.”
“You sure?” he said.
“Yeah,” she grinned. “I am.”
Dad nodded. “Alright,” he said. “They’re ready for their check.”
“Got it!” said Izzy. She strode confidently to the register and punched in the price for the fries from memory. She hit print and watched the receipt curl its way out of the machine.
“You know,” Dad said. “I believe in you.” Izzy looked up and saw that Dad was watching her, eyes brimming with pride.
Izzy beamed. “Thanks, Dad.”
“Just don’t put fries under the register again,” he said with a wink.
Izzy tore the receipt and made her way across the dining room, toward Kassy and her friends. As she collected their cash, Kassy snickered. “She finally decides to acknowledge us,” Kassy said.
Izzy looked at Hannah, and they grinned at each other. Hannah held up her hands like pincers, and so did Izzy.
“You all have a good day,” Izzy said. She snapped her pincers, and Hannah did too.
“Um, weird?” said Kassy. “What are you doing?”

“Duh,” said Hannah, “it’s the crab dance!” She and Izzy broke into giggles, and Izzy started to crab dance back to the register. As she crossed the dining room, she realized she didn’t feel embarrassed. Maybe Kassy couldn’t appreciate The Monmouth Diner, but other people could. And to Izzy, it was the most special place in the world.

options.label: Be true to yourself, even when it is difficult.
Be nice to others, even if they are unkind to you.
Tell the truth at all times, even if it is embarrassing.
Always do your job well, even if others will not notice.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses the theme of the passage?

options.label: Kassy makes Izzy concerned about her grades.
Kassy makes Izzy too nervous to do her job properly.
Kassy makes Izzy realize that her family's diner is special.
Kassy makes Izzy distrustful of kids from Monmouth Academy.

questions.prompt: How does Kassy affect Izzy's actions in the story?

Izzy is proud of the diner after Hannah shares how impressed she is that Izzy's family owns it.
Izzy wishes she does not have to work at the diner after Hannah tells Izzy she is a bad waitress.
Izzy is excited to invite other students from her school to the diner after Hannah suggests it is a great place to hang out.

questions.prompt: How does Hannah influence Izzy's point of view about the diner?

options.label: She is angry at her dad for questioning her.
She spits out food while she talks to her dad.
She struggles to tell her dad about her problems.
She is excited to tell her dad how well she is doing.

How do paragraphs 53-70 add to the overall meaning of the story?

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Izzy shares about how the Monmouth Diner is a special place for her and her family. What special places do you have in your life? What makes this place special to you or your family? How do you feel when you spend time in this place?

In the story, Izzy's dad trusts her to work as a waitress at her family's diner. Have you ever been entrusted with an important responsibility? What was it, and how did it feel to be trusted in this way?
In the text, Hannah gives Izzy advice on how to approach Kassy. Do you agree with Hannah's advice? What advice would you give someone to help them deal with a difficult social situation?
In the story, Izzy ends up befriending Hannah. Which of the three characters — Izzy, Hannah or Kassy — would you choose to be friends with? Why? What character traits do you look for in a friend?

articles.title: The Donkey, the Fox, and the Lion
articles.author: Aesop
articles.description: A fox chooses to betray his friend, the donkey, to save himself from the lion.
articles.intro: Aesop was a storyteller who lived in ancient Greece between 620 and 560 BCE. This story is part of his collection of tales known as Aesop's Fables , which did not survive in writing but were passed down by people retelling them. They have deeply influenced children's literature and modern storytelling culture.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify the different promises made in this fable.
articles.permissions: "The Donkey, the Fox, and the Lion" by Aesop is in the public domain.

blocks.text: The Donkey and the Fox, having entered into partnership together for their mutual protection, went out into the forest to hunt. They had not proceeded far when they met a Lion. The Fox, seeing imminent danger, approached the Lion and promised to contrive for him the capture of the Donkey if the Lion would pledge his word not to harm "brown fox on snowfield" by Ray Hennessey is the Fox. licensed under CC0. Then, upon assuring the Donkey that he would not be injured, the Fox led him to a deep pit and arranged that he should fall into it.
The Lion, seeing that the Donkey was secured, immediately clutched the Fox, and attacked the Donkey at his leisure.

options.label: Betraying a friend can have terrible consequences.
The desire to survive is strong, but it is not as strong as friendship.
Being clever can be more useful than being strong.
Friendships made between cowards never last.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following best describes a major theme of the text?

options.label: "The Donkey and the Fox, having entered into partnership together for their mutual protection, went out into the forest to hunt." (Paragraph 1)
"The Fox, seeing imminent danger, approached the Lion and promised to contrive for him the capture of the Donkey" (Paragraph 3)
"Then, upon assuring the Donkey that he would not be injured, the Fox led him to a deep pit" (Paragraph 4)
"The Lion, seeing that the Donkey was secured, immediately clutched the Fox, and attacked the Donkey at his leisure." (Paragraph 5)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which quote from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: The fox becomes frightened, and he tries to save them both from the lion.
The fox tries to make friends with the lion in spite of the donkey's warning about the lion.
The fox tries to trick the lion into falling into a pit so that he and the donkey can escape.
The fox is quick to betray the donkey by offering to trick him for the lion.

questions.prompt: How does the fox react to the arrival of the lion?

options.label: The fox's betrayal at the end of the story contributes to the idea that people cannot stop themselves from being what they are, like the cowardly fox.
The fox's betrayal at the end of the story contributes to the moral: do not trust other people to help you and instead help yourself, as the donkey should have done.
The deaths of the fox and the donkey contribute to the moral of the story: it is better to be united against an enemy rather than divided by our differences.
The lion's betrayal of the fox contributes to the idea that betraying a friend can have terrible consequences, just as the fox's betrayal led to his own death.

questions.prompt: How does the ending of the story contribute to its overall meaning?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you think the fox was smart to try to save himself? Why or why not? What else could the fox have done to save the donkey and himself from the lion?
In the context of this tale, what drives someone to betray? Use evidence from this text, your own experience, and other art, literature, or history in your answer.

articles.title: Aurelia Dobre: World Champion Gymnast
articles.author: Chris Woods
articles.description: A speaker describes Aurelia Dobre's gymnastics routine.
articles.intro: Aurelia is a former gymnast from Romania and the 1987 world all-around champion. In this poem, a speaker describes Dobre participating in several gymnastics events.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the author uses figurative language to describe Dobre’s gymnastics routine.
articles.permissions: "Aurelia Dobre: World Champion Gymnast" from Recovery by Chris Woods. Copyright © 1993. Used by permission of Enitharmon Press. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Spins clockwork,
then oscillates
between the bars.
Time waits
as she unwinds.
Walks the plank.
Defies the natural order.
Points toes
to test the water.
Walks on that.
Skims and ducks
and drakes
across the mat.
Creates
ripples of applause.
Computes her vault
on magnetic
tape.
The kick
is muscle memory.
Traces her geometry;
spirals, circles.
No straight edge,
all curves, curls,
and smiles.

options.label: People can do amazing things with hard work and practice.
The human body is limitless, if you take good care of it.
A person must love what they do in order to succeed at it.
Nothing is ever as easy as it appears.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following identifies the theme of the poem?

options.label: "Time waits / as she unwinds. / Walks the plank." (Lines 4-6)
"Walks on that. / Skims and ducks / and drakes" (Lines 10-12)
"The kick / is muscle memory. / Traces her geometry;" (Lines 19-21)
"No straight edge, / all curves, curls, / and smiles." (Lines 23-25)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the poem best supports the answer to Part A?

options.label: It emphasizes how easy gymnastics is for Dobre.
It depicts Dobre's impressive gymnastics abilities.
It stresses how unsure Dobre is of her abilities.
It highlights the excitement of the audience as they watch Dobre.

options.label: "Walks the plank. / Defies the natural order." (Lines 6-7)
"Points toes / to test the water." (Lines 8-9)
"Computes her vault / on magnetic / tape." (Lines 16-18)
"No straight edge, / all curves, curls," (Lines 23-25)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which quote from the text best supports the answer to Part A?
How does the figurative language used in lines 7-12 contribute to the poem?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Why is the speaker impressed by the gymnast? What about her routine is special?

In the context of the poem, why do people succeed? How do you think Aurelia Dobre became a world champion gymnast? Cite evidence from this text, your own experience, and other literature, art, or history in your answer.

articles.title: Training the GOAT
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke describes the important job of Tom Brady's athletic trainer.
articles.intro: Tom Brady is a famous American football quarterback for the National Football League (NFL). In this text, Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke describes the job of Alex Guerrero, Tom Brady's athletic trainer.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on people’s opinions about Alex Guerrero.
articles.permissions: "Training the Goat" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Mention Tom Brady's name and people's reactions range from cheering to choking. Patriots and Buccaneers fans adore him. Competitors abhor him. Despite wide-ranging opinions, it is difficult to deny that Brady is the GOAT, the greatest quarterback of all time. Seven Super Bowl championships with two teams. Five-time Super Bowl MVP. Over 600 career passing touchdowns. The oldest player in the NFL.
To whom does Brady give credit for his success and long career as a quarterback? His athletic trainer, Alex Guerrero.

From high school sports practices, college games, to pro matches, trainers maintain athletes' strength and treat their injuries. On the field, they quickly assess and attend to injuries, staying calm in stressful situations. Off the field, athletic trainers help athletes recover from injuries and achieve maximum physical performance. They encourage athletes and stay optimistic when coaching athletes towards recovery.
Brady's long-time trainer, Guerrero, has unconventional training methods — he's definitely not the typical trainer. For his approaches to training, Guerrero is both praised and critiqued.
Early Influences

Guerrero grew up in California, the fourth of six children raised by immigrants from Argentina. His family spoke Spanish and attended the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. This belief system continues to influence his values today.

After watching his father-in-law suffer from skin cancer which caused sores on his arms, neck, and shoulders, Guerrero decided to study traditional Chinese medicine. Traditional Chinese medicine focuses not only on treating diseases but also on preventing them. Traditional Chinese medicine prioritizes balance in the body and holistic approaches to health — medicine alongside alternative approaches like massage, acupuncture, and mediation. His studies of Chinese medicine have guided his training methods.

In 1996, Guerrero opened a rehabilitation center and worked with track athletes in Los Angeles. When Guerrero noticed runner after runner suffering from the same hamstring injury, he decided to observe their training. He wanted to understand why they were getting injured. He noticed that runners built bulky muscles during training and then tried to be light and nimble during races. Influenced by his training of runners, Guerrero supports athletes to build soft, flexible muscles. He calls this pliability.
An Out-Of-The-Box Approach

Over the course of his career, Guerrero developed a training program that combines nutrition, hydration, mental strength, exercise, and massage. While most athletic trainers spend their time treating athletes, Guerrero prioritizes injury prevention. He aims for athletes that are mentally strong and injury resistant.

Most athletic trainers work alongside doctors to help athletes bounce back after an injury. With doctors, they develop and carry out injury recovery plans. In contrast, Guerrero mostly trains solo, according to his belief about how bodies recover. He believes in using the brain to relieve injuries. For example, rather than elevating a broken foot, Guerrero encourages “re-educating” injured parts to work properly using massage, meditation, and exercising them far sooner than doctor-recommended.
Credited and Critiqued

But, like Brady, Guerrero has plenty of critics. Some medical professionals say that his focus on pliable muscles is not scientifically-based and has no proven results. They critique Guerrero for pitting western medical treatment against more holistic practices. Others say he overpromises what his training methods and products can achieve.

In addition, critics call into question Guerrero’s schooling. Athletes’ trainers typically hold a bachelor's degree and study human anatomy, physiology, and nutrition. According to the National Athletic Trainers Association, more than half of athletic trainers earn masters or doctoral degrees. Some critics claim that Guerrero is not appropriately qualified as a trainer. SAMRA University, where he studied, is a school of Chinese traditional medicine, with little priority on the hard sciences, western medicine, and modern technology.

But with Brady's career as proof, many regard Guerrero as a top trainer. Brady has worked with Guerrero, who he calls his “body coach,” since 2006. Brady was sold on his approach to training after a 2008 ACL injury where Guerrero helped him recover and get back on the football field.

Describing Brady and Guerrero's relationship, a New York Times Magazine author wrote, “While Guerrero is known as Brady's 'body coach,' that label significantly understates his exhaustive reach into Brady's life... He accompanies Brady to almost every Patriots game, home and away, and stands on the sidelines. He works with Brady's personal chef to put together optimally healthful menus; he plans Brady's training schedule months in advance. Above all, during the football season, he works on Brady seven days a week, usually twice a day.”

Brady and Guerrero opened a training center called TB12, focused on holistic approaches to health and wellness for athletes. The goal of TB12 is to focus on “pre-hab” or proactively building physical and mental health to prevent injuries and perform at peak levels. Both professional athletes and everyday people seek the TB12 method to recover from injuries or increase their athletic performance.

options.label: Alex Guerrero: Unconventional Training for Great Results
Tom Brady: The Man, The Myth, The Legend
Traditional Chinese Healing Practices
How to Heal Your Injuries Like a Pro

articles.title: Climate change has finally caught up to this Alaska village
articles.author: Craig Welch
articles.description: Craig Welch discusses the effects of climate change on a small Alaskan village.
articles.intro: Craig Welch is a journalist for National Geographic who focuses on climate change and oceans. In this article, he reports on the experiences of a small Alaskan village.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, identify the challenges Newtok’s residents are experiencing.
articles.permissions: Craig Welch, NG Image Collection

blocks.text: “Many folks are not happy to be leaving the place they’ve known their whole lives.” After 20 years of melting permafrost, residents of Newtok now must move.
They’ve boxed their clothes and piled into boats and said goodbye to neighbors — for now.

This month, a group of residents from the Alaska village of Newtok finally began settling into a brand new town. In the process they have become some of North America's earliest climate change transplants.

The Yup'ik village of about 380 people on the Ninglick River near the Bering Sea has spent more than two decades preparing to move. Thawing permafrost and erosion has increased flooding risks and caused the land around their homes to crumble and sink. The community landfill has washed away, fuel storage tanks are damaged, and some houses have already been torn down because they were in danger of collapsing.

So, after years of planning and construction, families two weeks ago began arriving in the new village of Mertarvik, about 10 miles southeast on Nelson Island. During breaks in the high winds and heavy rains that lashed the area last week, 18 families began unpacking belongings in their newly built energy-efficient homes.
“We're moving, literally, in the calm between storms,” says Andrew John, Newtok Village tribal administrator.

More families are expected to make the move. However, it will probably take until at least 2023 before enough homes are built for everyone. Until then the Mertarvik-Newtok village will operate from two locations separated by water.
“It will be challenging, but this is a very strong community,” says Gavin Dixon, a manager of a community organization. He is assisting Newtok residents with the move.
A big thaw

For thousands of years, until the early 20th century, the Yup'ik people were hunters who moved between camps as they hunted seals, moose, and musk oxen and gathered berries and wild greens. While this community still maintains a hunter-gatherer lifestyle, residents were forced in 1949 to settle in Newtok after the government chose the site for the village school without first seeking residents' input.

Since then, climate change has warmed the planet and started thawing the frozen ground that sits below the surface across the far north. Melting permafrost is causing roads, pipelines, and building foundations to crack while also releasing more greenhouse gases. This drives global temperature increases even higher. Meanwhile, as sea ice melts and moves offshore, storm surges cause rivers to rise, destroying river banks and splashing into communities. Rising seas accelerate this erosion.

As a result, Newtok residents for years have watched enormous chunks of once-stable soil crumble into the Ninglick, bringing the waters ever closer to homes, sometimes as much as 83 feet per year. Floods frequently leave households isolated. One study in the early 2000s showed vast sections of the town could become part of the river as early as 2027.

But as with many other isolated Alaskan communities facing similar problems, securing an alternative home and finding money to move has been a long process. The government hasn’t been willing to invest in services in the meantime. So for decades residents have lived without plumbing, instead gathering drinking water in jugs. The lack of proper sanitation has led to health problems, especially among infants.
In 2003 Congress finally agreed to create the new village of Mertarvik on higher ground.

Since then, money slowly has come from state and federal agencies to build roads, a community center, a landfill and a power station. A water treatment plant will be finished in a few weeks and a new school will be up and running in November. A landing strip for airplanes is coming soon.

But only about a third of the 60 or so new homes that are needed have been built. They have electricity but don't yet have access to public water or sewer systems. The community first wanted to get as many homes built and occupied as possible. Getting money to install running water and sewer could take many more years.
So for now, residents get to begin transforming Mertarvik into a new community — all while maintaining their old one several miles away.
A difficult move

It won't be simple. For now, some tribal officials will live in Mertarvik while others will remain in Newtok. There will be school teachers in each village, as well as a principal on each site. Some educating will be done via video.
But with 40 students in one community and 60 in the other, “half of their friends will be 10 miles away,” Dixon says.

Emotions about all of this change are mixed. Newtok resident Martha Kasaiuli, 19, already has had her Newtok home demolished. Her family moved to Mertarvik last week, but she plans to remain on the other side of the water with friends for a few months. She expressed her thoughts in a poem, which reads in part:
The unwanted feelings about moving grow stronger.
However, staying here isn't fun though.
We will be moving to an unfamiliar place.
But this place will grow vacant as the years go by.
Dixon puts it this way: “Many folks are not happy to be leaving the place they've known their whole lives.”
John says some residents are relieved while others feel anxious, and a few already experience separation anxiety. Some are too busy gathering food for winter to give it much thought.
The move might leave some residents a bit farther from traditional hunting grounds, “but frankly that's a small price to pay for the safety and security they will now have,” John says.
“I think as a people, our greatest attribute has been our ability to adapt,” John says. “Our people have been flexible. We've found a way.”
Or, as Kasaiuli sees it, “Our story is unfolding to a better ending, even if we don't want to leave this place.”

options.label: Changes in the ocean are affecting several coastal communities in many different ways.

articles.title: The Anklet
articles.author: Neil Philip
articles.description: A young woman must deal with unkind and spiteful treatment from her two older sisters.
articles.intro: Neil Philip is a writer and poet who has retold the best-known stories from The Arabian Nights for a modern day audience. The Arabian Nights is the English-language nickname frequently given to One Thousand and One Arabian Nights , a collection of folk tales written and collected in the Middle East during the Islamic Golden Age of the 8th to 13th centuries. In this tale, a poor young woman must deal with mistreatment by members of her own family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the youngest sister's actions and feelings.
articles.permissions: "The Anklet" from The Arabian Nights retold by Neil Philip. Copyright © 1994 by Neil Philip, published by Orchard Books. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Sheherazade told stories night after night: all the voyages of Sinbad the Sailor, and the adventures of Land Abdullah and Sea Abdullah, the fate of the man who stole the dog’s golden dish, the story of the ruined man who became rich again through a dream. And one night she told the story of
The Anklet

It is said, O King, that there were once in a city three sisters, who lived together and earned their bread by spinning flax. The youngest was also the prettiest, radiant and graceful as the moon. Her elder sisters, who were born to a different mother, hated and envied her, both for her beauty and for her skill at spinning.
One day the youngest went to the market, and, finding herself with a small coin left over, bought a little clay pot to hold flowers.
“You silly girl,” shrieked the sisters. “We can’t waste our money on fancies and fripperies.”
She made no answer, but placed a single rose in the pot, and sat down to her spinning.

The days passed, in drudgery and silent toil. The two older sisters kept nagging the poor girl and making fun of her. Her only pleasure in life was to fill the little pot with flowers, which she could look at and smell as she worked.

Now one day the sisters were out, and the girl, alone with her thoughts, burst into tears. “Oh, little pot,” she said, “you are my only friend. My sisters have gone out and left me to work all by myself, without anything to eat.”
And the little pot heard her, and brought forth sweetmeats for her to eat. For there was a jinni in the pot, and whatever the mistress of the pot asked for, the jinni would provide.

The girl kept the secret of the pot from her sisters, but whenever they were out, she would ask for whatever she fancied. She would eat and drink her fill, and dress up in beautiful clothes; but when her sisters came home, she was always careful to be back in her rags and hard at work at her spinning wheel.
Now it happened that the king announced that he was going to hold a great feast for all the people of the city. Everyone was invited, even the three poor sisters.
The two older sisters dressed up in their threadbare best, and set out for the feast. “You can’t come,” they said. “You would shame us in those rags. You’re not fit for such fine company.”

But as soon as they were gone, the girl asked her pot for a beautiful green robe and scarves and garments of the finest silk. She asked, too, for sparkling rings and turquoise bracelets, and gold anklets studded with diamonds to wear around her slender ankles.

When she entered the king’s harem, where the women’s part of the entertainments was being held, everyone there gasped at her beauty, and at the richness of her magic clothes and jewels. Even her sisters were moved to tears at her grace and charm, and never guessed that this lovely princess was their own despised sister.

The girl slipped away before the end of the feast, so as to be home first. She took off the diamond anklets in order to be able to run faster, and in her haste she did not notice that she dropped one. It fell into the water trough where the king’s horses drank.
Next morning the horses refused to drink, shying away from the trough in terror, for the anklet shone and flared beneath the water, frightening them.
The groom took the jewel to the king’s son. He turned it around and around in his hands, saying nothing. Finally he declared, “The girl whom this anklet fits shall be my wife!”

He took the anklet to his mother. “Mother,” he said, “you know about such things. Please find the girl to whom this anklet belongs. I am in love with her already, and long to see the anklet gracing her slim, shapely ankle.”

So the queen organized a search of the whole city. She visited all the harems, and all the houses, but no one had an ankle slim enough to wear the anklet. Finally she went into the very poorest quarter, to the house of the three sisters. The two older sisters tried in vain to force the anklet on, but when the queen tried it on the youngest, it fitted perfectly. The search was over.

The queen led the girl back to the palace. The celebrations went on for forty days and forty nights, while the wedding was prepared. At last, the day arrived, and the bride was taken to the baths by her sisters, who were to dress her.

The older girls had worried away at the poor, trusting girl until they had wheedled the secret of the magic pot from her and they, too, had made requests of the jinni. They dressed her hair with the diamond pins the jinni provided, and as the last pin went into place, the girl was turned into a white dove, which flew out the window in panic. She had been transformed by the magic pins.
When the queen asked where the bride was, the sisters just said, “She has gone.”
The prince sent out search parties to scour the city for his bride, but she was nowhere to be found. Without her, he began to sicken and waste away.

Every day, at dawn and at dusk, the white dove came to the prince’s window, and cooed to him in his misery. The prince grew to love the bird, which seemed to be the only creature that could sooth his lonely heart. Once a day he reached out to touch her and, seeing she did not fly away, took her in his hand.

The dove cooed at the prince, and he began to smooth her feathers. Noticing something hard beneath them, he scratched the place, and out fell a diamond pin. He pulled out another, and another. And when the last pin was gone, the dove ruffled her feathers and shook herself back into her true shape, his bride once more.
The prince and his love lived many years in happiness, blessed by children as beautiful and kind as themselves. But the two sisters died of jealousy, poisoned by their own spite.

options.label: Family members don't always get along and work together.

articles.title: After Hours in Kindergarten
articles.author: Kim Roberts
articles.description: A speaker describes art projects in the halls of their school during geography week.
articles.intro: Kim Roberts is an award-winning poet and editor. In this poem, a speaker describes looking at a school's art projects during geography week.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the speaker’s reaction to the different art projects they view.
articles.permissions: Reprinted from The Scientific Method (WordTech Editions, 2017) by Kim Roberts (http://www.kimroberts.org), with permission of the author.

blocks.text: It’s geography week at school.
The kindergarten halls are lined
with identical pictures: Mrs. Benton’s
penguins, repeated blobs in black and white.
I move out of the polar regions.
What is that odd smell hiding beneath
disinfectant? On a low table I find
white styrofoam painted mud-brown,
notched rectangles that once enclosed
computer components, now glued
in a standing row, topped with toilet paper rolls,
also painted, topped with little paper
Chinese flags, yellow stars on red.
Why, it’s the Great Wall of China!
Styrofoam walls, cardboard watch towers
— I kneel to look closely —
one of the wonders of the world,
here between the girl’s bathroom
and the janitor’s closet,
fantastic in fluorescent light.

options.label: Something that looks ordinary at first can actually be beautiful or magical when you look more closely.
Children are more often excited by the simple pleasures of life than adults.
A child's imagination can allow them to explore any area of the world they wish to see.
Art has the ability to bring wonderful and strange places to life through the imagination of the artist.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which statement expresses the theme of the poem?

options.label: "with identical pictures: Mrs. Benton's / penguins, repeated blobs in black and white." (Lines 3-4)
"I move out of the polar regions. / What is that odd smell hiding beneath / disinfectant?" (Lines 5-7)
"Styrofoam walls, cardboard watch towers / — I kneel to look closely —" (Lines 15-16)
"one of the wonders of the world, / here between the girl's bathroom / and the janitor's closet" (Lines 17-19)

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the poem best supports the answer to Part A?

articles.title: A Day in the Life of An Astronaut
articles.author: Marie Droual
articles.description: Marie Droual describes how to become an astronaut and the important work that astronauts do.
articles.intro: Marie Droual is a former scientist who loves sharing the wonders of the natural world with everyone. She is based in New York City. In this informational text, Droual discusses what it is like to be an astronaut.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what life is like on the International Space Station.
articles.permissions: "A Day in the Life of An Astronaut" by Marie Droual. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: It’s a career many aspire to, but few make the elite crew of astronauts that explore space. What does it take to become an astronaut? Once you become one, what do you do?

The application process to even become an astronaut is very competitive. In 2017, over 18,000 people applied but only 11 people were selected for the elite program. What exactly are they looking for? There’s various criteria, such as having a scientific background and a master’s degree, but there’s also less measurable characteristics that they are looking for, like good communication, teamwork, leadership, and followership skills.

The selection process is only the start on the long journey to becoming an astronaut. The selected candidates then have to go through a rigorous program that will prepare them for the journey into space. They first take classes in multiple subjects ranging from geology to public speaking to survival. These courses prepare the future astronauts for everything they will encounter in their career, like identifying different rocks and minerals or making speeches to the public.

In addition to their schooling, astronauts also go through many different simulations of what they will be doing in space to allow them to practice the skills. For these simulations, there are life size models of parts of the International Space Station (ISS) and vehicles they will use. This allows them to become familiar with what they will see in space. Astronauts also practice their space walks underwater, because the buoyancy of the water mimics microgravity, or the lack of gravity. Another way they simulate microgravity is in a plane called KC-135, also known as the Vomit Comet. This plane will alternate flying between upward and downward arcs, providing astronauts with 20-30 second bursts of weightlessness. This motion can sometimes cause astronauts to get sick, hence the nickname.

After up to two years of training and preparation for their voyage, astronauts are ready to be launched into space. Where exactly do they go? Usually, they are sent up to the ISS, a spacecraft orbiting Earth. In this structure there are facilities for astronauts to live and do research about space. Countries from all over the world participate and collaborate on this orbiting vessel.

You might just be wondering, just how do astronauts live in the International Space Station? When gravity is taken out of the picture, it makes a lot of things that are quite simple on Earth much more complicated. Having your morning bowl of cereal wouldn’t work in space — the milk and cereal would float away! How about when you want to go to sleep? You can’t lie down in bed in space!

Many preparations are made for the food in space. Everything is prepared and packaged properly on Earth. A lot of the food is either ready to eat out of the package, or the astronauts just add water to it. With the help of nutritionists, astronauts have a balanced diet that gives them the right amount of nutrients and minerals. But don’t worry, they still have fun food, such as freeze-dried ice cream. To keep morale high, each country has contributed different foods important in their home country. The Japanese Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA) invented a rehydratable noodle, and the Italian Space Agency created an espresso machine that works in low gravity.

To actually eat the food, astronauts have to strap themselves into chairs, and eat off of tables with magnetized utensils that stick to the trays and tables. The food is prepared in packages that are designed to prevent the food from flying away. They have salt and pepper, but in liquid form, so it doesn’t fly into the vents. Because even humans' sense of taste is affected by microgravity, astronauts like spicy and stronger flavored food, like hot peppers or wasabi.

Once fueled up with food, the astronauts have a lot of tasks to fill their day. Like a large building requires a lot of maintenance the International Space Station requires constant attention and care to keep in functioning shape. It’s quite a large size, almost the size of an American football field, so you can imagine all the repairs and maintenance it requires.

Besides the maintenance of the station, the primary focus of the ISS is to conduct experiments in space. In space, everything appears to be weightless. In this microgravity environment, there is a lot we can learn about potentially living in space permanently, like how small organisms or different plants grow. The astronauts themselves will participate in medical experiments so that scientists can learn more about how the human body reacts to living in space. This will help build the body of knowledge that might allow humans to permanently live in space.

After a long day of working on the space station, it’s time to get into bed and lie….down? Up? Sideways? Because of the microgravity, it doesn’t really matter what direction or which way the astronauts lay. Each astronaut is assigned a small sleep station the size of a telephone booth, in which they have a sleeping bag that is strapped to the wall. While it may look uncomfortable to have to sleep upright, it makes no difference when there’s no gravity.
To become an astronaut, you not only need to have the specific skills and background to get in, but also the physical and mental strength to continue once you’ve gotten into the space program.

options.label: Becoming an astronaut is very difficult.
Anyone can become an astronaut if they work hard.
Traveling to the International Space Station makes some people feel sick.
Some scientists are unprepared for life on the International Space Station.

articles.title: Waste Not, Want Not
articles.subtitle: Turning waste into energy
articles.author: Lisa Christensen
articles.description: A tomato farm partners with a power plant to make their products more environmentally friendly.
articles.intro: In this article, a tomato farm forms an unlikely partnership with a power plant.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the author promotes reducing and reusing waste in order to protect the environment.
articles.permissions: "Waste Not, Want Not" by Lisa Christensen, Muse. © by Cricket Media, Inc.. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: People throw out a lot of garbage, but there are some places where waste is worth its weight in gold.

Houweling’s Tomatoes is a business that grows and sells greenhouse vegetables. Company chairman Casey Houweling runs the business. He has always been interested in making sure the company’s greenhouses really are “green” — meaning friendly to the environment. A green business may use fewer resources than similar businesses or make a very low level of carbon dioxide (CO2) and other air pollutants. These pollutants are sometimes called greenhouse gases because they trap warm air in Earth’s atmosphere like a roof traps warm air in a greenhouse. In recent years, Houweling’s took the “green” concept a step further, says David Bell, a spokesperson for the company.
Good Neighbors

The company decided to search for a new place to build a greenhouse that would let them capture and reuse industrial heat and CO2 waste to grow tomatoes year-round, he says. Mona, a small town in the central region of Utah, was exactly what the company was looking for. After three years of planning, Houweling’s built a greenhouse next door to a power plant.

The power plant creates electricity by burning natural gas. Besides electricity, the process produces heat and carbon dioxide. Before Houweling’s came to town, these were considered waste products. Houweling’s worked with the owner of the power plant, a company called Rocky Mountain Power, to funnel the extra heat and carbon dioxide into the greenhouse. The heat keeps the greenhouse toasty warm during cold Utah winters, and the tomatoes grow faster because the carbon dioxide helps fertilize them.

Rocky Mountain Power provides electricity for customers in Utah, Wyoming, and Idaho. The plant in Mona is what’s called a combined-cycle power plant . That means that it initially burns natural gas to turn two big turbines, which look like the fan in a jet engine. They’re connected to a generator. When the turbines turn, they create electricity through the generator. The exhaust from that process is sent to what’s known as a heat recovery steam generator , where the extra heat boils water to create steam. The steam turns another turbine, which produces electricity through the generator too.

Combined-cycle power plants can create up to 50 percent more electricity than simple-cycle power plants. In simple-cycle power plants, electricity is generated only by burning natural gas, without using the extra heat to boil water and produce more electricity with the steam. Combined-cycle power plants are also known to be reliable, an important quality for energy producers. Customers are counting on the lights turning on when they flip the switch!

“As customer needs increase, we have to increase our generation, and as customer needs decrease, at night, we have to decrease our output,” says Dave Eskelsen, a spokesperson for Rocky Mountain Power. “I like to say electricity is the only product that is manufactured and delivered in the same instant the customer asks for it.”

Eskelsen says the process of siphoning off the waste heat and carbon dioxide doesn’t affect the power plant at all, and the company’s leaders were happy to work with Houweling’s. Capturing the waste carbon dioxide and heat means that original material used by the power plant gets a third use.

“Our company has a long interest in environmental responsibility,” he says. “Our first and highest duty is to supply our customers with electricity, so we don’t do a lot of cutting-edge or experimental work in that area.” But when another company had an innovative, “green” idea, Rocky Mountain Power signed up.
Heat Plus Power

Reusing waste is a smart recycling practice in general. Using the leftover heat from a power generator has a term and history all its own: cogeneration, or combined heat and power (CHP) . Although it’s uncommon in the U.S. today, it’s not a new idea. In 1882, American inventor Thomas Edison used the idea of CHP in the very first commercial power plant, Pearl Street Station in New York City. CHP works well when a power plant is near its customer for leftover heat, such as the Houweling’s greenhouse.

The idea stuck around through the early years of commercial electricity but grew less popular as consumers began to demand more and more power and companies started building larger, more centralized power plants to produce enough electricity to keep up. Because CHP plants tend to be more complex to build, simpler forms of power generation ruled.
Garbage to Burn

But in Europe, CHP is much more widely used, especially in Sweden. There, the fuel of choice is not natural gas, but garbage. People in this Scandinavian country recycle most of their household waste. Anything that can’t be reused or recycled is burned for heat and hot water for millions of people. In 2016 alone, the country burned nearly 2.3 million tons of household waste for energy. They’re so efficient with recycling or reusing waste that they sometimes don’t have enough of their own waste to burn and get more from other countries, which pay Sweden for the service. But all of this didn’t happen overnight.

Sweden started burning garbage for heat and energy in 1904. Through the 20th century, officials encouraged residents to separate recyclables. In the 1970s, 38 percent of household waste in Sweden was recycled. Today, more than 99 percent of Sweden’s waste is recycled or burned in power plants. By comparison, in 2018, the last year that data is available, Americans recycled just 32 percent of their household waste. Anna-Carin Gripwall, the communications director for Swedish Waste Management, says Sweden is so good at recycling in part because the government has worked hard to make recycling easy and to tell people why it’s important.
“If you can give them the reason why they should recycle, they’re more likely to do so,” Gripwell says. “When everyone communicates the same thing, it gives more impact.”

Recycled paper becomes new paper products. Recycled plastic becomes new plastic products. And recycled food scraps turn into compost and dirt. About half of household waste can’t be recycled into new things, so it’s burned for energy instead. The fires heat water, which generates electricity and also helps heat homes. Because burning is a dirty process, the smoke runs through a series of air filters before it escapes the plant.
Though some might think otherwise, Gripwell says, “Basically, what comes out of the chimney stacks is water.” The dirty air filters are even reused afterward. They help fill abandoned mines.
Worth a Second Look

The United States is far from Sweden’s rate of recycling and tends to use different fuel sources. But the efficient combined-cycle power plant is common here. And some American companies are looking at CHP again.

Houweling’s, the greenhouse attached to the power plant, expanded in size in recent years. As of 2021, it could grow 750 acres worth of tomatoes with a fraction of the resources. Bell says it is able to do more with the heat and carbon dioxide from the power plant. Turning greenhouse gases into greenhouse vegetables? Sounds like a pretty tasty plan.

options.label: Reusing waste as an energy source is an expensive and time-consuming process.
Reusing waste to create energy for electricity can reduce harm to the environment.
Agriculture would be less expensive if every country began using the combined heat and power model.
Sweden is able to reuse most of its waste because it has a small population that does not create much waste.
