articles.title: Falling through Earth might be a long and fruitless trip
articles.subtitle: Air resistance and friction would prevent reaching the other side
articles.author: Emily Conover
articles.description: Emily Conover discusses the possibility of someone falling through the center of Earth.
articles.intro: When you were younger, did you ever think about digging a hole from one side of the Earth to the other? Physics can take this mind game one step further to figure out what would happen if you fell through the Earth.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what physicists consider when determining whether someone can fall through the Earth.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2016. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs, or textbooks, visit: https://www.societyforscience.org/permission-republish .

blocks.text: The Earth is largely solid or molten rock that’s hot enough to melt iron. So you could never build a tunnel through its diameter. But let’s play a mind game and imagine that you could burrow from one side of the planet through to the other. Physicists do this all of the time. And falling down a hole through the center of the Earth would be rough, they note. Indeed, some now conclude, it might be downright impossible. (And yes, for reasons other than the magma-hot rock or dense metal core.)

As you fell, you would be propelled by the force of gravity. You’d fall and fall and fall an immensely long way. The planned distance would span more than 12,000 kilometers (7,500 miles). There’s also friction to consider. The drag of air resistance and the friction of the tunnel walls would serve to slow your fall.

A new study calculates how these factors would affect an intrepid traveler who jumped down that imaginary tunnel through the center of the Earth. In so doing, it tackles what has become a classic problem for physics students: imagining a hole through the middle of the Earth.

If you could jump into such a hole — or, say, could ride through it on a futuristic transit system — gravity would initially accelerate you downward. It would quickly increase your velocity, or speed. Once you passed Earth’s center, you’d keep going. Now you’d actually be moving upward toward the other side’s surface. Gravity’s inward pull would gradually slow that rise. Your momentum, though, would carry you to the other side. There, you might be able to clamber out of the hole. And all of this, physicists have estimated, could take less than an hour.
Or maybe not.

“There’s a number of assumptions that you have to make to solve this problem,” says Alex Klotz. He is a physicist at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge. He was not involved in the new study. To simplify the calculations, scientists have had to make many unrealistic assumptions. They assume that Earth is a sphere of uniform density (it isn’t). They assume the planet is not rotating (it is). And they assume that there is no drag from friction or air in the tunnel (there would be).
The new study added drag from air resistance and friction from tunnel walls into its calculations. As such, this is “a step forward,” Klotz says.

With those factors, it could be well more than a year before you even reach Earth’s center. And you’d never make it to the other side. Scientists report this in a paper published online June 3 at arXiv.org.
The quandary is fun to think about, say the authors. Thomas Concannon and Gerardo Giordano are both physicists. They work at King’s College in Wilkes-Barre, PA.

To calculate the drag caused by air in the tunnel, the pair assumed that you would travel in a vehicle with a shape similar to that of a large airplane. They calculated that it would take a whopping 1.8 years to get to Earth’s center under such conditions. But you might not even make it there. That’s because the air deep down would be so highly pressurized that it would behave more like a solid than a gas. The scientists concluded that the tunnel would have to be emptied of all air in order to function as intended.

Yet even in an airless tunnel, there would be friction from contact with the walls or the rails that would direct the underground shuttle. In such a scenario, the scientists determined it would take roughly 19 minutes to get to the center. But energy is lost due to friction. That loss means you wouldn’t be able to overcome the pull of gravity to make it to the other side. Instead, you would fall back down before reaching the far side’s surface. And the process would repeat itself. That would cause you to oscillate back and forth through Earth’s core until finally you slowed to a stop at the center.

“Neither of us think this technology is going to be available anytime soon," says Giordano. The technical challenges of building such a tunnel would be insurmountable. Instead, the problem is a thought experiment and a tool for instructing physics students. And their new result may further satisfy eager science enthusiasts.

In a 2015 paper in the American Journal of Physics , Klotz attempted to solve the problem too. He used a realistic model of Earth’s density. No one had done that before. But he didn’t account for friction. When his result was covered in online news outlets, Klotz says commenters complained: “This idiot didn’t even take friction into account — this sucks!” It seems there are always critics. Even for mind games.

options.label: The recent information collected about the conditions in the center of the Earth have convinced scientists that travel through it is not possible.
Scientists will continue to consider the mind game until they determine a way to successfully travel through the center of the Earth.
The main reason travel through the center of the Earth isn't possible is because of the rock and metal at the Earth's core.
When scientists consider the pressure of air and the loss of energy from friction, they conclude that you couldn't fall through the center of the Earth.

It emphasizes how difficult it is to determine what would happen to someone who traveled through the Earth.

options.label: The author describes the variables included and excluded from the mind game and how it affects the experiment.

articles.title: Working the Farms
articles.subtitle: Children on the Move
articles.author: Mike Weinstein
articles.description: Mike Weinstein discusses the migrant children who work on farms in the U.S.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Mike Weinstein discusses how children as young as 7 years old work on farms picking fruits and vegetables as migrant workers.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the working conditions migrant children face.
articles.permissions: "Working the Farms: Children on the Move" by Mike Weinstein, AppleSeeds, © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Carus Publishing Company, d/b/a Cricket Media, and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: Migrant farm children grow up quickly. A migrant is someone who moves from one place to another. Many migrant children work beside their parents in the fields when they are as young as 7 years old. By the time they are 13 or 14, they are often full-time workers.
The children help make money for their families. The work they do is important, because farm workers put food on America's tables. But it is not an easy life.
Migrant workers mainly pick fruits and vegetables. They also plant crops and work in packing houses, where fruits and vegetables are put into cans and jars.

When fruits and vegetables get ripe, they must be picked off the plants. Many of the plants grow low to the ground. So the workers have to kneel and crouch to reach the fruits and vegetables. They do this kind of work all day in the hot sun or in the rain. They also carry heavy baskets filled with fruits and vegetables.

Often, these workers start working in the early spring in the southern part of the country, in places such as Florida and Texas. During summer and fall, the migrant workers gradually move north because different plants ripen in different places at different times. They often finish the year in northern states such as Oregon, Montana, and Connecticut. California is one of the busiest places for migrant workers. Its warm climate allows many fruits and vegetables to grow there.

With all the moving around, it's hard for migrant children to keep up with their schoolwork. Sometimes, parents want the kids working in the fields instead of going to school. The family makes more money if everyone, including the children, helps with the work. But migrant parents want their children to get an education, too. They know that their children need an education to get better jobs as adults.
Many migrant children do not live in a house or apartment. They live in temporary cabins near the farm fields.

No one knows exactly how many migrants work on American farms. The number of migrants probably ranges from almost 1 million to 5 million. One expert estimated that 400,000 children travel with migrant families.

Though living and working conditions are far from perfect, things have gotten better for many migrant children. The federal government has started special programs to improve their education, health care, and housing. California runs housing centers for migrant families. A few migrants have even bought houses or mobile homes in southern states. They still travel a lot, but now some of them have a place to call home.

Laws protect young children from dangerous work such as driving tractors, climbing on ladders, and working with insect sprays. In some places, children under 12 years old are now forbidden from farm work. But many of these laws are hard to enforce. Education is the key for a brighter future. With better schooling, migrant children can have more hope that this will come true.

options.label: Migrant children prefer working on farms with their parents over attending school.
Migrant children work hard on farms and do not have the same chance to get an education.
Migrant children are better prepared to be adults because they already have job experience.
Migrant children are used for farming because they pick fruits and vegetables faster than adults.

questions.prompt: What is the central idea of the text?

options.label: It emphasizes how important migrant children are to the industry.
It highlights how hard it is to pick fruits and vegetables outside.
It suggests that migrant workers are happy working outside.
It suggests that migrant workers are healthy.

options.label: Migrant children fall behind in school because they are often working.

permitted

options.label: New laws require farmers to provide migrant workers with homes.

questions.prompt: PART B: Which detail from the text best supports the answer to Part A?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you think it's fair that migrant children have to work to help support their families? Why or why not? How do you think the government could help these families so that it's not necessary for their children to work?

In the text, the author discusses how new laws have made it safer for children to work. Do you think this is enough? Why or why not? If you were going to write a new law to protect migrant children, what would it be and why?

In the text, the author claims that 13 to 14-year-old migrant children typically work full time. Full-time work is about 40 hours a week. Do you think you would have time to go to school and work this much in a week? How do you think working this much would affect your performance in school and other activities?

articles.title: San Martín in the Mist
articles.author: Laura Resau
articles.description: In this story from Mexico, a young girl fights against injustice in her village.
articles.intro: This story was told to Laura Resau by her friend Doña Epifania García Diaz, a Mazatec Wise One who lives in rural Oaxaca, Mexico. In this story, a young girl named Alita wants to become a Wise One.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how hoja del sueño is important to the story.
articles.permissions: "San Martin in the Mist" by Laura Resau, Cricket , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Muddy paths curve over the mountainsides of my village, as though long ribbons fell from the sky and settled there. Grandfather and I walk along them every day, winding between fruit trees and coffee bushes and mushrooms as we gather healing herbs. We pick oranges and guavas along the way, too, and our hands and faces grow sticky with sweet juice.

“Here it is!” I call out, tossing my braid over my shoulder. I slip down the path to the patch of hoja del sueño , the sleeping herb, hidden in green shade near the river. This secret grove is the only place it grows.

Grandfather walks behind me, never rushing. As he picks bunches of shiny star-shaped leaves, he thanks them. He says that every herb has a spirit behind it. Grandfather knows all about herbs, because he is a Wise One, a healer.

He seems impressed at how fast I found the hoja del sueño. Now, I think, is the time to ask him, now that I am twelve years old and nearly grown up. “Grandfather — “ I take a deep breath. “Do you think I’ll ever be a Wise One?”
“Perhaps, Alita.” He smiled under the rim of his hat. “If you have the calling.”
“How will I know?”
He looks at me closely. His eyes shine like river stones. “You will know. When your help is needed, you will know.”
I wonder: if my help is needed, will I be ready? Me, a girl who fears so many things — the dark, snakes, ghosts, dogs. Will I ever be a Wise One?

On the way back, when our basket is overflowing with fragrant herbs, we pass through the town center. It is a patch of mud surrounded by a cluster of one-room buildings — the church, the town hall, and a few houses — all made of crumbling adobe. Usually only a few people gather here, chatting as their paths cross. But now, even though it’s cold and drizzling, a crowd is standing in front of the town hall. Three strangers stand on the steps. They wear fancy black pants and narrow shoes that cover their whole feet. They are shaking the mayor’s hand — a greedy hand, smooth from lack of work. Grandfather once said that the hands of the mayor take much more than they give.
Word ripples through the crowd. “The mayor will make an announcement!”

The mayor raises his hands to quiet the chattering and says, “Ladies and gentlemen, I have news! A great dam will be built on our river. It will bring jobs and electricity to the humble villages of our valley. In only two years this magnificent dam will be completed. And then, ladies and gentlemen, we will begin the second half of the twentieth century with the modern luxuries we have dreamed of!”
People grow even more excited, talking in high, fast voices. Oh, it is so easy to believe these cheerful promises. Yet surely their hearts must be warning them.
Grandfather asks, “If you stop the water from flowing, Hugo, where will it go?” Grandfather doesn’t call him Mayor, just Hugo, because he is a man like any other.

The mayor doesn’t meet Grandfather’s eyes. He looks at his feet and then at the mountaintop. “Well, the river will rise.” He clears his throat. “The families near the river might have to move. But the splendid dam will be worth it.”
Silence. The mayor makes the hair on my arms stand on end. I slip my hand into Grandfather’s.
“How high will the river rise?” Grandfather asks.
The mayor looks annoyed, as though Grandfather were a fly he’d like to swat. “To just below the town center.”
“Hundreds of people live there,” Grandfather says.
Suddenly everyone is asking angry questions. “How long will the jobs last, anyway? And how do we know we’ll get any of this electricity?”
Again, Grandfather speaks, and even though his voice is frail, people listen. “This valley doesn’t not belong to us. What will San Martín, its true owner, say?”

San Martín is the spirit-lord of the mountain. He lives in an underground palace, bigger than a cathedral, painted gold and silver, with floors of polished green stone. At the entrance to his cave, people leave white candles burning on an altar so San Martín will protect their families. Grandfather and I go there often to thank him for sharing his forest’s herbs. A framed picture of him hangs in our house. He sits on a handsome horse and he wears an elegant blue cape, red pants, a golden shirt. His skin is pale and smooth as milk, his hair white like yellow corn.

Once, when I asked if San Martín was good or bad, Grandfather said, “He is like a storm that gives water to the plants but also makes lightning. He helps those who respect his land. But he is fierce as thunder to those who do not.”

Grandfather’s bold questions to the mayor are making my heart swell with pride. When Grandfather talks, people begin to remember the mayor’s other false promises. Before he was elected, he promised to raise funds to build a school. His first week as mayor, he convinced people to give him the power to make business deals. “Money to pay for our dear children’s education!” he said as people applauded. A week later, he sold part of our sacred woods to a logging company. It was the special part where the forest spirits lived, where no human should enter. Now it is a stump-filled clearing. And worse, after the logging deal, the mayor built new houses for himself and his friends. But never a school.

So even though we all want light bulbs and radios, no one trusts the mayor. And no one wants houses destroyed, fields drowned, trees dead. One old woman yells at the strangers, “Go somewhere else with your dam!”
But it is too late. The mayor has already signed the papers. “The dam will be built,” he says. And with his words, a cold wind rises from the valley and chills us to the bone.

That night, I’m drifting to sleep on my petate under a musty wool blanket when I see water rising. Deep green, angry water. I open my eyes, but still I see the stream raging against a giant wall of concrete and stone. I see our land drowned. I see old people dying of sadness. I see San Martín’s cave and the patch of hoja del sueño underwater.
Night after night, these visions leave me shivering and empty.

The next week, trucks carry gravel and stones and tools to our village. The tires make deep ruts in the mud that look like footprints of a strange, dangerous animal. Men from our valley have refused to work on the project, so the company has hired laborers from the outside.

One night, in a waking dream, I see the shape of a man on a horse. The way he sits, straight and proud, makes me feel brave. I slip out from under the wool blanket and stand up. Grandfather’s words come back to me. When your help is needed, you will know.

Usually I never go outside at night, not even to visit the outhouse. There could be dogs or snakes in the darkness. I take a deep breath. Barefoot, I tiptoe across the cool dirt floor, put on my sandals, and throw on my shawl. Outside, tiny droplets of water hang in the night air. Crossing our small courtyard, I stop by the kitchen to grab a basket of day-old tortillas. Then I head down the muddy path toward the river.

The mist traps the moon’s fuzzy light. I can see the steps in front of me, but not much farther. I pray that no ghosts will leap out at me, that no snakes will slither over my feet. When dogs start to growl as I pass their yards, my heart races. They run toward me, their white teeth slashing through the fog. With shaky hands, I toss them a few tortillas. After gulping the food, they wag their tails and lick my palms, suddenly gentle.

I breathe a small sigh of relief and walk on, clutching my basket. Down by the river, the mist is thick as smoke. I can barely see the stones piled everywhere. Some of the stones are as small as my fingernail; others are bigger than I am.

How will I even make a dent in this? I am too small for such a giant task. I sit down at the river’s edge and feel tears slide down my cheeks. Pools of light dance over the dark water lapping at the shore. Crickets chirp, their music muffled in the heavy fog. Somewhere, night-blooming trees give off a secret scent. All of this will disappear if the dam is built. It is this thought that gives me the strength to lift the first stone.

I carry it to a hiding place — San Martín’s cave. The cavern reaches back, into damp, dark chambers where people never go. I take two candles, still burning, from the altar and move deeper into the cave. What will I do if San Martín appears, angry at me for walking into his home, for taking his candles? Will he punish me with his lightning and thunder?

I try to let my fears drift away like the candle smoke, but my own shadow on the cave wall makes me jump. In a deep chamber, I lay the stone down and then head back outside, down the path. I move stone after stone, basket after basket of gravel. Little by little I move them, until my hands are raw and bleeding. Again and again I make my way up the trail, sliding on the slick mud, toward the cave.
Suddenly, something emerges from the whiteness. It is the figure of a man on horseback, sitting tall, a cape settled over his shoulders.

I drop the rock. I turn and run, slipping and falling down the path. When I look back, I can barely make out his figure through the mist. He is standing beside the horse, strapping the rock to its back. He leads the horse away.
My heart is pounding, my whole body trembling, but my curiosity is stronger than my fear. I follow him up the path. He brings the rock into the cave and reappears moments later.

The rest of the night, San Martín and I hide stones and gravel and tools in the cave. Even though we don’t speak, his company gives me strength, and by the time I leave, a little before dawn, we have hidden a surprising number of supplies.

When the workers report the missing materials the next day, the mayor and his friends grow red-faced and furious. They search all the houses in the village and find nothing. People are buzzing about it all day. “San Martín is saving our land!” they say.

That afternoon, the mayor announces he will hire a man to guard the supplies at night. But every man refuses, fearing the wrath of San Martín. For days, the mayor and his friends search nearby villages for someone willing to stand guard.

Meanwhile, every night, San Martín and I work together in silence. Every night a lacy veil settles in the valley, holding just enough light to let me see San Martín’s form, but never his face. Every day, exhausted, I snore over the metate , drift off in the chicken hut, and nap in the cornfield. Grandfather gives me odd looks but says nothing.
After three days, the mayor has still found no one to stand guard, so he and his friends decide to do it themselves. The mayor takes the first night.

With a nervous feeling in my belly and a bag of dried hoja del sueño in my basket, I walk down the muddy path in the dense fog. I hear the mayor whistling through his teeth. I smell wood smoke and, then, fresh coffee. Finally I see him, sitting on a log by the water, a long rifle across his lap. By his feet, a pot of coffee simmers on hot ashes, and on the log at his side sits a dented tin cup, steaming.

I crawl up behind him, slow as a slug. Luckily, he is still whistling, and the crickets are chirping, and the river is murmuring. He doesn’t hear the rustle of my feet on the wet leaves. I sprinkle the herb into his coffee and stir it with a stick. Then I back away and crouch behind a tree, waiting. He takes a sip of the coffee. And another. And another. Soon his whistling fades into gentle snoring.
I carry the first load of stones to the cave. When I return for the next load, San Martín is behind me. Together, we work all night, like old friends, until shortly before dawn.
Every night, for a week, we work. Night after night, another of the mayor’s friends falls asleep with my hoja del sueño. All seven of them. None of them is brave enough to stand guard a second night.
One week later, the mayor announces, “The dam will not be built in our valley. The investors decided to go elsewhere.” The mayor looks humble now, his shoulders hunched over in defeat.
After his announcement, people stream to the altar of San Martín, to offer candles and thanks.

The next day, Grandfather and I are gathering herbs by the stream. Raindrops run off the tips of tree leaves, off the point of my nose, off the rim of Grandfather’s hat. The paths under our feet are part of us, like the lines criss-crossing our palms. We stop to leave a candle at San Martín’s cave. The altar is lit up, brilliant orange with so many tiny flames. There is barely room for ours. “Thank you,” I whisper.
We head back down the path. From behind us comes the faint sound of hoof-beats. I turn and see San Martín, sitting on his horse just outside the cave. I smile and hold up my hand.
Grandfather looks at me, surprised. “So, Alita, you know San Martín, do you?”
I nod and bite my lip. Does he know I helped San Martín hide the materials? Will he be angry?
But he just pats my shoulder and says, “Not many people can see him, Alita. Only the Wise Ones.” He tips his hat to San Martín, who waves back and then disappears into the mist.

articles.title: Different Ways
articles.author: Christine Fournier
articles.description: Christine Fournier describes the different ways in which two farmers grow crops.
articles.intro: In this informational text, Christine Fournier discusses how two farmers grow their crops.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Tom’s and Anne’s farming practices.
articles.permissions: "Different Ways" by Christine Fournier, Appleseeds , © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited.

blocks.text: Two farmers, Tom and Anne, live only miles apart down a narrow blacktop road in central Illinois. Each year, they plant seeds in the same rich soil and face the same hot, sticky summers. Fifty years ago, these neighbors may have had a lot in common. Nowadays, Tom and Anne have different ideas about what to grow and how to grow it. Tom grew up on his farm. When he was a kid, his dad farmed about 80 acres. A big farm "Tomato Garden" by Elaine Casap is licensed back then was 240 acres. Today, Tom uses 2 under CC0. tractors, sprayers, and combines to do the work much faster. As a result, he is able to farm 700 acres of corn and soybeans. Tom’s tractor is connected to a computer and a GPS system to help him plant seeds and distribute chemical fertilizers efficiently. Scientifically designed seeds, called hybrids, help Tom to win battles against weeds, pests, and drought. Special herbicides keep weeds away but allow the corn and soybeans to thrive. Thanks to these changes, Tom can produce almost three times more bushels of grain per acre than his father did. Ten years ago, Anne started up a small 15-acre farm. She believes in a different kind of farming and eating, called the Slow Food movement. Italian Carlo Petrini started the Slow Food movement to help people combat the “crazy speed of the fast life,” eat good local food, and preserve the environment. Like Petrini, Anne wants to keep the soil rich for years to come. So, instead of chemical fertilizers, she mixes compost and dead fish into her soil. (Compost is fertilizer made of decayed plant and animal materials.) Anne plows her fields with a tractor. 1. a unit measurement of land area 2. a machine that harvests grain crops 3. a measurement used for dry goods 4. Decay(verb)to rot Then, to save fuel, she pulls out the hand tools. She hires three or four helpers each season to plant seeds for hundreds of different vegetables, from broccoli to turnips and chard. Anne is not as concerned about the quantity of her food as she is about the quality of it. In the fall, Tom sells his corn and soybeans to a grain dealer. Some of Tom’s grain becomes animal feed, and some is used to make renewable fuels like ethanol. A portion of Tom’s grain is often shipped on a barge to countries around the world.

Unlike Tom, Anne keeps her products very close to home. She mainly sells her food at farmer’s markets and to individual customers who visit her farm on a weekly basis. Anne’s market may seem small, but it is growing. In the past ten years, the number of farmer’s markets in Illinois has almost tripled. Neither Tom nor Anne knows exactly what the future of farming will be. Tom’s grandfather never would have dreamt that technology could advance as far as it has today. Anne hopes farmers will plan more for the well-being of future generations. Both hope that their farms will continue to succeed, providing people with some of their most important and basic needs. "Different Ways" by Christine Fournier, Appleseeds, © by Carus Publishing Company. Reproduced with permission. articles.title: Toxins
articles.author: Mitu Malhotra
articles.description: The daughter of Indian immigrants struggles to embrace her two worlds: home and school.
articles.intro: Mitu Malhotra is an author-illustrator with an MFA in Writing for Children and Young Adults from the Vermont College of Fine Arts. In "Toxins," the daughter of Indian immigrants struggles to straddle two worlds, life at home with her Dadi (grandmother) and the way her illness is viewed at school.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Minal’s feelings about her relationship with her Dadi.
articles.permissions: "Toxins" by Mitu Malhotra. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: “Na! Na! NA!” Dadi pulls the plate with watermelon rinds from my hand and raises her eyebrows to the skies. “Don’t throw these, it’s food,” she says.

“What? Seriously Dadi, you are going to feed us garbage?” Horror swarms inside me like a hundred hungry gnats as I stare down at my grandma’s wiry four feet tall frame and then at the dustbin where I was aiming to throw those peels. My fingers scratch the dry skin on my left inner elbow, but it fails to calm me this time. Dadi’s saree is wrapped tight around her, like armor. It’s as if being here with us in New Jersey is like being on a battlefield for her. Most days it is for me in school…and now at home too.
Dadi piles the peels into a bowl, covers it, and frowns at me. “Shaak maate.”
“Minal…” Ma signals to me after she places a bowl of chopped watermelon cubes in front of my younger brother, Manek, who is busy clicking away on his game controller in front of the TV.
I walk across and hiss to Ma. “Peels are fed to cows in India; Dadi treats me like a cow just because I weigh more than you!”

“You’ll never understand Minal, you were born here.” Ma sighs. “… it’s hard for your Dadi to be in a foreign land…as it’s hard for you…” she murmurs as her eyes trail down my face and fixate on my bloated belly. I am sure Ma is thinking about my stupid thyroid medication and how my body seesaws like a dysfunctional balloon.
“You eat poisonous rinds then.” My nails give my elbow a final scratch. “I’m having pizza with Adriana.”
“Vegetarian? Huh?” my mother asks. It’s more of a plea than a question.
“Why was I born into this crazy vegetarian family?” I yell and walk away without giving her the satisfaction of knowing my choices in this life.

Covid has really cracked my Dadi. My grandpa passed away in India right before the pandemic began. Pappa insisted on bringing Dadi to live with us here. And boy has she been acting up! Hoarding lentils, rice and atta in the first few months of the pandemic. Then she pickled every green chili and yellow lemon in sight. No one could convince her that there wasn’t going to be a food shortage in America, that it is the land of plenty. Now she is cooking vegetable scraps!!!

Dadi infects my life like a persistent virus. Dadi and Manek share my old bedroom. Adriana’s dad lent Pappa tools and helped him put up dry walls and a window to turn our one car garage into a room for me when Dadi moved here. I hear every conversation that happens on our kitchen table late at night or early morning because it is right next to the wall of my garage-turned-into-bedroom. Dadi filling Ma’s ears with poison like: ‘ This Navratri when Minal turns sixteen introduce her to Urvi-ben; her son is also little plump, good match ,’ or something like, ‘English speaking, US passport, no boy from our village will say no to that just because she is little fat. ’

I hated when people made fun of me at school, whispering and gossiping about my condition. But thank God we have returned to in-person school! I was choking to death trapped at home with Dadi raising her eyebrows at everything I eat, drink or wear.

Everyone else is happy with her. Ma gets to be with Pappa at Dunkin’ while Dadi cooks and takes care of Manek, who adores her. Dadi calls him Manek Maharaj as if, at eight-years-old, he is king of our house. She constantly says that my brother’s eyes and nose are just like our grandfather’s and proclaims that one day Manek will own not one but six Dunkins’ according to divine revelations from her very own Lord Krishna. Besides, Dadi feeds Manek everything with a dollop of butter on top. And butter is Lord Krishna’s favorite food. To me, Dadi will give no pat of butter, always a dry rotli with shaak.
“Didi…are you going to Adriana’s?” Manek looks up from his video game.
“None of your business.” I grab my backpack and stomp out towards my friend’s house.

“Good you’re early. With Mr. Kim’s six worksheets, forget any TV today.” Adriana puts the pizza box on the table between us. Since my best friend works at the Pizza Hut on the weekends, she gets preferred pricing that is so discounted it costs her next to nothing to buy a large pie with all the toppings. By turns we eat snacks at my place or pizza at hers while we finish our homework. I am in my favorite spot, a perfect pizza slice in hand, watching the world go by from Adriana’s front porch. I pick and chew the pepperoni rounds one by one. Their greasy salty ooze tickles my tongue, and fills my mouth. I know Dadi peeks out from the side window of our house to check on us…from four houses down…no way can she see the topping on our pizza. Adriana and I have sworn a pinky promise on this one.

“Dadi’s regressing: we ate cauliflower stems yesterday, and she’s cooking watermelon rinds today. Aren’t vegetable peels toxic?” I ask Adriana, who is so smart that she beat hundred other freshman applicants to get selected for a day camp on toxicology at our state university. After Adriana’s summer at the Rutgers campus we have been planning to make our college applications stellar and use our gray cells to escape this teeny town with its twenty-four people. Out of which twelve people are my very own blood relatives.
Adriana taps her pencil on my worksheet. “Most peels are safe, good sources of vitamins.”
“You’re saying we should eat peels? Eeew! GROSS!” I pinch my slim nose, the only part of my body that my family proudly agrees is perfect, the part I focus on when taking selfies.
Adriana’s fingers fix her fringe. “Don’t you eat apples and cucumbers with their skins?”
“Yes…” — my fingers find the raw eczema patch on my inner elbow again — “…but we don’t eat watermelon peels.”

“Undercooked food can be ‘toxic’ but cooked stuff is fine; cauliflower stems, turnip greens, carrot leaves are not ‘toxic.’ The world wastes too much food. Our ancestors ate every edible part of a plant, plenty of forgotten foods out there that are not toxic.”
“You’re telling me Dadi’s sane — she’s not trying to poison me?” I hate it when Adriana emphasizes words with finger quotes.

“Stop overreacting Minal Patel.” Adriana raises her eyebrows at me. “Your Dadi’s not got dementia or Alzheimer’s or trying to poison you with systemic toxicants. She made the most awesome samosas last week.”

Adriana loves to use fancy medical words because she dreams of being a doctor. I dream of becoming a PA — physician’s assistant, wear scrubs, and work in a sanitized doctor’s office far away from all the Patels and all the Shahs who spend all their lives either in a Dunkin’ or Subway kitchen loading unloading soiled crates of milk, buns and doughnuts.
“Old people should just disappear.” I tear off the largest slice of the cheesy pizza to keep my fingers from tearing at my elbow’s patch of eczema.
Adriana glares at me, makes the sign of the cross thrice, plugs in her buds.

“Sorry…I didn’t mean it.” I whisper and shrug my shoulders. I really don’t mean to upset Adriana because I know how hard it was for her when her Lola suffered from Covid and passed away. I don’t want Dadi to die. I just hate that she can’t think beyond cooking and marriage for girls.

Adriana is truly the sister I wish I had; she understands me. Period. We both aced freshman biology. This year I am helping her with English essays and she is helping me with math homework. A scholarship is my only ticket out of this life. Even though Pappa has agreed to pay for two years of community college, but, if I continue to live at home, it won’t be long before Dadi arranges some weird match for me with the astrological blessings of her village priest in Gujarat. And I have sworn not to work at Pappa’s Dunkin’ Donuts with our whole extended family there. Pappa employs his younger brother and two of his brother-in-laws, and their families live within five-square inches of us. In our town most kids work in the family owned shops and businesses right out of high school. Adriana can work at the Pizza Hut or McDonald’s but Ma is dead against that for me. I can’t be touching or cooking raw meat — what if stuff splashed on me!

For now, I try to focus on the homework like Adriana. Analyze the Bill of Rights and the practices of the new Republic for history. Check. Use the notion of a point and line to develop definitions for angles, circles, and parallel lines for geometry. Check. Explore the representation of a subject in two different artistic mediums: “Landscape with the Fall of Icarus” by William Carlos Williams and Breughel’s painting with that identical title. CHECK. Adriana hasn’t spoken to me this entire time. I gather my books. I rub my left elbow. As I watch the fireflies twinkling in the bushes, I search for the piece of gum I need to pop in before going home.
Next morning Ms. LeBlanc sees us in the hallway and asks, “Why so glum?”
“Fess up!” Adriana nudges me, then continues, “Minal’s mad cause her grandma cooked watermelon rinds. It’s some secret family recipe.”

I grimace and slam my book into Adriana for blurting that out, especially to Ms. LeBlanc who will write my college recommendations. I don’t want any trouble with that. What will Ms. LeBlanc think? That our family is totally uneducated? That I come from a poor family that eats vegetable peels?

But Ms. LeBlanc chuckles; her uplifting, musical, warm-eyed laugh echoes around me. “Sweet Jesus, I had no idea people in other places on this planet cooked watermelon rinds. Down south, in Louisiana, my MawMaw pickled them in vinegar and sugar, and I ate them as a child.”
“What did I tell you?” Adriana’s eyebrows shoot up through her fringe and she turns towards me.
“No… way…” I mumble.
“Supreme serendipity, Minal…” Ms. LeBlanc says, “A Pulitzer Prize winning author pickled melon rinds, too.”
“Who? Harper Lee? Hemingway?” I stare at my English teacher.
“Eudora Welty sent jars of scrumptious rind pickle to William Maxwell, who was her longtime friend and editor.”

“Welty who wrote The Worn Path … pickled… WATERMELON… rind?” I utter each word with a hiss of hot air from inside my cheeks. I can’t believe I am having a conversation about vegetable peels with my favorite teacher.
Adriana chimes in, “Didn’t German immigrants make sauerkraut with whole cabbages, stems and all, for winter use back when there were no supermarkets selling packaged foods?”
Ms. LeBlanc nods. “And down south people devised new recipes during the food shortages of the Civil War. Tell me Minal, how was your grandma’s recipe?”
“Oh… I didn’t try it…” I look down and rub my thumb on the sleeve of my tee cutting into my inner elbow.

“Maybe you should. Perfect inspiration for the memoir piece in your language arts portfolio.” Ms. LeBlanc spirits away leaving us in the middle of school-bell-ringing-chaos-ensuing-teens-rushing-mayhem.
The rest of the school day is uneventful. And later, at my house, Adriana cheers up when Dadi fries spicy samosas for our snack.
“This Saturday, I’ll treat you to a special vegetarian pizza with jalapenos, Dadi.” Adriana smiles as Dadi slides two extra samosas on her plate.
“Na… Na, no pissa,” Dadi waves off her hands, “I like home cooking; it’s free.”

I smirk at Dadi’s pronunciation of pizza sans the ‘t’ sound and watch her chatting with Adriana in her broken English. Adriana devours the samosas and the attention. Both of them raise eyebrows in unison, Dadi and my best friend look like long lost twins. I gape at these two people, from two ends of the world, one I used to love, the other I used to hate. Now, I am not sure how I feel about either of them.
“Move it, move it, we have tons of homework.” I grab my plate of samosas and charge towards my room. Adriana grumbles and follows me.
That night, when I walk past my old bedroom, I hear Dadi teaching Manek her prayer chants. He repeats them like a parrot.
“Why do you pray so many times?” Manek asks Dadi.
“Mornings I pray for full family; afternoons I pray for Manek Maharaj; evenings I pray for Minal,” Dadi replies.

My feet stall at the mention of my name. I shrink into the wall, cloaked by the dark in the passage that leads to the first-floor bathroom, I see Dadi’s silhouette from where I stand. She sits cross-legged on the carpet in front of her tiny altar between their beds.
“But Didi fights with you every day,” Manek snickers and jumps up onto his bed.

“Arre… Minal’s body makes her angry. Even your Kaki has same disease. I pray to Krishna to cleanse your sister’s blood of poison so I can see her happily married before I die.” Dadi sighs and touches her forehead to the floor in front of her idol of Lord Krishna.
“Didi and Kaki are both fatties!”
“Chup! Big body, bigger brains.” Dadi smacks Manek’s knee. “Minal got A+ in all her exams, your Pappa told me.”

I look down at my hypothyroid-bloated-body, the butt of a billion jokes in school, the reason I work hard — to show all the bullies I have the smarts to leave them behind scrubbing shop floors while I will wear real proper hospital scrubs. I had no idea my aunt suffered like me. I had no idea Dadi prayed for me. I had no idea Pappa told her about my grades.

I inch back into my room. Lying in bed I wonder about those toxins in my blood. According to the dictionary, toxic can also mean “extremely harsh, malicious, or harmful,” when referring to a person. I slip into slumber with my mind snapping up every nasty comment I have made about Dadi over the last twenty months, or six hundred and nine days to be precise.

I wake up to pings. Adriana’s texts. She has the slow Saturday morning shift. Ma and Pappa would have left early and dropped Manek and Dadi at my aunt’s place. I roll over. I can lie in bed till noon. As I scroll through my cell, Dadi’s prayer songs waft in through the wall; why is she home? Dadi’s sing along voice whistles through my thoughts like wind through drafty windows. Should I knock on the wall, my signal for Ma, and hope Dadi quietens down? But my legs have their own agenda; they slip off the bed and cart me off to the kitchen. My mouth is talking before my brain is functioning: “You didn’t go to visit Kaki?”
“Full family’s coming for lunch. I sent Manek away with your Ma-Pappa. I’m cooking especial food: undhiyu-puri, shrikhand ne khandvi.” Dadi hands me a cup of chai.
I sit down across from her. I rub my elbow. I dip a cookie in the fragrant chai. One bite — the sweet drowns my doubts. I ask Dadi, “don’t you get bored here?”

“I know two things, cook and pray, and my time passes with that,” Dadi replies. “Lucky you — work a computer, soon you’ll drive a car like your mother, then earn money. My time girls were only taught housework.”

I look at Dadi’s creased face as she wipes her nose with the edge of her saree. I notice for the first time in my life that I have inherited her shapely, slim nose — my lips stretch into a smile. I don’t have to prompt Dadi after that. Slowly stories drip out of her then flood my morning with images. Dadi helping her mother grow vegetables. Dadi drying onions and cauliflowers in the winter sun for use in other seasons. Dadi making tea with camel’s milk. Dadi reminiscing about one harsh summer when all the vines of karela and dudhi, bitter gourd and bottle gourd, shriveled up, only the watermelons survived. Dadi cooking watermelon rind, drying every watermelon seed, peeling them as snacks for her younger siblings. Not just the sweet red flesh, every bit of the watermelon gave them nourishment.

Two hours later, I put perfect cubes of watermelon to chill in our fridge. The pungent spicy aroma from Pyrex dishes on the kitchen counter fill me with bittersweet. My insides deflate like a punctured boil oozing out its toxins.

“NA! Pachal Hato!” Dadi holds my elbow, nudges me back from the sputtering mustard seeds and whole dried red chilis in the heated oil. Her wrinkled fingers right on my itchy patch feel cool and soothe my skin that flares up just as often as my temper. Under Dadi’s watchful eye, I slide the chopped watermelon rind into the dance of spices in the pan.

How do those relationships help you understand yourself?

articles.title: The Farmer and the Viper
articles.author: Aesop
articles.description: A farmer finds a snake frozen in his field and decides to help it.
articles.intro: Aesop was a storyteller who lived in ancient Greece between 620 and 560 BCE. This story is part of his collection of tales known as "Aesop's Fables," which did not survive in writing but were passed down by people retelling them. They have deeply influenced children's literature and modern storytelling culture.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, note the language used to describe the snake and the farmer.
articles.permissions: "The Farmer and the Viper" by Aesop is in the public domain.

blocks.text: One winter a Farmer found a Viper frozen and numb with cold, and out of pity picked it up and placed it in his bosom. The Viper was no sooner revived by the warmth than it turned 3 4 upon its benefactor and inflicted a fatal bite upon him; and as the poor man lay dying, he cried, “I have only got what I deserved, for taking compassion on so villainous a creature.” Kindness is thrown away upon the evil. "Snake" by I for Detail is licensed under CC BY 2.0.

options.label: The man helps the viper out of fear.
The viper bites the man out of fear.
The viper bites the man out of instinct.
The man helps the viper out of love for all God's creatures.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements best describes the actions of the viper and the farmer?

options.label: Good deeds are rarely rewarded.
People should not put themselves in danger to help the untrustworthy.
We betray those who help us because we know they will forgive us.
The needy are often dangerous and should not be helped.

questions.prompt: PART A: Which of the following statements best describes the central theme of this fable?

"The Viper was no sooner revived by the warmth" (Paragraph 1)
"I have only got what I deserved" (Paragraph 1)

Have you ever showed kindness upon someone only to feel betrayed in the end? Why do you think that person did not repay your kindness?
Do you believe that the man in the story deserves to be so hard on himself for doing a good deed? Why or why not?

articles.title: The Brave Librarians of Timbuktu
articles.author: Barbara J. Tuttle
articles.description: Librarians in Timbuktu organized a secret mission and saved 377,000 manuscripts from destruction.
articles.intro: Religious extremist groups, like Al Qaeda, use violence to force people to follow their religion and often limit people's rights. In this text, a man tries to rescue ancient manuscripts before it is too late.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how the author describes the city of Timbuktu.
articles.permissions: "The Brave Librarians of Timbuktu" by Barbara J. Tuttle, Faces , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Salt comes from the north, gold from the south, but the word of God and the treasures of wisdom are only to be found in Timbuktu.” — 15th century West African proverb

Ever hear the expression “from here to Timbuktu”? People use the word “Timbuktu” to mean a faraway, remote, and possibly mythical place. But not only is it a real city in the north of Mali, it was once the crossroads of the world. Hundreds of years ago, caravans passed through Timbuktu, trading gold, salt, and other goods between the Middle East and Morocco. Timbuktu became a city of wealth, not just in material riches, but also in learning and ideas.

Until recently, many people, even some scholars, believed that Africa had no written language until the arrival of the European colonizers. But Timbuktu proves that to be false. While Europe was still in the Middle Ages, the scholars of Timbuktu created hundreds of thousands of manuscripts in beautiful calligraphy (decorative handwriting) that discuss every possible subject: astronomy, poetry, mathematics, medicine, botany, religion, law, politics, and history. These manuscripts, which date back to the 1100s, are fragile and written on dried animal skins. The residents of Timbuktu guarded them in their homes for centuries.

In 2012, their existence was threatened by an invading group of jihadis (jee-hah-dees), warriors from a branch of Al Qaeda, the group responsible for the 9/11 attacks on New York City’s World Trade Center in 2001. They set fire to 4,200 manuscripts they found. But thanks to a brave group of librarians and their helpers, all the other manuscripts, 377,000 in total, were evacuated to safety.

Timbuktu lies in a region called the Sahel, which means “shore.” It’s a band of land along the south border of the Sahara Desert, which people thought of as a sea, a sea of sand. The Sahel is a crossroads of ethnic groups. Because Timbuktu sat on the great trading route, scientists, poets, philosophers, and engineers gathered there to exchange and debate ideas. Teachers and students met in the mosques to learn and debate, and together these mosques became one of the world’s first universities. Of the 100,000 people who lived in Timbuktu in the 1600s, one-quarter were students. Everyone could read, including women, children, and enslaved people.

Eventually, Timbuktu was conquered by Morocco and occupied by other civilizations. Trade across Africa declined with the European discovery of America. Timbuktu faded in population, wealth, and importance and was forgotten. But the treasured manuscripts remained in the care of various families.

Abdel Kader Haidara comes from one of those families. His grandfather, a well-known scholar, chose Abdel to take care of all the family’s manuscripts. But Abdel came to see that all the manuscripts of Timbuktu should be collected and placed in a library where they would be stored properly. Many of them were falling apart from water and termite damage.

He raised lots of money and traveled throughout north Mali, asking families if he could buy their manuscripts. He placed them in the newly built Ahmed Baba Institute, named for a great Timbuktu scholar of the 1600s.

In the 2000s, the world began noticing Timbuktu again. This was because of the document collection, which had gained some fame, and because of the new international popularity of the music of the Sahel. The annual Festival in the Desert drew musicians and fans from around the world.

But when Al Qaeda overtook all of north Mali in 2012, they enforced a rigid version of Islamic law. Women had to be completely veiled, and no music was allowed on the radio, only recitations of the Quran, the holy book of Islam. It was only a matter of time before they came to destroy the manuscripts, which didn’t fit with Al Qaeda’s idea of Islam.

Haidara assembled a network of librarians and others they could trust. Secretly, at night, careful not to attract the attention of the Islamic police who patrolled the city, he and his team brought hundreds of footlockers to sneak the manuscripts out of the Institute. Then dozens of volunteer drivers, passing through checkpoints manned by police and jihadis with assault rifles, making many dangerous trips back and forth, sneaked them out of north Mali and south to safety in Bamako, the capital.

Every single one of the 377,000 manuscripts made it through safely. Eventually, the French army came to help drive the jihadis out of Mali. Now the manuscripts are digitized on computers and cataloged so that people around the world will always be able to see them. This drama also brought world attention to the fact that hundreds of years ago, Timbuktu had a tradition of writing and scholarship as rich as anywhere.

options.label: Abdel Kader Haidara's brave actions helped preserve the written history of Timbuktu.
Timbuktu was a city with little cultural or academic history before it was taken over by Morocco.
Abdel Kader Haidara hid many ancient manuscripts so his connection with Al Qaeda would not be discovered.
Timbuktu fully recovered from the Al Qaeda invasion and thousands of manuscripts were returned to its library.

"While Europe was still in the Middle Ages, the scholars of Timbuktu created hundreds of thousands of manuscripts in beautiful calligraphy (decorative handwriting) that discuss every possible subject…" (Paragraph 2)

discussionQuestions.prompt: In "The Brave Librarians of Timbuktu," we learn about the people of Timbuktu — especially women — having their rights suddenly taken away by Al Qaeda. How do you think it would feel if your freedom and rights were taken from you? Why?
In "The Brave Librarians of Timbuktu," we learn that Al Qaeda is an extremist religious group. What role, if any, do you think that religion should play in society? Why?

By destroying libraries and schools, Al Qaeda and other extremist groups limit the opportunities that citizens have to be educated. What do you think the goal of education for young people should be? Why should schools exist in a society? Who should decide what children are taught?

In "The Brave Librarians of Timbuktu," the author states that until recently many people did not understand the truth about Timbuktu's rich cultural history. Why is it important to learn about and remember truthful and accurate accounts of history? What problems could happen when we are only taught one point of view about history?

articles.title: Open to Experiences: An Interview With Yo-Yo Ma
articles.author: Elizabeth ten Grotenhuis
articles.description: Cellist Yo-Yo Ma discusses his life and the importance of listening to diverse perspectives.
articles.intro: There are millions of immigrants living in the United States of America, and many people who live there are descended from immigrants. Yo-Yo Ma is an immigrant to the United States who is also a world-famous musician.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what Yo-Yo Ma says about his experience as an immigrant.
articles.permissions: "Open to Experiences: An Interview with Yo-Yo Ma " by Elizabeth ten Grotenhuis, Cobblestone , © by Cricket Media, Inc. Reproduced with permission. All Cricket Media material is copyrighted by Cricket Media, Inc. and/or various authors and illustrators. Any commercial use or distribution of material without permission is strictly prohibited. Please visit http://www.cricketmedia.com/childrens-content-licensing for licensing and http://www.cricketmedia.com for subscriptions.

blocks.text: Yo-Yo Ma is a world-famous cellist. Part of his fame may come from the fact that he has been playing the cello for most of his life — since he was four years old. He has spent his life bringing music to people around the world, trying to encourage connections across cultures through music. Ma has recorded more than 90 albums and won more than 18 Grammy awards. Recognizing that Ma is more than a gifted musician, President Barack Obama presented him with the Presidential Medal of Freedom, the nation’s highest civilian award, in 2011. Ma shared some of his thoughts about being an immigrant and how that made him open to new experiences.
Your father, a violinist, and your mother, a singer, moved from Nanjing, China, to Paris, France. What caused them to leave China?

My father left China as a young man of 25 in 1936 to study at the Sorbonne in Paris, where he received a doctorate in musicology. My mother moved to Paris in 1949 to study voice. Although my parents had met in China, it was not until they were in Paris that they became well acquainted and then married. My sister was born in Paris in 1951. I was born there four years later in 1955. Our father taught us music from the time we were very young. I began playing the cello at age four. My sister is a wonderful violinist who also became a pediatrician.
Your family immigrated to the United States when you were seven years old. Why did your family move again, and how did you experience that move?

Our immigration to the United States came about thanks to a chance encounter. My uncle was working as an engineer in Rochester, New York. In 1962, we made a trip to the United States mainly to see my uncle but also to visit other places as tourists. In New York City, my sister and I gave a concert. A French American lady, who had founded the K-6 independent Trent School, came to the concert. She was so impressed that she offered our father a position teaching music at her school. My father felt this would be a wonderful opportunity for our family and accepted. So, we came to the United States, and I enrolled in the Trent School as a second grader.
What are your most vivid memories as a child moving from Paris to New York City?

It was quite a shock to move from Paris to New York. For example, I was struck by the tall skyscrapers in New York. They made the sky seem smaller, more distant than in Paris. On the other hand, I was amazed that you could walk, even run, on the grass in Central Park. In Paris, you were not permitted to walk on the manicured park lawns.

I think my experience helped me begin my journey of being open to different ways of seeing and experiencing life. I started to become comfortable with being both an outsider and an insider in a place. I knew I could never be in two (or more) places at one time, and yet those different places were always a part of me. They shaped who I was and who I would become. Just think — I learned nursery rhymes and songs in French. Now, many years later, I read nursery rhymes to my grandchildren in English. But the two languages and the different countries they represent still mingle in my mind.
You have spoken about France and the United States. Do you still have relatives in China? Do you ever visit them?

I travel to China quite often to give concerts and workshops. Many of my Chinese relatives live in the city of Ningbo, not far from Shanghai. That is where our ancestral Ma gravesite is located. I have visited this gravesite with my relatives and lit incense to honor our ancestors. We have been able to trace my paternal ancestry back 18 generations to the year 1217. Many of my ancestors were involved in the fields of education or the military.
What kind of impact have immigrants had on music in the United States?

Immigrants have brought music from their original cultures that has influenced and helped create new musical styles in America. Recently, I have been thinking about the Czech composer Antonín Dvorák. He came to the United States in 1892 and stayed for three years, as director of the National Conservatory of Music in America in New York City. For me, Dvorák responded like an open-minded immigrant during his time in New York. He listened intently to many new musical sounds and quietly absorbed them. Then he interpreted what he heard in his own original compositions, such as in the New World Symphony . Isn’t it amazing that even though the New World Symphony was written by a Czech, it has come to represent not only the United States but also our global community? [U.S. astronaut] Neil Armstrong took a recording of the symphony on the 1969 Apollo 11 mission to the Moon!

Equally important to writing his own music, Dvorák taught his American students to listen carefully and absorb what they heard and to convey this open way of listening and responding to their own students. Students of Dvorák’s students helped create new American forms of music. I believe that famous American artists who are ultimately indebted to Dvorák include the jazz musician Duke Ellington, the composer Aaron Copland, and the composer and pianist George Gershwin.
What advice would you give to young immigrants and to young people who interact with immigrants?

First of all, I think it is important to remember that most Americans are descended from immigrants. Immigrants have the opportunity to integrate different cultures into their lives. I think it is vitally important that all of us try to listen carefully to everyone around us and try to enlarge our way of experiencing life. Recent immigrants may be able to do this more easily because they are still grappling with different cultures. But even if our families have been settled in the United States for a long time, we can still try to listen carefully and absorb new influences and be open to recent immigrants with whom we come in contact. My great hope is that everyone will think deeply about who they are, where they came from, and what they want to contribute to our remarkable country.

options.label: "Ma has recorded more than 90 albums and won more than 18 Grammy awards." (Paragraph 1)
"I knew I could never be in two (or more) places at one time, and yet those different places were always a part of me." (Paragraph 5)
"Immigrants have brought music from their original cultures that has influenced and helped create new musical styles in America." (Paragraph 7)

"I believe that famous American artists who are ultimately indebted to Dvorák include the jazz musician Duke Ellington, the composer Aaron Copland, and the composer and pianist George Gershwin." (Paragraph 8)

discussionQuestions.prompt: Does your family have an immigration story? If so, do you know about it? If your family did not immigrate to the United States, what do you know about your family's history in this country, or the country you live in? How does your family's history make you who you are?

If not, look online or talk to others to learn about a contribution from immigrants and share it with the class.

articles.title: Reversing Extinction
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Conservationists have worked to save the "P-horse," the last wild horse breed.
articles.intro: Conservationists are scientists who work to protect nature. In this text, Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke explains the work that conservationists are doing to save an endangered species called Przewalski's horse.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how people’s actions have helped and harmed the P-horse.
articles.permissions: "Reversing Extinction" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Meet Przewalski’s (shuh-VAL-skee) horse, affectionately known as the “P-horse” or “ takhi ,” which means spirit in Mongolia. The P-horse looks like a mash-up of its equine relatives. Picture a horse with a large, broad head; a donkey’s stocky body; and a zebra’s cropped, mohawk-like mane.

P-horses are the last of the world’s actual wild horses. Like the American Mustang, today’s “wild” horses descend from domesticated horses that escaped their herds and adapted to life without humans. In contrast, humans never domesticated P-horses, which means they never tamed and rode them, or used these wild horses on farms.

P-horses once roamed throughout Europe and Asia. But, farms and livestock overtook their grazing land, pushing P-horses east. Finally, P-horses settled in the steppes of Mongolia, a country in Asia between Russia and China. Mongolia had vast lands for grazing, but the habitat was harsh. The temperature reaches 104 degrees Fahrenheit in summer and 50 degrees below zero in winter. P-horses grew thick, fuzzy winter coats as protection from the bitter cold and shed them in the summer heat.

Scientists did not document or name the P-horse as a species until 1881. The P-horse came to their attention when Nikolai Przewalski, a geographer and explorer, sent a skull to the Zoological Museum of the Russian Academy of Science. Scientists concluded the skull came from a wild horse and named the horse after Przewalski.

Sadly, in less than 100 years, the P-horse went from scientific discovery to extinct in the wild. A combination of factors led to their untimely end. In the early 1900s, a German merchant named Carl Hagenbeck captured P-horse foals and sold them to circuses and zoos around the world. To capture foals, hunters often killed the herd’s dominant stallion disrupting the natural breeding of P-horses. In addition, Mongolians interbred P-horses with domesticated horses which reduced the number of purebred, wild P-horses even more. Farmers and livestock continued to push P-horses from their habitat. Finally, harsh winters led these wild horses to the brink of extinction. By 1970, P-horses were declared extinct in the wild .

These once wild animals urgently needed human help. So conservation groups and zoos around the world committed to breeding P-horses. In about 70 years, P-horse numbers skyrocketed from only 31 P-horses living in two European zoos to nearly 1,000 horses living in 129 zoos in 33 different countries.

One Swiss behavioral ecologist named Claudia Feh spent years breeding P-horses at a zoo in France and reintroduced a herd back into the wild in 2003. Feh and her team flew the horses to Mongolia in the cargo holds of large planes, feeding them apples and soothing the horses with stories during the long trip. Once the aircraft landed and the cargo doors opened, the zoo-bred horses ran into their native Mongolian habitat.

Today, more than 500 P-horses graze the Mongolian steppes due to captive breeding and reintroducing P-horses into the wild. With growing numbers, The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species reclassified P-horses from extinct in the wild to critically endangered in 2008. P-horse upgraded again in 2011 from critically endangered to endangered .

Committed scientists saved P-horses from extinction. But harsh winters, competition for land, and interbreeding with domestic horses still threaten P-horses in Mongolia. To survive, P-horses need ongoing help.

Mongolia has established national parks and reserves where P-horses can safely live. Second, scientists around the world continue to bolster P-horse numbers through captive breeding. In 2013, the Smithsonian Conservation Biology Institute in Washington, DC celebrated the birth of Anne, the first P-horse foal born through artificial insemination. The institute hopes artificial insemination will allow for more efficient breeding as zoos can share P-horse sperm rather than moving male horses, which is costly and traumatic for the animals. Finally, scientists continue to monitor P-horse numbers in Mongolia and study their genetic diversity to ensure that herds maintain healthy wild populations.

options.label: P-horses are an interesting animal that you can learn more about in zoos.
Scientists are working together to save the last species of wild horse.

P-horses are similar to many other types of horses in America.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?

options.label: by describing what the P-horse looks like

questions.prompt: How does the author introduce the text in paragraph 1?

"Scientists did not document or name the P-horse as a species until 1881." (Paragraph 4)
"Sadly, in less than 100 years, the P-horse went from scientific discovery to extinct in the wild." (Paragraph 5)
"So conservation groups and zoos around the world committed to breeding P-horses." (Paragraph 6)

articles.title: Noticing Mistakes Boosts Learning
articles.subtitle: Children who pay closer attention to mistakes improve skills more quickly, study shows
articles.author: Alison Pearce Stevens
articles.description: Alison Pearce Stevens discusses how noticing mistakes can help us learn.
articles.intro: In this informational text Alison Pearce Stevens discusses a study by psychologist Hans Schroder about what happens when we make mistakes. While making a mistake might feel like a negative experience, noticing these mistakes could be the key to learning.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how growth mindset and fixed mindset impact children when they make mistakes.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , April 19, 2017. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: https://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: Mistakes get a bad rap. People often brush them aside by saying, “I’ll do better next time.” But students who pay close attention to their mistakes actually do learn a task faster than kids who ignore them. Focusing on what went wrong helps us learn, a new study shows.

Hans Schroder is a psychologist at Michigan State University in East Lansing. He and his team wanted to know how people’s brains respond to mistakes. People can ignore a mistake by simply pretending it never happened. Or they can mull it over. They can try to figure out what went wrong and where. Schroder suspected that which response people chose might strongly affect how well they learned.

To find out, the team recruited 123 children, all six to eight years old. This is an important time in a child’s life. It is when most kids are beginning school. How well they do in school can be related to their mindset about learning and intelligence.

A mindset is a particular attitude about a situation. Students who have a “fixed” mindset tend to believe that they are born with a certain level of intelligence. They don’t believe it can ever change. Students with a “growth” mindset, however, think they can get smarter through hard work. Scientists have shown that this mindset can affect how well students learn.

To figure out whether each child had a fixed or a growth mindset, Schroder asked the recruits a series of questions. He then put a special cap on each child’s head. That cap held 64 small sensors called electrodes . The cap held these against the child’s scalp and recorded electrical signals as they sparked between the child’s brain cells. This let Schroder spy on patterns of activity inside each child’s brain.

While wearing the cap, children played a computer game. In it, they rounded up animals that had escaped from a zoo. Players had to press the space bar when they saw one of the escaped critters. But the game came with a twist. Three orangutans were also helping round up the animals. When players saw the orangutans, they were not supposed to press the space bar. The children could make two kinds of mistakes — either responding when they shouldn’t or not responding when they should. As they played, the electrodes recorded their brain’s activity.

When Schroder examined the data, he found a clear pattern. Small regions of the brain responded in the children who had a fixed mindset. Each response lasted just 150 milliseconds. The brains of children with a growth mindset showed much more activity. What’s more, a larger network of areas responded. And those areas did so for longer periods — up to 500 milliseconds. This shows that these brains were paying attention to mistakes, Schroder says.

Children with growth mindsets were also better at bouncing back after their mistakes. “They were more likely to get the next trial right,” Schroder says. “It was almost as if the children with growth mindsets were willing to engage with their mistakes in order to correct them.” In contrast, “those with fixed mindsets wanted to ignore their mistakes,” he says.
His team’s results appear in the April issue of Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience .

“This research demonstrates one way that a growth mindset helps you learn more,” says Allison Master. She is a psychologist at the University of Washington in Seattle. She was not involved with the study. “When you face your mistakes and are ready to learn from them,” she says. “Then you can get better over time. But if you run away from your mistakes and try to ignore them, you’ll never improve.”

options.label: Accepting and learning from your mistakes helps you improve when you try again.
Students have been taught to avoid mistakes rather than accept them.
Making mistakes shows that you're someone who's not afraid to take risks.
People with fixed mindset don't learn from their mistakes because they don't make them often.
