articles.title: Tiny Plastic, Big Problem
articles.subtitle: Scientists find that tiny pieces of plastic travel great distances, threatening the ocean ecosystem
articles.author: Alison Pearce Stevens
articles.description: Much of our plastic trash ends up in the ocean and causes major problems for underwater ecosystems.
articles.intro: Alison Pearce Stevens is a writer for many publications including Science News for Students. She is a self-proclaimed former beekeeper and forever science geek who specializes in writing about science and nature for kids. In this article, she explores what happens to discarded plastic. Purpose of Reading: To understand how and why plastics affect marine life.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the problems that plastic pollution presents and possible solutions mentioned in the text.
articles.permissions: "Tiny Plastic, Big Problem" from societyforscience.org , © 2015, Society for Science. Reprinted with permission, all rights reserved.This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs, or textbooks, visit: https://www.societyforscience.org/permission-republish .

blocks.text: Plastic bottles lying in the gutter. Grocery bags tangled in branches. Food wrappers scuttling across the ground on a windy day. Although such examples of litter easily come to mind, they only hint at the serious and growing problem of plastic pollution — a problem mostly hidden from view.
The problem with plastics is they do not easily degrade. They may break down, but only into smaller pieces. The smaller those pieces get, the more places they can go.

Many pieces wind up at sea. Tiny bits of plastic float throughout the world’s oceans. They wash up on remote islands. They collect in sea ice thousands of kilometers from the nearest city. They even meld with rock, creating a whole new material. Some scientists have proposed calling it plastiglomerate (pla-stih-GLOM-er-ut).

Exactly how much plastic is out there remains a mystery. Scientists are hard at work trying to find out. So far, though, experts haven’t found as much plastic floating in the oceans as they expected. All that missing plastic is worrisome, because the smaller a plastic bit becomes, the more likely it will make its way into a living thing, whether a tiny plankton or an enormous whale. And that may spell some real trouble.
Into the Soup

Plastics are used to make countless everyday products — from bottles to auto bumpers, from homework folders to flowerpots. In 2012, 288 million metric tons (317.5 million short tons) of plastic were produced worldwide. Since then, that amount has only grown.

Just how much of that plastic winds up in the oceans remains unknown: Scientists estimate about 10 percent does. And one recent study suggests as much as 8 million metric tons (8.8 million short tons) of plastic wound up in the ocean in 2010 alone. How much plastic is that? “Five plastic bags filled with plastic for every foot of coastline in the world,” says Jenna Jambeck. She’s the researcher from the University of Georgia, in Athens, who led the new study. It was published February 13 in Science.

Of those millions of tons, as much as 80 percent had been used on land. So how did it get into the water? Storms washed some plastic litter into streams and rivers. These waterways then carried much of the trash downstream to the sea.

The other 20 percent of plastic ocean trash enters the water directly. This debris includes fishing lines, nets and other items lost at sea, dumped overboard or abandoned when they become damaged or are no longer needed.

Once in the water, not all plastics behave the same way. The most common plastic — polyethylene terephthalate (PAHL-ee-ETH-ill-een TEHR-eh-THAAL-ate), or PET — is used to make water and soft-drink bottles. Unless filled with air, these bottles sink. This makes PET pollution tough to track. That’s especially true if the bottles have drifted to the ocean depths. Most other types of plastic, however, bob along the surface. It’s these types — used in milk jugs, detergent bottles and Styrofoam — that make up the abundance of floating plastic trash.

Abundant, indeed: Evidence of plastic pollution abounds across the world’s oceans. Carried by circular currents called gyres (JI-erz), discarded pieces of plastic can travel thousands of kilometers. In some areas, they amass in huge quantities. Reports on the largest of these — the “Pacific Garbage Patch” — are easy to find online. Some sites report it to be twice the size of Texas. But defining the actual area is a difficult task. That’s because the garbage patch is actually quite patchy. It shifts around. And most of the plastic in that area is so tiny that it’s hard to see.
Millions of tons… gone missing

Recently, a group of scientists from Spain set out to tally just how much plastic floats in the oceans. To do so, the experts traveled the world’s oceans for six months. At 141 locations, they dropped a net into the water, dragging it alongside their boat. The net was made of very fine mesh. The openings were only 200 micrometers (0.0079 inch) across. This allowed the team to collect very small bits of debris. The trash included particles called microplastic.

The team picked out the plastic pieces and weighed the total found at each site. Then they sorted the pieces into groups based on size. They also estimated how much plastic might have moved deeper into the water — too deep for the net to reach — due to wind churning up the surface.

What the scientists found came as a complete surprise. “Most of the plastic is lost,” says Andrés Cózar. This oceanographer at the Universidad de Cádiz in Puerto Real, Spain, led the study. The amount of plastic in the oceans should be on the order of millions of tons, he explains. However, the collected samples lead to estimates of just 7,000 to 35,000 tons of plastic floating at sea. That’s just one-hundredth of what they had expected.

Most plastic that Cózar’s team fished out of the seas was either polyethylene or polypropylene. These two types are used in grocery bags, toys and food packaging. Polyethylene is also used to make microbeads. These tiny plastic beads can be found in some toothpastes and facial scrubs. When used, they wash down the drain. Too small to be trapped in filters at wastewater treatment plants, microbeads continue to travel into rivers, lakes — and eventually down to the sea. Some of this plastic would have been too small to have been caught in Cózar’s net.
Most of what Cózar’s group found were fragments broken from larger items. That comes as no surprise.

In the oceans, plastic breaks down when it’s exposed to light and wave action. The sun’s ultraviolet (UV) rays weaken the otherwise strong chemical bonds within the plastic. Now, when waves smash the chunks against each other, the plastic breaks into smaller and smaller pieces.

When the Spanish team began sorting its plastic by size, the researchers expected to find larger numbers of the very smallest pieces. That is, they figured that most of the plastic should have been tiny fragments, measuring just millimeters (tenths of an inch) in size. (The same principle applies to cookies. If you were to smash a cookie, you would wind up with many more crumbs than you would large pieces.) Instead, the scientists found fewer of these tiny bits of plastic.
What had happened to them?
Entering the food web

Cózar proposes several possible explanations. The tiniest bits might have broken down quickly into particles too small to catch in his net. Or maybe something caused them to sink. But a third explanation seems even more likely: Something ate them.

Unlike the organic matter found in living things, plastics do not provide energy or nutrients to growing animals. Still, critters do eat plastic. Sea turtles and toothed whales gulp down plastic bags, mistaking them for squid. Sea birds scoop up floating plastic pellets, which can resemble fish eggs. Young albatross have been found dead from starvation, their stomachs full of plastic garbage. While feeding, adult seabirds skim floating trash with their beaks. Parent birds then regurgitate the plastic to feed their young. (These plastic bits eventually can kill them.)
Yet such large animals wouldn’t eat pieces just millimeters in size. Zooplankton might, however. They are much smaller marine creatures.

“Zooplankton describe a whole range of animals, including fish, crab and shellfish larvae,” explains Matthew Cole. He is a biologist at the University of Exeter in England. Cole has found that these tiny critters are just the right size to snap up the millimeter-size bits of plastic.

His research team has collected zooplankton from the English Channel. In the lab, the experts added polystyrene beads to tanks of water holding the zooplankton. Polystyrene is found in Styrofoam and other brands of foam. After 24 hours, the team examined the zooplankton under a microscope. Thirteen of the 15 zooplankton species had swallowed the beads.

In a more recent study, Cole found that microplastics limit the ability of zooplankton to consume food. Zooplankton that had swallowed polystyrene beads ate smaller bits of algae. That cut their energy intake nearly in half. And they laid smaller eggs that were less likely to hatch. His team published its findings January 6 in Environmental Science & Technology.

“Zooplankton are very low on the food chain,” Cole explains. Still, he notes: “They are a really important food source for animals like whales and fish.” Reducing their population could have a widespread impact on the rest of the ocean ecosystem.
And, it turns out, not just tiny zooplankton are eating the plastic bits. Larger fish, crabs, lobster and shellfish do too. Scientists have even found plastic in the guts of marine worms.
Once there, the plastic tends to stick around.

In crabs, microplastics remain in the gut six times longer than food does, says Andrew Watts. He is a marine biologist at the University of Exeter. What’s more, eating plastic causes some species, such as marine worms, to store less fat, protein and carbohydrate, he explains. When a predator (such as a bird) now eats those worms, it gets a less nutritious meal. It also ingests the plastic. With each meal consumed, more and more plastic makes its way into a predator’s body.
That’s cause for concern. “Plastics might pass up the food chain,” says Cole, “until it gets into food that ends up on our own dinner plates.”
An accumulating problem

The thought of eating plastic isn’t pleasant. But it isn’t just the plastic that’s cause for concern. Scientists also worry about a variety of chemicals found on the plastic. Some of those chemicals come from the manufacturing process, explains Kara Lavender Law. She is an oceanographer at the Sea Education Association in Woods Hole, Mass.
Plastics also attract a variety of dangerous pollutants, she notes. That’s because plastic is hydrophobic — just like oil, it repels water.

But plastic, oil and other hydrophobic substances are attracted to each other. So oily contaminants tend to glom onto pieces of plastic. In a way, plastic acts like a sponge, soaking up hydrophobic contaminants. The pesticide DDT and polychlorinated biphenyls (or PCBs) are two such toxic contaminants that have been found in ocean-going plastics.

Even though both contaminants have been banned for decades, they are slow to break down. So they persist in the environment. To this day, they hitch a ride on trillions of pieces of plastic floating in the oceans.

One reason these contaminants were banned is because of the way they affect animals and people. When eaten, the chemicals work their way into an animal’s tissues. And there they stay. The more of these chemicals a critter consumes, the more that gets stored in its tissues. That creates a constant exposure to the pollutants' toxic effects.

And it doesn’t stop there. When a second animal eats that first critter, the contaminants move into the new animal’s body. With each meal, more contaminants enter its tissues. In this way, what had started as trace amounts of a contaminant will become increasingly concentrated as they move up the food chain.

Whether contaminants hitching a ride on plastic work their way into the body tissues of marine animals in the same way remains unknown. But scientists are concerned that they might. Just how much of these chemicals in marine organisms came from eating contaminated plastic and how much from eating contaminated food is a big question, says Law. And no one yet knows whether the problem affects people.
Managing microplastics
The very nature of microplastics makes cleanup impossible. They are so tiny and so widespread that there is no way to remove them from the seas, notes Law.

The best solution is to prevent more plastic from reaching the ocean. Trash traps and litter booms can snag garbage before it enters waterways. Even better: Reduce plastic waste at its source. Be aware of packaging and buy items that use less of it, Law suggests. Skip the plastic bags, including zippered ones used for foods. Invest in reusable water bottles and lunch containers. And say no to straws.

Law also recommends asking restaurants to stop using polystyrene foam containers. These break up quickly and are not recyclable. Talk to friends and parents about the problems of plastic, and pick up litter when you see it.
Law recognizes that reducing plastic use won’t be an easy change. “We live in an era of convenience,” she says. And people find it convenient to throw things away when they are done with them.

That’s not to say that we should do away with plastic altogether. “Plastic has a lot of beneficial uses,” says Law. But people need to stop looking at plastic as disposable, she argues. They need to view plastic items as durable things to hold on to and reuse.

articles.title: How a Flamingo Balances on One Leg
articles.subtitle: These extreme bird balancers stay upright with little muscle effort
articles.author: Susan Milius
articles.description: These extreme bird balancers stay upright with little muscle effort
articles.intro: Flamingos are well known for their tendency to stand on only one of their legs. But how do they do it? In this informational text, Susan Milius discusses scientific studies that help answer this question.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how scientists study flamingos’ ability to balance on one leg.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , August 16, 2017. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: https://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: Why do flamingos stand on one leg? Flamingo researchers get asked this question all the time. But why flamingos ever bother standing on two may be the bigger puzzle, new research suggests.

Flamingos have balance aids built into their bodies, the new study finds. That lets them stand on one leg with little muscle effort. The stance is so stable that a bird sways less (to stay upright) when it appears to be dozing than when it’s awake.

“Most of us aren’t aware that we’re moving around all the time,” says Lena Ting of Emory University in Atlanta, Ga. Just to keep our bodies standing up, we have to sense our position constantly and use our muscles to correct wobbles. Ting studies this movement, called postural sway, in people and other creatures.

Even standing robots “are expending quite a bit of energy,” Ting says. Flamingos look relaxed when they stand. But they might be working hard to stay upright too, she points out, since limiting sway isn’t always easy to see.

Ting and Young-Hui Chang of the Georgia Institute of Technology (also in Atlanta) tested balance in Chilean flamingos. These animals reside at Zoo Atlanta. Zookeepers there coaxed fluffy young birds onto a platform. It was attached to an instrument that measured how much the birds swayed. The researchers especially wanted to study birds sleeping on one leg. So the zoo let the scientists visit after feeding time. That way they might catch young birds ready to nap. “Patience,” Ting says, was key to her team’s success.

As a bird stood on one foot, the instrument tracked changes in the foot’s center of pressure — the spot over which the bird’s weight was focused. This spot wobbled around as a bird shifted to preen its feathers or look at a neighbor. But once a bird tucked its head onto its pillowy back and shut its eyes, the center of pressure made smaller changes. When birds were dozing, their center of pressure moved within a radius of 3.2 millimeters (0.1 inch), on average. That center of pressure expanded to 5.1 millimeters when the birds were active.
The researchers also studied flamingo skeletons in a museum. They saw features of the skeleton that might make birds more stable. Still, bones didn’t tell the whole story.

Researchers learned more from the whole bodies of a few dead Caribbean flamingos that a zoo had donated to them. “The ‘ah-ha!’ moment was when I said, ‘Wait, let’s look at it in a vertical position,’” Ting remembers. All of a sudden, the bird specimen settled naturally into a one-legged lollipop stance. There was no way a dead bird could be putting muscle effort into keeping that position. So the body must have some built-in ways to hold this stance without effort.

A flamingo’s hip and knee sit high up inside its body. What bends in the middle of the long flamingo leg is not a knee, but an ankle. (That explains why, to human eyes, a flamingo’s leg looks like it bends the wrong way.) Ting didn't find anything that locks the leg bones upright. She did, however, see other features of the skeleton that might help flamingos stand steadily.

How the bird distributed its weight also seemed important for one-footed balance. The flamingo’s center of gravity was close to the inner knee. That’s where the bone starts to form the long column to the ground. This made the shaky-looking one-leg nap actually stable. In fact, tests with dead birds showed the body was floppier and less stable when researchers fastened it upright on two legs instead of one. The researchers reported their findings May 24 in Biology Letters .

Reinhold Necker is cautious about saying the one-legged stance saves energy until researchers discover more. “The authors do not consider the retracted leg,” says Necker. At Ruhr University in Bochum, Germany, he has written about one-legged standing in birds. He says keeping one flamingo leg tucked up might take some energy, even if easy balancing saves some. (Ting thinks flamingos might have some special energy-saving way of holding up that other leg. The question needs more research.)

The new study helps researchers understand how flamingos stand on one leg. Still, it doesn’t explain why they do it, says Matthew Anderson. He’s a comparative psychologist at St. Joseph’s University in Philadelphia, Penn. He’s found that more flamingos rest on one leg when temperatures drop. So he thinks that keeping warm might have something to do with it.
The mystery of flamingos, in other words, still stands.

options.label: Studies have revealed that flamingos' skeletal structure and how they hold their weight contribute to their amazing balance.
Scientists have studied flamingos' balance in order to explore improving the balance of robots.
While scientists are unclear about how flamingos balance on one leg, they know it is to save energy.
Scientists have answered the question of why and how flamingos stand on one leg by studying dead birds.

articles.title: At Your Fingertips
articles.author: Ruth Tenzer Feldman
articles.description: Ruth Tenzer Feldman discusses the benefits of the National Weather Service.
articles.intro: The information that weather forecasts provide is more important than people may realize. In this informational text, Ruth Tenzer Feldman discusses the benefits of one agency in particular, the National Weather Service.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the information that the National Weather Service provides.
articles.permissions: "At Your Fingertips" by Ruth Tenzer Feldman, COBBLESTONE, March 2012 (text). Copyright © 2012 Cobblestone. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: You can find all kinds of weather information, from today’s clouds over Topeka to next year’s predicted rainfall on the West Coast, by going to the National Weather Service (NWS) at www.weather.gov. The NWS is the federal agency that provides weather, hydrologic (water-related) and climate forecasts and warnings for the nation, including U.S. territories and ocean areas. The agency, an arm of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) has "Strong Extratropical Cyclone Over the US about 4,800 employees in 131 weather Midwest" by NASA Goddard Space Flight Center forecast offices, 19 river forecast centers, and is licensed under CC BY 2.0. 6 national centers. The NWS has teams of 2 3 scientists studying satellite, buoy, radar, and computer information to forecast when and where any given condition will likely occur. The NWS Storm Prediction Center forecasts severe weather up to three days in advance and updates information as severe weather approaches. A weather alert system now warns people about 11 minutes before a tornado strikes — which is about 11 minutes longer than people had 20 years ago! The Climate Prediction Center studies weather patterns lasting months or years. These depend on El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO), which is a measure of the surface temperature of the 1. a scientific agency within the United States that focuses on the conditions of the oceans and atmosphere 2. an anchored float 3. a system for detecting the presence, direction, distance, and speed of objects tropical Pacific Ocean. Every month, the center publishes the ENSO Diagnostic Discussion forecasting climate conditions in the nation.

NWS information is used for adjusting airline flight routes, protecting property from floods or hurricanes, managing crop harvests, and even stocking up on toilet paper. Each year, Americans get more than 1,584,000 NWS forecasts as well as up to 50,000 potentially life-saving severe weather warnings. So the next time the NWS accurately predicts a clap of thunder — or anything else — be sure to give the agency some appreciative claps of your own.

discussionQuestions.prompt: How has a weather forecast helped you prepare for your day? Has a weather forecast ever helped you avoid a potentially dangerous situation? If so, how?

In the context of the text, who is in control: man or nature? How does this article help explore the relationship between humans and the Earth? How has weather forecasting allowed humans to withstand natural disasters?

In the context of the text, what are the costs and benefits of technology? How has technology contributed to humans' ability to predict the weather? Do you think there are any disadvantages to these types of technology and knowledge?

articles.title: High Hopes and Extreme Plans to Save Northern White Rhinos
articles.author: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke
articles.description: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke describes the ambitious plans scientists have to save white rhinos.
articles.intro: Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke is a science and humanities teacher based in Washington, DC. White rhinos are large land mammals that live on the continent of Africa. These amazing animals are in danger of going extinct, or no longer living on Earth. In this informational text, Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke describes the work that scientists are doing to save these animals.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on the scientists’ opinions about the plan to save the white rhinos.
articles.permissions: "High Hopes and Extreme Plans to Save Northern White Rhinos" by Jacqueline Pratt-Tuke. Copyright © 2021 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

blocks.text: Happy birthday, Edward! This spunky one-year-old celebrated his birthday with quite the party. Decorations. Cards pouring in from friends. Colorful presents. A mud-pit and hay treats. Wait, what… mud-pit? Hay treats?
Your birthday party might include a bounce house and a triple-decker cake with thick frosting, but for Edward, a southern white rhino, it’s all about rolling in the mud and snacking on hay.

Edward celebrated his first birthday in July 2020 at the San Diego Zoo in southern California. “Even though he doesn’t know it’s his birthday, it is very special to see Edward—a healthy, robust rhino—running around, enjoying his special treats,” shared Barbara Durrant Henshaw, the Director of Reproductive Sciences at the San Diego Zoo. “We aren’t just celebrating his birthday but also are celebrating the significance of his birth.”
A SCIENTIFIC SUCCESS

So, what was special about Edward’s birth? Edward was the first southern white rhino born through artificial insemination in the United States. Artificial insemination is when doctors directly inject male sperm into a female’s body. Veterinarians at the San Diego Zoo artificially inseminated Edward’s mother, Victoria, with frozen sperm from one of the zoo’s male rhinos. Sixteen months later on July 28, 2019, baby Edward was born.

Over 12,000 rare and endangered animals captivate visitors at the San Diego Zoo. But behind closed doors, there is a zoo of another kind—San Diego’s Frozen Zoo. Since 1975, the zoo’s Institute for Conservation Research has collected animal tissue and used this tissue to grow cells in a lab. The collection of animal cells is stored in five steel tanks, each about the size of a washing machine, at -321 degrees Fahrenheit. That is two times colder than the frigid temperatures of Antarctica.

The Frozen Zoo in San Diego has the world’s largest collection of animal cells with cells from over 10,000 different species, according to CBS . They hope to use these cells to save endangered animals and even bring back nearly extinct species, like the northern white rhino.
ONLY TWO LEFT

On the continent of Africa, two different subspecies of white rhinos roam the grasslands, the southern white rhino and the northern white rhino. As their names suggest, southern white rhinos live in southern Africa while northern white rhinos live in central Africa. Southern white rhinos are considered “near threatened” which means they could become an endangered species in the future. According to the World Wildlife Fund (WWF), about 18,000 live in the wild.

For the northern white rhino, the situation is far bleaker. On March 19, 2018, Sudan, the last male northern white rhino in the world, died. With Sudan’s passing, only two female northern white rhinos remain—Najin and Fatu. Both ladies live at the Ol Pejeta Conservancy in Kenya and are protected by seven-foot fences and armed guards.

With a weight of 5,000 pounds, the white rhino is the third largest land mammal, behind the African elephant and hippopotamus. Despite their enormous size, northern white rhinos are known as gentle giants and are the most social of the rhino subspecies. Sadly, the desire for rhino horns led to the mass killing of northern white rhinos. Rhino horns are worth about $3,000 per kilogram, but can sometimes fetch up to $60,000. With two horns that weigh about 2.5 kilograms each, rhinos have a price tag on their heads between $15,000 and $300,000. Poaching and the illegal sale of rhino horns drove northern white rhinos to extinction in the wild.
HOPE FOR THE NORTHERN WHITE RHINO

Edward and the San Diego Frozen Zoo offer hope for northern white rhinos. Edward showed that artificial insemination could produce a healthy baby rhino, so scientists believe that they can use a similar process to save the northern white rhino.

In the wake of Sudan's death, scientists preserved some of his sperm, which they hope to use for breeding northern white rhinos. But the process is more complicated than Edward’s artificial insemination. The last two female northern white rhinos are too old to have babies, so scientists can not use artificial insemination to inject Sudan’s sperm into them. Instead, scientists must extract their eggs and fertilize these eggs with sperm in a lab. This fertilized egg is called an embryo. Scientists hope that these embryos can one day be implanted in the womb of a southern white rhino. So Edward’s mom could give birth again, but this time to a lab-created embryo of a northern white rhino.

Scientists are also planning for another complication in the “save the northern white rhino” project. With no more male rhinos and only two aging females, they have limited access to rhino sperm and eggs. So scientists are working to create sperm and eggs in a lab. Scientists are taking cells from rhinos’ skin and transforming them into stem cells. Stem cells are cells that have the ability to grow into other kinds of cells. Scientists hope that they can manipulate rhino stem cells into sperm and eggs. From here, they would pick up the process of creating an embryo and putting the embryo inside of a southern white rhino.

But, not all scientists are on board with this extreme plan to bring back northern white rhinos. They question whether humans should manipulate the natural world, literally creating babies in a laboratory. Thus, Edward’s birth using science and technology holds both hope and questions for the future of the northern white rhino.

options.label: Edward is special because he is the last remaining southern white rhino in the wild.
Edward is not able to live like a normal rhino because he was created from stem cells.
Scientists are trying to educate the public about how to protect the remaining white rhinos.
Scientists are attempting to use new methods like artificial insemination to save white rhinos.

discussionQuestions.prompt: Poaching, or the illegal killing of animals, has killed many rhinos. In fact, they are in danger of going extinct, or no longer existing. Why are rhinos poached by humans? What do you think we should do to stop this from happening?

In the article, scientists are using methods like artificial insemination and stem cells to help the rhino species survive. In your opinion, do you think scientists should do things to help animals that are in danger survive? Why or why not?

In the article, the author describes how excited scientists are about Edward's birthday. Why is his birthday so exciting to them? What does Edward's birthday represent to them? How would you want to celebrate a big accomplishment?

articles.title: Awa
articles.author: Keisha Bush
articles.description: A bright Senegalese girl's life changes with the help of a wise four-legged friend.
articles.intro: After beginning her career in international business, Keisha Bush spent four transformative years in West Africa that would lead her down the path to becoming the award-winning author she is today. Her debut novel, No Heaven for Good Boys , and most recent short story, "Awa," both explore the beauty and complexity of Senegal's capital city, Dakar. In "Awa," a bright, scientifically-minded young girl must leave school to support her family.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on Awa’s attitude education throughout the story.
articles.permissions: "Awa" by Keisha Bush. Copyright © 2022 by CommonLit, Inc. This text is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

blocks.text: The final bell rings, and Awa bursts through the schoolhouse doors. She walks home with her friends, Khady and Fatou. She snaps a selfie of herself and scrutinizes it before posting it with the baby filter to her social media account.
“So cute!” Khady comments on Awa’s post.
“I agree,” Fatou says.
Awa looks up from her phone to exchange a smile with her friends when Khady’s brother, Ousmane catches up with them.
“What are you all planning to do for your science project?” he asks.
“I’m going to show how you can use everyday kitchen things as alternate battery sources. Like an egg or a potato,” Fatou says.
“What about you, Khady?” Awa asks.
“Whatever’s easy. It’s just a grade,” Khady replies, scrolling through photos of Beyoncé on her phone.
“I might do an experiment that extracts DNA from a strawberry and other fruit,” Awa says.
“Cool! I don’t think anyone is working with DNA in our class,” Ousmane says.

Awa smiles shyly at Ousmane as they walk towards her house. Fatou lives near Khady and Ousmane in Ngor, so the three of them will catch a Car Rapide the rest of the way home. Awa waves goodbye to her friends before entering the house.

She is surprised to find her parents in the living room. Her father is reading the daily paper, while her mother sips a cup of herbal tea and rubs her protruding belly. Usually, they’d both be still at work.
“Is everything okay?” Awa asks, worried about the baby.
Her mother looks at her with a pained look on her face.
“How was your day at school, my flower?” her father asks, pulling her into a hug.
“Good,” she replies. She then shares her science project idea.
“You’re going to be a fine scientist,” her father says, exchanging a brief look with her mother. “But that is what we need to talk about with you.”
“It’s not wasteful,” Awa says, prepared. “I’ll only need one strawberry!”

“No, Awa, it’s not that.” Her mother pauses. “I was let go from work. Your school fees are due, and with the new baby on the way... we need you to take time off from school to work, while I look for a new job.”
Awa’s head swirls.
“I can’t go to school?” She asks in a tiny voice.

“Just for a little while,” her father says. “I’m looking for more work, but we can’t make ends meet with your school fees. If you work for a few months, it will make things easier. Can you do this for your family?”
“We’ve found you a job,” Awa’s mother says softly, cutting into the silence of the room.
“Oh,” Awa replies. So soon.

Her mother tells her the details. She’ll be working for a family in Les Almedies as a maid, assisting the main housekeeper. It pays well: fifty-five thousand francs a month. It’s only a thirty-five-minute walk away, which means she won’t have to pay for transportation and she can live at home. Most maids make fifteen thousand francs a month and must live with their employer because a maid’s day begins at five in the morning and does not end until nine or ten in the evening. The ability to return home every night is a blessing. Awa has never lived away from her parents.
“You start Monday at six o’clock in the morning,” her mother says.
Awa nods. She doesn’t trust her voice for fear that she may cry.

Awa misses school the rest of the week. Her parents let her teachers know that Awa will not be returning. She spends the weekend wondering if she should finish her incomplete book report. Maybe her teacher will accept it when she goes back to school.

On Monday morning, when she arrives at her destination, Awa taps lightly at the gate of her employer’s compound so as not to wake her new employers. It’s a large multi-story, white house. The neighborhood of Les Almedies is home to some of the richest people in Dakar, both Senegalese and foreign nationals. Awa hopes everyone is nice. She is dressed in an old ankle-length wrap skirt and simple short sleeve t-shirt. Her hair is tied up in a scarf to protect it. No one wears their best to clean a house.

A Senegalese woman with a round, kindly face opens the gate. She gives Awa an approving look and waves her inside. The staff moves around quiet as mice in the early hours to prepare a seamless morning for the family.
“My name is Madame Diop,” says the woman, “and I’ve worked as the housekeeper for this family for five years.”

On their walk to the kitchen, Madam Diop tells her all kinds of things. The family moved here from South Africa. The husband works for the World Health Organization, and the wife works for Unicef. Then, she tells Awa about the previous maid, who had to travel back home to help her ailing mother. Then, she shares a little about herself, and how she lives with their employers because her village is more than three hours away in northern Senegal. She visits her family once a month for a day and a half. Awa is grateful her home is only thirty-five minutes away.

In the kitchen, Awa meets the cook, Madame Sow. She looks old enough to be Awa’s grandmother. Awa is told to sit on a stool outside the second kitchen in the back of the house to peel potatoes. After that, she starts on the carrots and then the onions. Once the family comes down for breakfast, Awa is sent upstairs to sweep and mop the floors. She cleans the bathrooms, then makes the beds with fresh linens. Fluffs the pillow just so. Replenishes the toilet paper. Puts away the dry cleaning. Cleans the windows. When she has a free moment, she’s sent down to the kitchen to wash the lunch dishes, prep for dinner, and on and on it goes.

She never meets her employers. When they return home for lunch, she is outside washing the laundry by hand. By the time they arrive home after work, her day has come to an end. Only the faint sounds of a baby can be heard throughout the home for most of the day.
The week passes by and Awa barely has the energy to check her social media posts. On Sunday, her only day off, she finds several messages from her classmates. She scrolls down to the latest ones.
Why aren’t you in school? Fatou texted her in the “Best Friends” group chat. Followed by, Are you sick?
Awa gasps when she sees the next text.
My mother said your mother lost her job. Is this true? Khady asked in the same group chat.
She doesn’t know what to say. She wishes she could make up some excuse, but there’s no use lying. Very few secrets can be kept in Dakar. Awa takes a deep breath and writes back.
I’m okay , Awa says. The baby is coming soon, and I’m working to help out.
We miss you so much , Khady writes with a sad-face emoji.

Awa wants to message back a thousand crying emojis, but that won’t fix anything. Awa’s parents would be so disappointed to know how she really feels. Awa turns off her phone. She would usually top her phone credit up every week with twenty-five hundred francs, but her days of getting an allowance are over. Her entire monthly salary will go directly to her parents at the end of the month. To stretch her phone credit, Awa decides she’ll only use her phone on Sundays for thirty minutes. That should give her a handful of Sundays to connect with her friends.

As the weeks go by, Awa falls into a rhythm, each day an echo of the day before it. Soon, she can do her tasks without thinking. Dust, sweep, mop, scrub, rinse, repeat. Do laundry. Run errands. Peel this. Clean that. Every once in a while, she catches a glimpse of her employers, whose daily rhythm moves to a drum beat different from her own. The twelve-hour day meanders by and, at six o’clock in the evening, Awa goes home. By the fifth Sunday, Fatou and Khady stop sending messages and sharing updates.

Baby Omar is born on the sixth Sunday. Awa’s grandmother comes to stay with them to help during the day. Her father leaves for Mauritania where new oil wells are being discovered every week. The money is good enough that when her father returns, Awa may be able to go back to school.

Some nights, after Awa returns home, she helps out by frying fish and making rice. By nine o’clock, Awa is deep within the land of dreams where she is older and studying to be a scientist. She travels to Asia, the Amazon, and Antarctica on scientific expeditions.

The summer in Dakar brings the rainy season. The dark clouds gather and open, releasing their tears on the inhabitants of the city, so Awa starts the day by washing the linens. This way, she can hang them up for at least two hours of sunshine and later they can catch the breeze from the coming storm. On one particular day, Awa is placing a pin on the laundry line when she spots a little black-and-white speckled cat. The cat comes up to her and rubs against her ankle.
“You’re not afraid of me, I guess,” Awa says as the cat sits down and licks the bottom of its feet. “Are you hungry?”
The cat meows.

Awa reaches into her apron pocket and pulls out the remainder of her sandwich from breakfast. The twelve-hour work day is long, and she only receives two meals during her shift. So, Awa takes the leftovers from breakfast and lunch, whatever the family doesn’t finish, to snack on until dinner at home. The cat stands up on its hind legs and reaches up for the salami. Awa takes a bite of the baguette and egg. The cat offers her a satisfied look and then asks for more.
“This is the last of the salami,” Awa says, giving the cat the rest of the meat and a piece of baguette. Hungry cats are not picky.

Leaving the cat to eat, Awa goes inside to mop the floors and dust. At the end of her work day, she is happy to make the slow walk home. It is her favorite time of day because there is nothing to do but be with her own thoughts. Today, she has a companion. The cat is waiting for her outside the compound walls. It walks beside her as she heads home. After dinner, the cat bounds up the stairs to her bedroom, where it curls up on the foot of her bed, watching Awa.
“What shall I call you?” Awa asks the cat.
“My name is Cabbage,” the cat says before curling up and falling asleep.
Awa shakes her head to clear her thoughts. She must be imagining things after such a long day. Yawning wide, she lies down and falls into a deep slumber.
The next morning on her walk to work, Awa looks down at the cat.
“Cabbage,” she says.
The cat looks up at her.
“So, that is your name?”
Silence.
“Well, aren’t you going to say anything?”
The cat meows and continues to walk alongside her. She feels stupid for imagining it could talk.

At home, Awa’s mother is fine with Cabbage accompanying Awa everywhere. She is too busy with the new baby and looking for a suitable job to give much thought to the cat. Several days pass before Madame Diop notices the cat, but she doesn’t mind either since he doesn’t attempt to enter the house. He prefers to watch Awa when she is outside laundering the linens, washing the dishes, or peeling vegetables. Madame Sow gives Cabbage his own set of bowls for food and water.

The rainy season soon becomes the cool dry season, and the turn of the new year arrives and departs. Awa turns fourteen years old; then her brother turns a year old. Awa’s father leaves Mauritania and travels to Nigeria for a better-paying job. Cabbage grows chunkier and more content, and a new rainy season arrives once again.

Returning to school fades to the back of Awa’s mind. She hasn’t seen her friends in more than a year. She cannot afford to buy phone credit, and between her work schedule and helping out at home, she has no time to visit them. The idea of taking a selfie or posting a picture to her social media accounts feels foreign and distant.
“Cabbage, you’re the only friend I have left,” Awa says one day on the walk home.
Cabbage stops and looks up at Awa.
“You’ll have more friends one day, Awa. This is just one of the many experiences you’ll have in your life,” Cabbage says.
He then stretches out his hind leg and licks the bottom of his foot.
“I knew you could talk!” Awa exclaims.
“Of course, I can talk. I just choose not to,” Cabbage states.
Cabbage starts walking again. Awa refuses to follow him. She knew he could talk! She wasn’t crazy!
“I don’t know what to think. We’ve been friends for a year. A whole year and you never said anything!” Awa says, folding her arms across her chest and pouting.
The little cat turns to look at her and sits. “You understand my meows perfectly fine, and I understand you. You know exactly how I feel about you,” Cabbage explains.

Well, that’s true. Awa can tell when Cabbage is hungry or needs to go outside to use the bathroom. She knows when he is sleepy and when he wants to be petted and snuggled. She also knows when he wants to be alone.

“Words are overrated. Most communication happens through body language, facial expressions, and tone of voice. Only humans need words, but too many use them to lie and hurt, so the animals help humans out by making you use your other senses to understand us,” Cabbage says. “For what it’s worth, I am sorry I upset you. Now come on, it’s going to start raining soon.”
Awa walks alongside Cabbage in silence for several moments, letting this soak in.
“So, all animals can talk?” she asks.
“No, cats can talk. Some birds and dogs can talk, too.” Cabbage says.
Awa bends down to pick Cabbage up before crossing the main roadway that serves as the border between Les Almedies and Les Mamelles.
“Why talk to me now?” Awa asks.
“The sadness in your voice is heavy.” Cabbages says, licking her cheek. “But you’re stronger than you think.”
“You sound like my grandmother,” Awa says, rolling her eyes.
“She knows what she is talking about.”
Awa chuckles.
“You will go back to school one day if that is what you want. In the meantime, ask Madame Diop if you could borrow books from your employer’s library so that you can continue to study on your own.”
“I never thought of that,” Awa says.
“You work hard, Awa. But you don’t achieve the things you want by dreaming about them. It takes action, too,” says Cabbage.
“Do you think they will say yes? They might not want a maid touching their expensive books.”

“Didn’t I ask you for food when we first met?” Cabbage asks. “You could have said no, and if you did, I would have found another option. Never be afraid to ask for what you want. There is a fifty-fifty chance the answer will be yes, but if not, that’s okay, too,” Cabbage says.
Awa pets Cabbage before setting him down and entering the house.
The next day, Madame Diop shares the good news that her employers will allow her to borrow one book at a time, and as long as she returns the book, she is free to read their entire library.

Awa is delighted. She floats through her tasks with ease, and she has just enough time to look through the library before her employers get home. There are hundreds, if not thousands, of books gracing every wall in the room, from floor to ceiling. Awa does the most sensible thing a person can do. She goes to the first shelf, climbs up the sturdy ladder, and picks the first book on the top shelf. She will read them in order of the alphabet. The first book she pulls is a paperback. It features an African mask on its orange and yellow cover. The title and author hover below the mask: Things Fall Apart by Chinua Achebe.

Later that evening, Awa sits in bed with the book. Cabbage lounges across her legs, and she reads the first couple of chapters out loud until he falls asleep. Awa finds the world of Okonkwo fascinating, even more so since her father is currently in Nigeria. She falls asleep mid-story dreaming of a time that seems so far from her own.

The books soon start to mean everything to her. She imagines herself walking the streets of worlds past and future: Nigeria, Ghana, New York, Paris, Madagascar, South Africa, Algeria, Russia, and more. During the morning walk to work, she fills Cabbage in on all the things he missed while asleep.
One dewy spring morning, Awa arrives at work to find Madame Diop in the kitchen looking forlorn.
“Tata, are you okay?” Awa asks.

“Yes, my dear,” she says. “I have news. The girl who used to work here has asked to come back. Her mother has passed, and she needs work to support her siblings and father. I will speak to the family when they wake, but if they say yes, they may let you go. It’s a hard decision. You are such a good girl and a hard worker.”

Awa’s head swirls. What will she do without the family’s library? Will she have to work somewhere else? What if the next family doesn’t let her borrow books? After Madam Diop dismisses her to start her chores, she immediately goes to tell Cabbage.
“What will I do?” Awa says, tears springing from her eyes.
“Remember when you had to leave school?” Cabbage asks.
“Yes.”
“Did it feel like this?”
“Yes.”
“Did you survive?”
“Yes,” Awa says, her frown disappearing.
“This is what experience feels like. Tough situations prepare you for future experiences,” Cabbage says. “Wait and see what the family says. Then go home and talk it over with your mother.”
“Okay,” Awa says, returning to the house to complete her morning tasks. Her heart is still heavy, but it feels easier to breathe after talking to Cabbage.

In the afternoon, Madam Diop calls for her to tell her the final decision. The family likes Awa, but they promised their other maid the opportunity to return and they want to keep their word. So, Awa will finish the month and then drop to part-time hours and half-pay for three months while she searches for other work.

That evening, Awa walks home with a new book, Wretched of the Earth by Frantz Fanon. A title that describes exactly how she feels. When she walks through the door, she finds her mother nursing her brother and smiling. The scent of Yassa poulet, Awa’s favorite dish, fills the air.

“Evening, Mama,” Awa says, kissing her mother on the cheek and plopping down on the sofa next to her. Cabbage curls up on her lap. Awa hasn’t seen her mother smile like this in quite some time, which only makes what she has to say harder.
“Mama, I have news — ” Awa begins to say.
“Awa, I have news — ” her mother says at the same time.
They look at each other with surprise and laugh.
“Sorry, Mama, you first,” Awa says.
“Your father is coming home, and I found a job!” her mother exclaims with an even bigger smile.
“What?!” Awa asks.
“He’s saved quite a lot of money. Enough to return home! And I begin my new job next month,” her mother says. “We’ll hire someone to look after your brother, and you will return to school.”
Awa blinks. Is she dreaming? She looks down at Cabbage and he licks her hand. She is not dreaming. She really is going back to school.
“What did you want to tell me, Awa?”
Awa hugs her mother with tears in her eyes. “Nothing, Mama,” she says, smiling.
Life is full of surprises, and when all seems lost, there is still something good to be found.

options.label: Real learning only happens inside of a school classroom.
Sometimes luck just does not go your way and it is time to move on.
Once you miss too much school, it is almost impossible to catch up again.
If a goal is really important to you, keep working toward it and never give up.

questions.prompt: Which statement best expresses a theme of the story?

options.label: It shows that Awa understands she may never go back to school.
It shows that Awa thinks that she can both work and keep up with her school work.
It shows that Awa cannot yet accept the reality that she will no longer be going to school.
It shows that Awa believes her teachers will find another solution and that she'll return to school.

It demonstrates that Awa is afraid her parents will be disappointed in her for losing the job.
It suggests that Awa feels betrayed by Madame Diop's loyalty to the first maid.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase, "Awa's head swirls," in paragraph 98 suggest about Awa?

options.label: "'You'll have more friends one day, Awa. This is just one of the many experiences you'll have in your life,' Cabbage says." (Paragraph 67)
"'You understand my meows perfectly fine, and I understand you. You know exactly how I feel about you,' Cabbage explains." (Paragraph 73)

questions.prompt: Which of the following details best supports Cabbage's belief that you can achieve anything if you want it badly enough?
How does Awa's attitude toward education change throughout the story? Use details from the text to support your response.

discussionQuestions.prompt: In the story, Awa must give up something she loves — learning at school with her friends — for the greater good of her family. Have you ever had to sacrifice something that you cared about deeply for the greater good? If so, how did it make you feel? If not, do you think you are the type of person who would be capable of making an important sacrifice for the good of your family?

In the story, Awa is lucky to have several wise characters around her from whom she can learn, including one very wise cat. Do you have a wise person in your life - regardless of their age or who they are to you - who just always knows the right thing to say to help you solve tough problems? What is that person like, and how do they help you figure out the answers to your problems?

In the story, Awa gains permission from her employers to read whatever she wants from their library, one book at a time. Why do you think the author included Things Fall Apart and Wretched of the Earth as the two named titles that Awa borrows from her employers? What might the author have been trying to communicate to the reader by choosing those titles? Re-read footnotes 18 and 19 for more information about the books and their authors.

articles.title: Explainer: Animals' Role in Human Disease
articles.subtitle: Wildlife, livestock, and pets are the source of most germs that can sicken people.
articles.author: Amanda Leigh Mascarelli
articles.description: Amanda Leigh Mascarelli discusses how diseases among animals can impact humans.
articles.intro: While we may not readily think of our pets or farm animals as being dangerous, animals play a significant role in human disease. In this informational text, Amanda Leigh Mascarelli discusses how humans are impacted by animals infected with a virus or bacteria.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on how scientists prevent viruses from spreading further after an outbreak is identified.
articles.permissions: From Science News for Students , 2013. © Society for Science & the Public. Reprinted with permission. This article is intended only for single-classroom use by teachers. For rights to republish Science News for Students articles in assessments, course packs or textbooks, visit: https://societyforscience.org/permission-republish

blocks.text: Nearly 75 percent of new, or emerging, infectious diseases in people were first spread by animals. Indeed, half of all germs known to cause human disease come from other animals. Some sources were birds, bats, and other types of wildlife. Livestock and pet animals have spread many other diseases. Scientists refer to the infections that people pick up from animals as being zoonotic (ZOO-oh-NOT-ik).
The germs and other infectious agents that cause these diseases are known as pathogens. Most are microbes such as viruses or bacteria; others include fungi — even teeny-tiny worms and ticks.

In zoonotic diseases, animals serve as a pathogen’s host . Over time, some long-term hosts no longer become sickened. When a virus commonly lives inside an animal without harming it, that host is now called a reservoir . For instance, birds — especially ducks — have evolved into a natural reservoir for flu viruses.

Pathogens move among hosts continuously, explains Jonathan Epstein. A veterinary epidemiologist, he’s a scientist who studies the spread of disease in animals. (He works at EcoHealth Alliance in New York City.) Many pathogens will encounter a human host. If that person’s immune system had never yet encountered the microbe, it will have built up no immunity to fight the germ. That lucky pathogen can now survive and spread to others.
Understanding how pathogens spread between species can help scientists not only combat current disease outbreaks, but also prevent or lessen future ones.
For instance, Epstein specializes in viruses whose reservoir is bats. He has been on the trail of numerous viruses that have spilled over into people from these mammals. Among them: Nipah.

This viral disease started in Southeast Asia during the late 1990s. Workers at a massive pig farm began noticing troubling symptoms. Their pigs came down with a loud, barking cough and behaved strangely. They twitched and developed muscle spasms. Some pigs died. Tragically, farm workers also started getting sick. In severe cases, people entered a coma and died.
No virus can survive long outside a living organism. So Epstein teamed up with other experts to hunt the reservoir animal that had allowed Nipah to enter pigs.

It turned out to be a bat species. It normally stays away from people, living in the nearby rainforest. But when farmers planted an orchard of mango trees close to their pigpens, bats came by to dine on the juicy fruit. Those bats shed germy saliva, urine, and feces onto the pigpens below them.
From 1998 to 1999, Nipah sickened more than 250 people. More than four out of every 10 of these people died. One million pigs were killed and disposed of to stop the disease’s spread.

It is important not to blame wildlife for diseases, says Kristine Smith, a wildlife veterinarian who works for EcoHealth Alliance. Instead, she argues, people must become aware of the risks of being in close proximity to animals and adjust their behavior accordingly.

options.label: Diseases that seriously harm animals have little to no effect on humans.
It's safer to avoid all contact with animals than to risk contracting a virus.
Animals help the humans they come in contact with build an immunity to diseases.
Humans can contract dangerous diseases by coming in contact with infected animals.

questions.prompt: Which statement identifies the central idea of the text?

options.label: It discourages readers from coming in contact with certain animals.
It puts the blame on humans for contracting diseases from infected animals.
It advises readers on what to do with their knowledge about infected animals.
It tells readers what they should do if they contract a disease from an infected animal.

questions.prompt: How does paragraph 11 contribute to the development of ideas in the text?

options.label: By studying animals, scientists can learn about the sources of certain diseases and prevent them from spreading further.
By studying animals, scientists can cure the infected animals so that they no longer spread diseases to humans.
By studying animals, scientists can determine which species humans are no longer allowed to come in contact with.
By studying animals, scientists can learn how they develop their immunity to certain viruses and apply it to humans.

questions.prompt: What is the relationship between studying animal diseases and protecting humans?
How does the author's discussion of the shared virus between the pigs and the bats help us understand animals' role in human disease?

discussionQuestions.prompt: Do you or someone you know have a pet? Have you ever worried about coming in contact with germs from them? What do you do to limit the risk of contracting anything dangerous from pets or other animals?

In the text, the author emphasizes the importance of studying animal diseases to help humans. What are some other examples in which scientists have to study something harmful to better protect people from it?

articles.title: Of Feathers, Fat, and Freezing
articles.author: Donna DeVoe DiFolco
articles.description: Donna DeVoe DiFolco explains why chickadees are able to survive in extremely cold conditions.
articles.intro: Chickadees are tougher than they look, possessing the ability to survive harsh winters in Alaska. In this informational text, Donna DeVoe DiFolco explains how Chickadees are able to survive dangerously cold temperatures.
articles.annotationTask: As you read, take notes on what actions chickadees must take to stay warm.
articles.permissions: "Of Feathers, Fat, and Freezing" by Donna DeVoe DiFolco, CRICKET, January 2012 (text). Copyright © 2010 Cricket Media. Used with permission. All rights reserved.

blocks.text: Chickadees living near Fairbanks, in interior Alaska, experience -30° to -40°F on a regular basis, with temperatures sometimes plunging to -60°F or colder. Birds have a body temperature of about 104°F. This means that sometimes there is well over a 100° difference between a chickadee’s body and the air temperature only an inch away.

What do you do when you go out in the cold? Throw on a big, warm coat? Like all birds, chickadees have feathers, so in a sense, they wear little down parkas all the time. The colder the temperature, the more they fluff their feathers by sticking them out, away from their bodies. Feathers are the perfect insulation, trapping air between them and acting as a barrier between the cold outdoor weather and the birds’ bodies.

Chickadees can fluff their feathers out to an inch thick — wider than their own bodies — to protect themselves against the cold. At extreme temperatures, they look like round, feathery balls. Their feathers provide such good insulation that chickadees don’t become covered with frost. The only places where chickadees lose heat are around their beaks and eyes, which become frosty at extremely cold temperatures.

But chickadees don’t have feathers on their legs or feet, so how do they keep their tiny toes warm? They don’t. Without any feathered insulation, heat would quickly be lost and much energy wasted in attempting to keep their toes as warm as the rest of the body. So they allow their feet to cool to just above freezing. They do this by exchanging heat between the blood vessels in their legs. Warm blood flowing into the legs is cooled by cold blood returning from the toes. Then, when this blood returns to the main part of the body, it is warmed up again as it passes the warm blood entering the legs. This way, the rest of the chickadee doesn’t get cold by having cold blood returning to the body from its legs.

But even such energy efficiency by itself is not enough to survive the frigid winter days. In the same way that we must burn more fuel in the winter to keep our houses warm, chickadees also need more fuel to stay warm. And what sort of fuel do chickadees burn? Food. The colder the weather, the more food they need. As the days shorten, the temperature drops, and since chickadees forage only during daylight hours, they have the least amount of time to eat during the season when they need the most fuel. During the shortest days, chickadees have only about 6 hours of daylight to forage for food. As soon as there is enough light to see by, they spend all of their time finding and eating as much food as possible until the light fades.

Chickadees begin preparing for winter while the green leaves of summer are still on the trees. They stash away bugs, seeds, or fat from dead animals, tucking these morsels into crevices in tree bark. After most of the other birds have flown south to warmer climes, chickadees return to their caches. They also seek out wintering insects between the cracks in the tree bark and feast at bird feeders stocked with fatty treats like sunflower seeds, peanut butter, suet, or mealworms. The higher the fat content the better, since calories — the units of energy in food — are concentrated in fats.

To a chickadee, body fat is a fuel. Since they don’t have crops in which they can store food to eat later, they must eat enough each day to survive the long, 18-hour night. By feasting on fatty foods, chickadees can increase their body weight by about 8 to 10 percent — which would be like a 100-pound kid gaining 8 to 10 pounds in one day. The amount of food these birds must eat depends on what kind of food is available and how active they are. Chickadees living near well-stocked bird feeders can survive much easier than those living far from humans. Increasing their body fat during the day is like stoking the fire before going to bed. Throughout the night chickadees burn their recently accumulated fat, and by the next morning, none is left.

Even with their feathered insulation and daily storing away of fat, chickadees must still conserve as much energy as possible at night. They do not roost together in flocks as some birds do but spend the night alone. As darkness falls they cram themselves into little hollows in the trees. Once settled, they lower their body temperature, just as we lower the thermostat in our houses to save energy at night. For humans, becoming hypothermic (having a lowered body temperature) like this would be deadly, but chickadees use hypothermia to their advantage. By lowering their body temperature, or “thermostat,” by nearly 20°F, their fat reserves, or “fuel” supplies, last longer, enabling them to withstand the cold until it’s light enough to forage again.

Although fragile in many ways, the tiny black-capped chickadee shows how tough it really is by surviving the bitterly cold winters of northern North America. The next time you see a chickadee visiting your bird feeder, just think, could you survive out there?
